### Scompy — 2015-12-14T04:29:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-184057>

**Sun., Dec 13th, 2015 (KICKING MYSELF TODAY)**

Wanted to kick myself today. In my kitchen along one wall, I've got a washer and dryer on the other side in the bathroom. I've known about the magnetic EMF coming off of it for a good 6+ months now and the field it generates. What I didn't realize was that it not only extends long my kitchen wall, it also penetrates my pantry IN A BIG WAY at the base too. I feel so STUPID today after what I noticed what was going on. I over-focused on when the drier was running, which is the upper-unit farther away from lower-pantry.

I've had a habit of storing store-bought, high quality waters at the base of the pantry. So I got out my meter and did some analysis today with an "oh crap" moment in my head. The washer is on the bottom unit, but because it spins at various times, in the past I didn't realize the amount of magnetic EMF that can come off that SUCKER. So I've been the SUCKER after realizing it's generating a crap-ton of gauss into that compartment right where I was storing food and water!

I want plentiful electrons in my food and water, so I spend the entire afternoon moving everything into cabinets where I know it's not being exposed to any stray electromagnetic fields. It was tricky too, because I couldn't use any of the cabinets close to the fridge as well that puts out a lot of EMF at certain places (including another cabinet area). Yet another problem solved again, but come on... I should have known better to check more detailed in the past.

### Scompy — 2015-12-16T19:16:57+0200

### Scompy — 2015-12-18T08:45:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-184424>

Heh, look what I found on Kickstarter tonight: [https://www.kickstarter.com/project...shoulder-pro-calorie-burning-vest?ref=popular](https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/969324769/the-cold-shoulder-pro-calorie-burning-vest?ref=popular)

**How does it work?**
_**The Science Behind Cold-exposure Based Calorie Burn**_

You don't need to be a scientist to understand that _too much_ cold exposure – such as, say, floating in the North Atlantic after the Titanic sank – can extract so many calories so quickly that it can kill you. On the other hand, _mild_ cold exposure is perfectly healthy, and has been studied by scientists for decades as a way to burn calories.

For example, Olympic swimmers burn many more calories per day than other Olympic athletes, and it has nothing to do with the exercise. _**It’s the water.**_ They spend their day in water that’s 20 degrees (F) below their body temperature, and this temperature difference makes them burn **_way_**more calories than the exercise alone.

The sun finally came out today in Seattle, winter solstace of pure magnetism. Christmas morning looks promising for a lot of sun+ snow, and I live on a hill! I'm planning on taking my son out for a few hours in the cold sun for some serious sledding and snow-people: massive health benefits.

MY NEW PET PEEVE (article below): Clueless scientists looking to **reinvent the wheel** because they are ABSOLUTELY lacking a knowledge set we have. Skin-on-skin = Grounding benefit = electron restoration to babies. Get a baby grounded and with DHA, you get a lower mortality rate. I wonder how many SIDS cases are the lack of grounding in a high nnEMF, little DHA and dehydration. Smart Meters and Wifi aside, I think a lot of parents are putting their cribs right on a wall, and that wall has a lot of energy pulsing through it pulling away electrons as they sleep. Sadly, I'm thinking most mothers are wearing non-grounding shoes in high-rise buildings 3+ stories off the ground as they breastfeed their children.

### Scompy — 2015-12-25T08:59:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-184920>

**Tues., Dec 24th, 2015 (My Christmas Gift is that I'm a Member)**

I'm so grateful I'm a member here. I've totally figured out the root of my almost 7-year old son's ailment this past week due to some serious monitoring. My son is 100% allergic to tree nuts. I'll put in my thoughts about the potential solutions needed based on my instilled knowledge.

**So what happened?**
1\. Took x2 probiotic chewables when he had a stomach ache. Within 1 hour, he was coughing and wheezing. Made with xylitol = birch trees.
2\. Next day, eats some short breads from Scotland and starts coughing 15 mins later. Back of box: not suitable for anyone with tree nut allergies.
3\. Yesterday, drinks coconut water for the first time ever in over a year... 20mins later, has a severe coughing fit. Have to give him some Breath Easy tea to help him out.

That was the clincher. Although I haven't had him tested, tonight he ate some new Christmas goodies that also said on the box that they are not suitable for anyone with Pine Nut allergies. Boom. I've removed all products from the house in case he reaches for anything with cross-contamination of nut allergens.

**Steps for reducing/curing his allergies.**
1\. He seriously needs UV and sun. In the past, he's been leaking light at night right after he goes to sleep. That was going on a good year off an on. Vacations are planned, but the main problem is living in Seattle. His school also has x3 recess times, which is amazing, but because it's cold up here, little to no skin exposure.

2\. I've made a lot of strides with him on hydration in the past 2 months. It's a BIG task to get him to own the idea that he needs to drink water all day long. I have to get on everyone about this and it actually pisses me off to no end (mentally on the inside) that it's such a basic and easy thing to do. I've even talked with his teacher at school about it and she noticed he's not drinking much water either--he gets x2 spring waters packed into his backpack, but teacher has noticed he doesn't drink much compared to the other students. Luckily, she's been reminding him too, and I'm often on him to drink water, but it's a catch-22 because I need to make him own it.

3\. I have a near-light IR machine at home. I'm planning on getting it on his back at least 10-15mins a day. He seems to be fine with that, but it's just a short-term hack. Sauna might be needed to be installed if I stay in Seattle longer than a year. Reminds me of my time in Finland where it's a norm there. Maybe I need a UV-light generator also for him. He really appears to be good benefactor for a Quantlet.

4\. He's now getting outside with me in the morning under real sunlight, which is great for his circadian rhythm, and sleeping in pure darkness. He's very well timed waking up around the same time each day about 15mins before sunrise and going to sleep around 8pm every night.

5\. Avoidance of the tree nuts is obviously required in the meantime...probably for years to come.

6\. nnEMF at his school is a huge negative. Measured 0.5 to about 1.5 milliW/Sq.m in the classroom on average. After my talking with his Principal at the beginning of the year about hardwiring their systems instead of Wifi and sending her some solid science, she's completely ignored me even when I smile and see her in the hallways. Just a few people making decisions for 200+ students really gets under my skin. They even have microwave ovens in the classrooms so students can have their food heated up for lunch. That ~bakes~ takes the cake, doesn't it?

7\. Fluorescent lights at his school is another huge negative, but at least I've told his teacher I don't want him right under the lights. She's put him over in a spot that's far less direct and has about half of the lights on/half off in the room. I've told her specifically that he does much worse studying under those lights and makes him feel sick over time, so she's aware. I'm hacking his light environment a little bit more because I have him dressed in long-sleeve shirts and pants to help prevent more fluorescent lighting on his skin.

Really, the solution is getting him out of the PacNW and down into a continual sun-environment will be the main cure for him (from my current understanding). The added issue is that the PacNW is the 'allergy capitol' of the world. Any other advice, recommendations, or specific labs I need to do or tell his doctor to do are well-appreciated in-advance.

### thisbirdhaswings — 2015-12-25T21:33:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-184947>

The good news about encouraging more water-drinking in your son is that he doesn't have to be personally invested in it, to consistently perform the habit of drinking water. As his dad, you can focus on habit design with him. Find activities he already does consistently, and link drinking to those. The activity he already does becomes the trigger for the new behavior. I've done this with my girls to incorporate "8 hugs a day". Hugs got incorporated after waking up, after meals, after sitting down to reading time during the day and at night, etc. There's more to designing new habits than linking them to an existing trigger behavior--I'm happy to provide more details if you are interested. You are motivating me to work on the same thing with my girls as one in particular has a harder time remembering to drink!

As his dad, you can focus on habit design with him. Find activities he already does consistently, and link drinking to those. The activity he already does becomes the trigger for the new behavior. I've done this with my girls to incorporate "8 hugs a day". Hugs got incorporated after waking up, after meals, after sitting down to reading time during the day and at night, etc. There's more to designing new habits than linking them to an existing trigger behavior--I'm happy to provide more details if you are interested. You are motivating me to work on the same thing with my girls as one in particular has a harder time remembering to drink!

Yeah, excellent advice. I have wrapped in various times of the day I get him a drink, but that's the catch22. I want him to ask me for a drink. When it's nighttime, he wants a placker and toothbrush and uses the toilet before going to sleep on his own. In retrospect as parents, we often put so much focus on the minor stuff, the major stuff like sun and water are way more ignored than they should be!

I also take him to school every morning, and on the way, I always push the straw in his mouth at times and have him drink a lot right before he goes in for the day. Rehydrating after sleeping all night is a really, really important thing I think many parents forget about doing for their children. I'm constantly thinking about everything I can do to mitigate his situation, and I think I've probably saved him from a lot of disease/sickness because of my attention to details.

### Scompy — 2016-01-04T19:50:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-185406>

**Mon., Jan 4th, 2016 (Gaming the Game Studies)**
These types of articles irritate me a lot.

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2015/12/151221194124.htm](http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2015/12/151221194124.htm)

Why? Anytime anyone makes an assumption to clump <enter large subject> into a study, they are not thinking hard enough about the details.

Does this article test against:
1\. The genre of video games played as well as the specific game itself!? (This is HUGE)
2\. Blue light exposure with the video game and time of day?
3\. Whether a controller (console) game is played vs. a PC mouse/keyboard game?
4\. Whether wifi is being used or not (nnEMF) and other RF-EMF around the players?
5\. The frequency that the specific game is pinging the magnetic EMF on the controller?
6\. Interval periods of time players are playing certain games?
7\. Game playing associations with food and other rewards during play?
8\. Number of OTHER players the person might be intereacting with?
9\. Etc...

I don't think I've ever seen a game study that tests the electronics independently of the game itself. It would be a pretty simple formula:
1\. Have players play an electronic-version of the game in Group 1 over time, and control for lighting used.
2\. Have players play the identical version of the game on a table in Group 2 over time, and control for lighting used.

This might come a little closer to testing if the game itself is the problem, or if the light and electronic-interactions with 'the game' is actually the problem. Yes, there are reward loops built into nearly every game on the planet. This is why each game itself has a diferent reward curve built into it. Slapping exciting blue-light onto your game screen and pinging nnEMF for a food-reward association is going to turn any normally addictive game into a harshly-addictive one.

### Scompy — 2016-01-06T08:07:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-185547>

**Tues., Jan 5th, 2016 (Mitochondrial Rabbithole and Game Talk)**
I know quite a bit about astronomy... took a year in college along with my Physics courses. In the past 5 years, I've been looking at Suspicious0bserver's works. Tonight it occurred to me that there might be an analog with the sun's 11 year cycle and pole flip and the mitochondria with seasonal patterns. At times, we see the potential for great sunspot activity, and other times, we go dormant.

As for video games, I'm 100% behind what I've read with Jack's analysis of low dopamine associations with addictive behaviors. What do video gamers do? They spend a lot of time around nnEMF, away from the sun, are constantly blue-light exposed and the senses are also being bombarded too if you're playing on a controller with a rumble pack in front of your surround-sound system. I'm old enough to remember the creation of RPGs in the mid-70s (Role-Playing Games) and it was still many years off until the Atari2600 became a norm. I'd consider RPGs my speciality because I've played nearly all of them, and I'm also published in both tabletop and video games as you know. But I also remember the days playing with a group of people around a table rolling dice charging into battles, killing dragons, escorting loads of gems across goblin-infested forests, and eventually taking on demi-gods similar to Hercules. Tabletop games were just as addictive for their mechanics--I'm going to go out on a limb and say even moreso than any video game to this day. The reason has to do with a few elements within good role-playing games: 1. the player's mental creation to make any type of character desired and have that character be fully unique, 2. overcoming challenges that were both memorable, obsurd, or just plain "there's no way to do that in real life.", and 3. the relatedness of sitting around with friends and socializing at the same time. That element of human interaction is still somewhat lacking in social media and doesn't feel good even over Skpye because we analyze situations based more on non-verbal communication and tone. So when I compare playing an FPS, MOBA or MMORPG online with someone else, text-boxes and voice only goes so far and there's an element of feeling disconnected. This is also why board games still exist today and have done fine because AI only goes so far to simulate the interaction with real humans. Aside from these three elements, all games have reward curves and frequencies as lures to keep players actively thinking about how to achieve a future challenge. RPGs are unique in that the game master can always change things up on the fly on a whim to make the game more 'unexpected' and interesting--more like what happens in real life situations when murphy's law appears, when the totally weird takes place or whatnot. But the timing and cycling of these mechanics make a huge impact. Often, there is a blend of short-term cycles (at the moment interactions), medium-term cycles (often combat turns) and longer cycles for decision-making. Good game masters learn to weave in the story with combat and adjust to the overall sentiment of the players at the table based on how past events played out and what subtle hints and cues are being made. It's really a deep, psychological experiment in human behavior going on at all times, especially when impromptu decisions occur (and if the game rules allow the freedom for players to do unexpected or crazy things). Video games in this RPG genre have attempted to simulate what the old-school RPGs did and still do to this day, but still cannot flat-out replace humans sitting around a table in my humble opinion. Additionally, good game masters instinctually can know and react to the loops that fit the addiction best, sprinkle in rewards, lure by challenges, and keep the game feeling so open in choice that players can get lost in their character's development and actually build an attachment to that character. In my days, I've seen many characters die and Player perspective is everything... I've seen some younger players outright rage and cry in some cases because of a longterm investment in the character: like 2+ years of playing that same character every weekend with friends. Yes, those geeky polyhedrons can really break a player mentally. This is how addicting it can be. A good game master is wise to tell his players periodically that they are playing characters in an overall story and just like in a story, if the character dies, it will forever be remembered for how it died as part of the overall story. A great example of this was when I was a player of a high-level dwarven wizard that had been half-mutated into part-dwarf, part-wolf over the later years of his life. After playing this dwarf for 3 years and getting some really high level spells including Turn Flesh to Stone, an epic moment happened when my entire adventuring party was on a cliff-side overlooking a great chasm on our way to a frost giant's stronghold to rescue a magic item that fell into the wrong hands that could be used for great evil. As we approached, a huge ice dragon came flying down off a mountain and attacked us. We all were trapped on that cliffside with no where to go. In the game ruleset I was playing, I had the ability to 'ready my spell' so that when the dragon came swooping down at 80 miles per hour, I could still try to get my spell off. I tried Turn Flesh to Stone about 3x in a row because my dwarf had memorized it that many times for that day, but after the first two fails, I knew it probably would fail the 3rd time because it only had a 10% chance of success. As odds have it, on the 3rd time, it did go off, but here is why it was memorable. In the air, the huge dragon turns to stone. Yay! Then we all look up at a huge dragon-meteor coming down on us trapped on that cliffside. Oh no! Yep, I caused an entire party extinction event--all 8 characters dying being hit with 20d6 damage at the same time. The other 7 players around the table looked at me with such happiness for taking out the dragon in the first 30 seconds, before the game master interrupted asking us to quiet down for the impact damage. You can imagine how that went. Anyway, you see what I'm saying about RPGs... they can be like living another life because they feed creativity and memory to such an extent, maybe moreso than watching any movie, play or opera because of the attachment and interactive qualities. Since all games require interaction and engagment, once the loops are found, only experienced gamers with higher dopamine levels ultimately discover the formula, thereby discovering the magic sauce, and then addiction is cured... or highly reduced. When everything is perceived as 'new' in a game, it usually feeds the addictive mind. For many of us older gamers, we've seen it all and we know every formula in the book, so it's very hard to get us addicted anymore because we know how the magic is made. This is also why I am fearing VR (Virtual reality) this year because it's being pushed so hard in the gaming world and the future impact on people that use it... I fear even more for the youth because most parents won't understand the potential harm, even when their kid vomits or has a seizure when using it at 2am, dehydrated, while eating a bag of potato chips.

### Scompy — 2016-01-06T19:28:55+0200

**Wed., Jan 6th, 2016 (Sherlock Holmes)**

Thinking about nnEMF today and its ability to leech electrons: looking down at my bag of veges, it occurred to me that the transportation and storage of foods could explain on a macro-level something about our food supply. Because a lower pH makes it more acidic (more protons, less electrons), we could test foods. Are we all eating more proton-dense foods compared to like 50 years ago? I'm thinking we are.

This whole thing on Chipotle makes me think there's more than meets the eye here. Putting on my Sherlock Holmes hat for a minute, who benefits from turning Chipotle's plate sour? So they make non-GMO food look 'less safe' while at the same time giving a $$ payback hit for the decision to go GMO-free. I'm with you on video gaming. I grew up with D&D and then with online gaming. Bookmarked the meter you just mentioned and am a step closer to collecting my own data. Thanks for sharing your journey and data....!

Thanks for the post!
Oh, the ear-ringing and the past retinal-bleeding are both peanuts compared to coming down with a full-blown neurological disease, cancer or even death. These symptoms are direct meters inside my body that 100% connects to everything Jack says about redox and calcium effluxing. About 5 years ago, I chalked it all up to excess glutamate and genetics, not fully understand why glutamate was acting poorly in my system. I've said it before many times in my journal, but I really think I should be dead based on what my environmental exposures have been... I haven't shared much about my work at Stanford, but I also had a bunch of exposures in labs there too. Luckily by 2010, I had discovered Leangains.com and knew about the benefits of intermittent fasting and ketosis, and getting an understanding of leptin and hormones, which lead me down the path to finding Jack.

My big takeaways have been to always keep as hydrated as possible all of the time, sunlight, grounding and eat seafood daily. These alone has put me back into almost a normal existence, but the leaky gut is still being repaired and I have to limit what I eat and be very methodical about everything I put in my mouth.

I also have some interesting stories to share about my mother that died 2 years ago of diabetic complications. She knew and saw how my ketosis cured me, but she didn't have the will-power to stop eating carbs but she knew I wouldn't live with diabetes because she saw my insulin resistance issues returning back to normal. She also knew the benefit of the cold... she craved it just like a bear and would sleep with windows fully open at night making it 50F in her bedroom. Sadly, she was at a critical stage by 2012 and couldn't reverse her heart arteries, had to have surgery but didn't survive it due to post-operation organ failure. She grew up in Cali and was fine all of her life until her mid-30s, which I attribute the continual lack of sun in Oregon along with all of us sleeping on the 3rd floor of our house. I also slept on a waterbed for 15+ years--I really want to know now the nnEMF I was under during sleep.

### Scompy — 2016-01-08T01:04:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-185703>

**Thurs., Jan 7th, 2016 (Scompy Gets Crazy)**

Part 1 Humor: So how do you absorb more electrons in a high nnEMF environment? Drink lots of water, but that also puts guys in-front of a urinal a lot. I got thinking, why not also touch the upper chrome part of the uninal while I'm peeing for a little electron love? It's my 45 second biohack.

Part 2 Serious: I've moved to a x2 meal per day plan. Why did I do this? Mainly because I don't want to eat at the SAME TIME with my daily dose of cheesecake (nnEMF). So I typically eat around 7.30am and then when I get home, I eat again around 5-6pm just after a 20min anaerobic workout 3x-4x a weekish. I think this is gonna have profound benefits. Strangely enough, I haven't been hungry at all today after skipping lunch and just drinking water. In the morning, I'm eating A LOT of protein 30mins after I wake up. I'm talking, I usually have 3x eggs coated in tumeric, salt and pepper, a full chicken breast or two, and a salmon slice or oysters. I usually do a big iced decaff coffee (for flavor) with coconut oil and grassfed butter and sip on it for 30mins in the car on the way to work. This seems to last me fine for 10 hours

I've done a lot of variances of IF in the past, and this 7.30am and 5.30pm eating times may be the most optimal ticket for me. Gonna give it a few weeks to see how it goes... I think it's a much better way to keep my stomach from hunger pangs during the late morning and afternoon to minimize any temptations under nnEMF.

### Scompy — 2016-01-13T23:03:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-186226>

**Wed, Jan 13th, 2016 (My N=1 Observations)**
Cold Thermogenesis: My CT is going quite well these days. The adaptation is so strong that it only takes me about 45 seconds to adjust to the shower. This morning though, I only had a glass of ice-water and no food... mistake there, but won't repeat it again. I've got an interesting head-to-toe method for the Shower. (First to mention, I have the house at 62F; my family hasn't yet complained). Right after breakfast, I start with a fairly warm shower and get my head wet and shampoo it, but next, I crank up the heat a bit more and my back warm and soap off the rest. The key here is that my front no longer has any heat applied and because it's 62F in the home, it is cooling down for about 2mins as my back is very warm.

Next, I turn the shower down to luke-warm to match my front-side because it's cold in the house. I acclimate for a minute, then turn down again colder... about 60-65F. It's totally fine for me because my back is very warm still (but now starting to get a bit colder). Then about 30 seconds later, I turn down the water pressure and nudge down the cold more and more slowing in about 20-second intervals. In this period of time, it's that cringing feeling, but mostly when droplets fall on my sides that are semi-warm still. I can get the the shower cranked fully on cold. I'm thinking this is right around 45F-50F right now... I'll have to measure it because it varies based on how cold it is outside, but it feels more like 30 F on me. Doesn't matter though, because at this point, I'm totally numb to the cold... I try to stay a good 8-10mins this way, and over that time, slowly turn here and there a bit to get more of my body cold. Typically, I can get most of my shoulders to trunk, and on occassion, I let the water run down the neck from just below the ears and jaw. I typically don't get my hair wet during this time, and leave it warmer from when I first shampoo'd my head.

Localized CT N=1: Okay, call this bat-shampoo-crazy, but after the shower, I dry off and just get my pants on and get to my infared light. Because my stomach and torso is still very cold, I warm my numb-cold torso in it for a few minutes. I'm working on that part of my body to diminish all remaining fat and stretch-marks where I used to be fat. What's funny, is that I thought about weaving in my stainless steel ice-tray because I use a tray of ice every morning for my decaf saltless-buttered-coco-oil-iced-coffee.

Placing the ice-tray at localized places is **killing 3 birds** with one stone in my mind...
1\. Keeps the localized area I'm working on cold as the IR is heating it
2\. IR-strikes the ice-cubes for potential EZ
3\. Heats up the ice-tray for my coffee

Btw, I typically use a mixture of RO+Alkaline+Spring Water for my ice cubes. I find about 5 minutes later, my ice cubes are starting to melt enough to crack the tray and use them for the decaf coffee.

\----
Food & Water: I'm still doing 2 or 3 meals a day, and judge based on my hunger levels. It will either be two huge meals, or two medium meals with lunch being a tiny meal around 300-400 calories. My morning has high protein and some fats and carb, but the note here is that all carbs are vegetable carbs and sugars that came from the vegetables--typically as onion, garlic and asparagus. If I do have lunch, it's just enough. Just a wedge of fish, oysters, or even a bag of non-nut kale-chips with some meat. Just enough to satisfy, while drinking a lot of water in the day. Then for dinner, I've been mixing it up, but pushing up the fats and reducing the protein into the 15-30 gram range. With my dinner, I often have another 32-40 oz of water, enough to get to a perfect point where a good bulk has turned to pee and is gone by the time I go to sleep 5-6 hours later (and then I can sleep through the night). Also right before dinner is when I typically get some exercise every other day or so. I try to do 45 pushups all days, even on non-workout days, to match my age.

\----
Light: Very reduced. During workdays, morning Eye & Skin exposures, go to work with window down for some added CT. Mid-morning walks. Getting outside again for lunch. Weekends are devoted far longer to being outside because of my worklife. Plus I live in Seattle, so no UVB. A reptile lamp is coming to my family on Friday. Off to Cali soon, so planning on some great sun replenishment.

\----
nnEMF & Redox: Lots of hacks with nnEMF and real-working solutions in my home. I turn off nearly all devices in my home (hold the fridge) when we sleep, and have hacked specific devices. My son always wants 'Scompy Soup' which I make for him specifically that's high in sulfur and cysteine... duram noodles, bone broth, garlic, onion, shredded chicken, salts--all organic. It's a thicker-soup because of the shreded chicken parts, but it's the best way I've found to sneak higher quality protein into his diet.

The Ranch would be build in mind around life's cycles. Instead of brutal workouts indoors under fluorescent lights, workouts would be outside under sunlight. ### Scompy — 2016-01-24T22:39:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-187291>

**Sun, Jan 24, 2016 (Creative Games)**
If you want to work your kids away of video games 24/7 for higher imagination and hands-on work, here is a way to do it.
It's called paper, tape, markers and creativity. I'll explain the rules how it works below. Probably for kids ages 6+.

1\. IMPORTANT: Tell kid that the game is about making lots of characters, but things earned along the way are kept forever
2\. Have your kid create a character stick-figure and name it; then have a 3x5 Character Heart Card upkeep and for Defeating-Key-Enemies and ultimate Character's Death Phrase
3\. Start Character with 20 Maximum Hearts and 20 Current Hearts for taking damage.
4\. Start Character with any weapon and pickaxe; weapon for damage and pickaxe for getting through barriers; Tape both to the character stick-figure.
5\. Start adventure and have that character fight bad guys in a forest, dungeon, castle, etc.
6\. When defeating a # of bad guys or a boss, reward a few permanent Maximum Hearts
7\. Write down on Heart Card how you defeated the boss enemy.
8\. Have Character find weapons that deal more damage and give to Character along the way

1\. CHARACTER DEATH: When character dies, tape the Character's stick-figure to the Hearts Card. (This is to file-away for long-term progression to remember the story about how the character died.)
2\. Write down how the Character died: what monster killed it, or anything epic you'd like for memory retention later on, like, "Goblin Guy died while fighting in an ocean of ink."
3\. Write down 'Dead' on the card and file it away in order of the adventure
4\. Have kid make a new Character, but keep the gained Maximum Hearts amount and use any weapons/items gained on past adventures if desired: These are part of the "Player" rather than the character, so progression is retained.

### Scompy — 2016-01-30T08:12:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-187663>

**Fri, Jan 29, 2016 (Vacations and Airplanes!)**
So, I just got back from my SoCal vacation after three days basking in the sun. I was ultra happy to have finally gotten my UV meter up to a 6 (Moderate-high UV right at 11am).

Although I am quite refreshed, I'll be spending much of this weekend updating my journal with some crazy and insightful information. I'm going to start with my airplane flights and being at the airport. I'm pretty pissed off that both planes I got on had new electric plugs right at my knee height (for charging phones, computers, etc.). I couldn't help to be within 1 foot of plug for the entire flight. But to top it off, trying to ground from the floor metal wasn't fun either because they have the ELECTRICAL BOX on the floor that wires up to the electrical plug by my knee! What rookie engineer created such a mishap... what I would have given to bring on my EMF meter and take some measurements. I was afraid it would have gotten confescated, ironically, while everyone is allowed to take on their other electronic devices and gadgets.

Then at the airport eating lunch at a deli, I sat down for just 5 minutes in a booth and started hearing tinnitus. A minute later the waitress told me, "if you want to use any of your electronics, there is a plug below." I proceeded to look below where I was sitting and the plug was BUILT INTO THE FRICKEN BOOTH! My leg was basically touching the electrical plug. When I found out, I immediately moved away, but was subtle about it. Another 5 mins later, all tinnitus went away. I did leave a note to the waitress, thanking her about the plug and told her about the EMF that was probably coming off of it and left it at that. It was a strong reminder to be vigilant and always be on the lookout in a changing or foreign environment to the hazards that may await you.

You won't believe some of the awesome benefits and some of the crazy stuff I saw on my vacation... coming soon with pictures and videos.

### Scompy — 2016-01-31T08:32:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-187747>

**Sat, Jan 30, 2016 (FREE ELECTRONS!)**
I found it amazing that on every attraction in both parks, people are getting FREE GROUNDING everywhere they go and are not aware of it. Standing in long lines may actually have a beneficial impact on people in the past, but now being forced to stand in long-lines, the free-grounding is about the only remaining way to mitigate the risks of the nnEMF around you. My theory now is that the physics lent itself to be "The Happiest Place On Earth' because people would be UNKOWLINGLY GROUNDED everywhere they went. Every bench had a metal footing touching the ground. Every non-bench place to sit down is made of stone. Every railing is connected into the earth. It was shocking for me to realize this fact that free-electrons is built to be everywhere! In a total nnEMF nightmare, a person can find some solace by leaning and touching. I noticed in particular, kids will be kids, but they appeared to 'want to touch', 'sit on' and 'play with' the metal beams and bars 24/7 as if they were drawn to it like a magnet. Yes, it's a bit of a monkey behavior to do it, but it made me think, "Is there more to it than just instinct to grab onto metal bars and railings?" Because lines are long, the adults mainly used it as support. Strangely, most people that were leaning against metal railings were not on cell-phones or electrical toys: most of those people were standing ungrounded. I'll post a video showing a really tough line.

### Scompy — 2016-01-31T08:56:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-187748>

**Sat, Jan 30, 2016 (Typical Line)**
So what's wrong with this video? Looks benign to any sheeple without knowledge. To the rest of us, it's profoundly telling.

1\. The RF-nnEMF is crazy in the lines; around 30% of everyone in line is actively using their cells or devices. A few lines I saw almost a 50% use-rate.
2\. Grounding available everywhere, few people are using it
3\. Look at about 5 seconds into the video: I purposely pan up to show the ceiling. Fans are twirling with lights both ON and ABOVE the fan-blades that cause a STROBE LIGHT EFFECT. It's also night-time.

When I got out of this section of line with the fans, it was 20 minutes later. Even with my blue-blockers on and grounding continually, my stomach was feeling a little uneasy. The ride is also a 3D one requiring the use of 3D glasses looking at huge screens and firing at targets at the screens. Now I'm very curious how many people a few minutes after this ride end up with a small headahe, or even a migraine or small seizure due to the physics going on around them. Or people that do the ride 3x+ in a row in these types of conditions packed in like sardines in nnEMF soup. Luckily, in some lines mid-day, there is strong sun in Cali this time of year. I was getting around a 4 to 6 UV index in the 10am to 3pm window.

The crazy part is that once the sun set, everyone removed their sunglasses to absorb their blue-lit screens under high-powered lighting. During the day, I saw many people avoiding the sun at all costs. The most obese people were the most active cell-phone users in lines, had on the darkest sunglasses and were covering their skin with a lot of clothing (especially black colors). Coincidence? I think not.

### Scompy — 2016-02-06T08:41:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-188402>

**Fri, Feb 5, 2016 (Quantum Game Ideas)**
"Health is merely the slowest form of death we create. Build wisely." [Jack's meme](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/jack-kruse.1031/) in Time #8 got me thinking again about game-related quantum ideas: what the best method would be to get the message out to the gazillions of kids, teens and young adults about quantum biology? It occurred to me that there are a lof of mechanics that play up to damage-influences in quantum biology (artificial lighting, nnEMF, etc.) would work great for a card game. I could see a premise that all Players start with 100 Health, and try to bring out cards to whittle the opponent down to zero health. Due to the 3-legged stool of Sun, Water and Magnetism, it would auto-balance in resources for stategic decisions.

The gameboard I imagine is a map of a state like California and initially it has little population with lots of wilderness. Over time, the land starts 'crowding-up' with modern crap like we see a fast-forward of 1900 to 2015 in a 1-hour game...modern towns and cities erupt and give a Sun, Water and nnEMF detriment to the landscape around that area. Factories, railways, freeways, etc. would start connecting the hubs. If the Player's chit is located in a more toxic zone with more negative influencs going on, then there is a higher potential for damage, but higher potential for $$ gain. Getting outside and remaining outside helps to extend life, but then it slows down the resources available to mitigate damage because income generation $$ is lower overall.

Simulating life, maybe the goal of the card game would be to try and stay alive as long as possible and keep the local environment around the Player's chit as clean as possible, while having enough $$ to keep mitigating. Players that spend a lot of time in city areas gain a lot of money, but it's higher risk because there are more cards that can lead to higher taxes, and higher damage situations. Last player standing wins the overall game. Players can use Biohack cards to extend life at the right times, especially while traveling into cities. I'm thinking each turn, the game auto-plays Event Cards based on what is randomly drawn to block sunlight, artificial lighting, Cell phone tower built, natural gas leak, sunscreen pollution on beaches, you know, all kinds of modern tragedies, etc.

Over time, the overall maps keeps expanding with population (high rise city pieces), Cell Towers and the like, so it soon squeezes off the available locations of safety. Then if a Player's chit is caught in a bad area, some damage is taken by drawing 1 or more special event cards. Then if there a compound issue like all three problems, the player might have to draw three cards to lose money, have a biohack fail, take some Health damage, etc.

I'm thinking that the Player can do things to restore certain territory probelms on the game-board and remove a problem from a location with specific cards gained. Each turn, a new event happens around a certain area on the board (usually negative, but sometimes beneficial), and each turn, a Player can decide to move his/her chit, and also try to clean up the mess created in the area to minimize the damage taken. Certain events may also require drawing from the Disease deck to see what is received (Diabetes, Alzheimer's, Obesity, Cancer, Surgery required, etc.) a long with the influence like -$$ and damage until the Disease Card can be removed. Each turn, there's probably a 1 in 6 chance that the Disease goes away naturally, but having more abilities to Biohack help to get rid of the Disease.

Yeah, it sounds pretty real and bad, but is awesome when you play a Card like "Morning Sunlight". "Reverse Osmosis Water", "WiFi systems removed", "Taken Residence in Sunville", etc. to remove Disease Cards so you don't carry them around with you every turn. Obesity could be an interesting one because it causes you to move slower on the overall map. This sort of game would bang people over the head and maybe get them curious enough to investigate what's going on. It has to play simple, but the game pieces and Special Event cards help to show details too. The best representations would be plopping down localized pieces of damage such as Tall buildings to block sunlight, Cell Towers for nnEMF and dried up or polluted water holes. Over time, Players would see the entire map get populated and filled up with Cell Towers. Special event cards would be specific stuff like Wifi in schools, working out under artifical lighting, etc.

### Scompy — 2016-02-11T09:12:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-188753>

**Wed, Feb 10, 2016 (Cell Phone Workers Play Dumb?)**
Two days ago, my own "Dumb Phone" broke because the screen wasn't coming on, so I went into the store to see what new phones were available. The first thing I asked the employee was, _**"What phones have the lowest SAR value?"**_

His answer, "What's a SAR?"

_**(In my mind at that moment, I was studying his face with great influence to see if I could detect any lie).**_ But I was satisfied that this kid really didn't know what the hell a SAR was even though he's working at a Cell Phone store. Then it occurred to me that most customers probably didn't ask that question and they already knew in their minds what they wanted coming into the store.

A long story-short, I talked to this young guy for a good 20mins. He was around 22ish years old and I had him looking up various SAR values on the phones he had, going to federal sites and reading the fine print. I was totally non-emotion and matter-of-fact about what I wanted, and he appriciated that I could tell. By the end of his conversation with me, he had learned all about RF-EMF and SAR values without me having to explain anything. I found their phone with the lowest value (around a 0.9) for the cheapest, and it turned out to be still a "Dumb Phone." What I mean by that is that if you don't know much about the SIM Chips, if you get the more modern **LTE Chip** ([http://www.gctsemi.com/html/LTE.html](http://www.gctsemi.com/html/LTE.html)) and throw away your old SIM Chip, you're going to be allowing 4G on your cell. I still had my ancient-tech SIM Chip that only allows 3G. hehehehe, suckers!

Anyway, the worker was asking me more and more about why I didn't want 4G, so I gave him some details about how many milllWatts/Sq.m sets off my tinnitus on average with exposure times. I could tell by his expression that he wanted me to shut up ASAP or quite down a bit after I said that with some other people in the store. Doesn't matter, he got my $$ and I'm happy with a cell phone that has better SAR value compared to my older, dead dumb phone.

### Scompy — 2016-02-16T01:56:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-189146>

**Mon, Feb 15, 2016 (Lemon Balm)**
Having some lemon balm today for the first time I think in my life, and amazing how amazing it tastes.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa\_officinalis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis)

Recent research found a daily dose of the tea reduced [oxidative stress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidative_stress) status in radiology staff who were exposed to persistent low-dose radiation during work. After only 30 days of taking the tea daily, consuming lemon balm tea resulted in a significant improvement in plasma levels of catalase, [superoxide dismutase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide_dismutase), and [glutathione peroxidase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glutathione_peroxidase), and a marked reduction in plasma DNA damage, [myeloperoxidase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myeloperoxidase), and [lipid peroxidation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lipid_peroxidation).[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-14)

The crushed leaves, when rubbed on the skin, are used as a [mosquito](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mosquito) repellent.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-15)

Lemon balm is also used medicinally as an herbal tea, or in extract form. It is used as an [anxiolytic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anxiolytic), mild [sedative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sedative), or calming agent.\[_[medical citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_\(medicine\))_\] At least one study has found it to be effective at reducing stress, although the study's authors call for further research.[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-16) Lemon balm extract was identified as a potent _[in vitro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/In_vitro)_ inhibitor of [GABA transaminase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/4-Aminobutyrate_aminotransferase), which explains anxiolytic effects. The major compound responsible for GABA transaminase inhibition activity in lemon balm was then found to be [rosmarinic acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rosmarinic_acid).[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-17)

Lemon balm and preparations thereof also have been shown to improve mood and mental performance. These effects are believed to involve [muscarinic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muscarinic) and [nicotinic acetylcholine receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicotinic_acetylcholine_receptors).[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-18) Positive results have been achieved in a small clinical trial involving Alzheimer patients with mild to moderate symptoms.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-19) Essential oils obtained from Melissa officinalis leaf showed high [acetylcholinesterase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acetylcholinesterase) and [butyrylcholinesterase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butyrylcholinesterase) co-inhibitory activities.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-20)

Its antibacterial properties have also been demonstrated scientifically, although they are markedly weaker than those from a number of other plants studied.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-21) The extract of lemon balm was also found to have exceptionally high[antioxidant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antioxidant) activity.[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-22)

Lemon balm is mentioned in the scientific journal _Endocrinology_, where it is explained that _Melissa officinalis_ exhibits antithyrotropic activity, inhibiting [TSH](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thyroid-stimulating_hormone) from attaching to [TSH receptors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TSH_receptor), hence making it of possible use in the treatment of [Graves' disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graves%27_disease) or [hyperthyroidism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperthyroidism).[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis#cite_note-23)

### Scompy — 2016-02-18T08:09:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-189291>

**Wed, Feb 17, 2016 (People eliminating themselves from the gene pool)**
Yes, very revealing.... distractions = accidents & deaths. Hmm, what causes the most distractions on the road these days? Nearly every driver I pass is chatting away, often with rectangle up to their ear or looking down at their little rectangle somewhere else in the vehicle.

“We don’t know with a lot of detail what the causality is. That information comes out later,” Hersman says, explaining that data from police reports, autopsies and other sources help determine some of the factors involved. Past NSC research has indicated that alcohol, speed and **distracted driving** are the [three major causes of fatalities on the road](http://www.nsc.org/learn/pages/nsc-on-the-road.aspx?var=mnd), contributing to 30.8, 30 and 26 percent of motor-vehicle deaths respectively.

The costs involved in motor-vehicle deaths, injuries and property damage—including “wage and productivity losses, medical expenses, administrative expenses, employer costs and property damage”—came to an estimated $412.1 billion in 2015. Because of changes to the NSC’s calculation and categorization methods, it says it wouldn’t be appropriate to compare the costs or the number of injuries to those in previous years.

"These numbers are serving notice: Americans take their safety on the roadways for granted," Hersman is quoted as saying in the NSC’s [press release](http://www.nsc.org/Connect/NSCNewsReleases/Lists/Posts/Post.aspx?List=1f2e4535-5dc3-45d6-b190-9b49c7229931&ID=103&var=hppress&Web=36d1832e-7bc3-4029-98a1-317c5cd5c625). "Driving a car is one of the riskiest activities any of us undertake, in spite of decades of vehicle design improvements and traffic safety advancements. Engage your defensive driving skills and **stay alert** so we can reverse this trend in 2016."

[http://www.msn.com/en-us/news/us/us...-years/ar-BBpDu10?li=BBnb7Kz&ocid=mailsignout](http://www.msn.com/en-us/news/us/us-traffic-deaths-biggest-percent-jump-in-50-years/ar-BBpDu10?li=BBnb7Kz&ocid=mailsignout)

### Scompy — 2016-02-18T08:29:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-189293>

**Wed, Feb 17, 2016 (Wallace please tell me, why is CHEESE such a problem for me?)**
How many of us are able to eat cheese and not have it influence our waistline? Cheese is my nemesis completely. A week ago, I started to include a bit of high quality organic grassfed cheddar from Eurpoe (8g protein per serving) and no bueno. I have to be very careful using it, the volume of it and the timing. Most American-created-cow cheese is filled with unwanted crap yes, but I'm also talking even grassfed cheese from Europe.

I don't think it's the casein, thinking it's a calcium-signaling pathway issue and the ease of triggering unwanted insulin problems in my system--stalling my gut bigtime and possibly added pancreatic insulin release. I've always been very sensitive to calcium effluxing also, and I do everything I can in my power to minimize nnEMF, as this was at the heart of the issue. I think I need to give cheese more time. I go as far to even get Sheep-feta from Israel or Greece, but still finding or believing the calcium is the main problem along with a possibly higher glutamate component that can also trigger unwanted insulin release, especially in those that have bad environments...low UV, high nnEMF. Finding that magnesium <--> calcium balance in my system has to be played perfectly for me to get the best results (enough Mg, but not overkill, and limiting Calcium). yes, I think nnEMF has blown open and/or radically altered my calcium channels open over the past 20+ years.

### Scompy — 2016-02-24T02:11:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-189683>

**Tues, Feb 23, 2016 (SEATTLE gets some UVB, Oh noes!)**

This story is a true event at what just happened less than 4 hours ago!

First, I was amazed today that UVB hit at noon and I had a full hour to get out into it:
[https://www.google.com/maps/place/Juanita Bay Park/@47.7039072,-122.2168598,17.75z/data=!4m2!3m1!1s0x0:0x16025f832f8affc1](https://www.google.com/maps/place/Juanita+Bay+Park/@47.7039072,-122.2168598,17.75z/data=!4m2!3m1!1s0x0:0x16025f832f8affc1)

Next, it was 55F outside and with the sun on the skin, felt like 70F. UVB meter index finally registered a '1' and close to a '2'. I had very short shorts on a white tank and was literally the only person in all of Washington State dressed this way. I found an amazing spot on the pier -- an ENTIRE GROUNDED platform was in the sun unobstructed. I laid out for a long time like a turtle there. Now, mid-day from 12pm-1pm, there are VERY FEW people at the park. I expected there to be 5x, if not 10x the numbers on a nice, sunny day. Maybe 50 to 60 individuals altogether making a loop around the map I posted above. I only saw one man (about my age) that was shirtless & no sunglasses, and he was absorbing the sun out of the back of his truck. He was the fittest person there, very ripped. I was #2 in fitness factor.

As I watched people pass by, nearly everyone had on sunglasses. About 70% had EXTREMELY DARK sunglasses. And over half had hats with their sunglasses. And finally, about 90%+ had on full-clothing revealing only a bit of the neck and face at best. THIS IS SEATTLE, what the heck is going on?!

**The KICKER**: So as I'm laying there and recharging, what do I hear UNPROVOKED?! **_"Make sure to use your sunscreen!"_** An older, large woman nearing obesity tells me this. She's got on pitch-black sunglasses and a golfing-brimmed hat. Fully clothed from head to toe walking with another older lady just about the same way.

Seeing how this unhealthy individual was giving me advice, I immediately responded back, **_"No way! I've got to get as much UVB as I can while it lasts!"_** She arrogantly chuckled back at me... translated, the laugh was this: **_"Okay, stay ignorant about the evils of the sun."_**

**OKAY ALL SEATTLEITES...** WAKE UP! You've got little to no UVB light right now at such a high latitude, and you're all paranoid about any drop of sun harming you! Those avoiding the sunlight are literal zombies wrapped up in their belief structure. I'm witnessing the zombification of humanity up here: pale and pruned-up skin, wincing when walking toward the sun, slowish-walking-corpse-looking-animations. It's something to behold. I see the remaining healthy individuals not protecting themselves from sunlight. This is like a big movie here... zombies and vampires destroyed by sunlight. And they want us to join their ranks.

Answer: They believe walking/exercising is the sole factor benefitting their health.

### Scompy — 2016-02-25T20:56:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-189782>

**Thursday, Feb 25, 2016 (Inward Reflections)**

I wrote this today on FB and because it got under my skin, I'll post it here. Society needs more "Tough love" to learn and build back-bone. I seriously believe Gen-X was the last generation that taught this as a norm. Political correctness, in part, has helped to zap away tough-love. Parents especially understand why tough-love works so well. Ironically, parents run out of time, so this helps build independence in children to do more for themselves than enabling them constantly...the Helicopter-parent.

Yes, there needs to be balance showing different forms of love. If you love another person so much, then you better damn well give that person the skills to survive independently, rather than continually enabling, giving freely with no reward structure in place, over-protecting and taking action for the person. Doing this over time for another is a great recipe to create a drug-addict later in life.

[http://www.yesmagazine.org/happines...ove-and-why-knowing-them-can-change-your-life](http://www.yesmagazine.org/happiness/the-ancient-greeks-6-words-for-love-and-why-knowing-them-can-change-your-life)

We also have many people running around that easily get in a tizzy about the smallest issues in life with only a narrow lens and scattered knowledge sets, not able to connect any dots anymore... The person believes everyone ELSE should be connecting the dots instead. Whittle this down to "no one wants to own their own shit."

A healthy person FIRST looks inward to oneself investigating for faults and wrong conclusions with an attitude of "wanting to improve one's life" -- rather than FIRST looking outside and attempting to place blame upon another. This skill is vital for a person's longterm health, but rarely taught anymore. The age of virtues has passed... virtues now only exist in corners of a distant land. This is at the heart of why many love and crave Jane Austin books. Characters within hold virtues to a high standard.

As a child, I watched my older sister continually make mistake after mistake. Now I know how lucky I was to experience that. I saw her get rewards ripped away and it put me in my place. Tough love helped me. I strived to the the 'right things' becuase I knew I'd get more rewards... I experienced the wrong-crap through her eyes because I had a sibling. This is why time and time again we see children with lots of siblings do really well over only-child families.

Just pop in Willy Wonka and the Chocolate Factory and ask yourself, "Are you raising a Veruca Salt?" And how many adults are now Veruca Salt equivalents?

[http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0067992/](http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0067992/)

This entire thought-process relates back to me about **personal responsibility and owning our own health**. We can't have a 'pocket doctor' ready to pull out whenever we want. Instead, we need to learn and understand WHY the information can answer specific questions. We need to do this on our own and be able to explain it to others when asked if we want any street cred. Confidence is part of this equation because if we cannot explain the initial physics or quantum bio (in a nice, fearless, matter-of-fact manner), then the non-scientific non-detailed-oriented people won't normally take our words seriously when first exposed. When attempting to express knowledge sets with emotional impact, it can be a hit or miss situation depending on the audience. Know your audience first if you can. I've found personally that if I don't have the time and/or the skillset to explain what is asked, I recommend them to poke around at jackkruse.com for connecting the dots on their own. That's how they own it. If I explain too much, I become the enabler. I don't want to be the enabler. I want the other person to own it.

[http://menwithpens.ca/dont-help-people/](http://menwithpens.ca/dont-help-people/)

### Scompy — 2016-02-26T02:19:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-189795>

**Thursday, Feb 25, 2016 (At the Docks, Part 2)**

Same place as Tuesday...Just reporting that I finally **GOT A TAD PINK** today in Seattle...a **UVB 5** today on my meter! Wows!

The sheeple were still fully-clothed, fully sunglassed. Even LESS PEOPLE outside today than on Tuesday. Maybe 40ish at best walking around as zombies. In contrast, there were 10 non-sheeples! They were playing volleyball in a sand-court...getting a ton of sun and grounding without knowing... I was shocked these 10 volleyball players that only 2 had on sunglasses, and most were in their swimsuits!

As I was laying out on the grounding dock, I heard lots of people walking and talking about how cold it was outside as they looked at me in shorts: 60F today! With sun-directly on the skin, it felt 100% perfect because I'm cold-adapted.

### Scompy — 2016-03-02T19:35:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-190092>

Dr. K says:

Pseudohypoxia = low O2 = Low NAD+/NADH ratio at cytochrome 1 = NAD+ drops in people with blue light exposure and microwave exposure = elevated ubiquitin rates = Increased % heteroplasmy = low levels of electrons locally on cell membranes = electron density in tissues is a function of the DHA concentrations = low melatonin recycling = low EZ size in cell water = dehydration = higher positive charges (protons) in proteins making them less hydrophilic = low intracellular pH = low redox potential = cell and mitochondrial swelling (cyto c release) = lowered magnetic and electric fields in mitochondria = increase % heteroplasmy = low ATP levels = up-regulation of AMPK = altered serotonin and dopamine levels in the skin, eye, and frontal lobes = NT release tied to calcium efflux = calcium controls voltage gated channels, NMDA, and glutamate excitotoxicity= low DC electric current = low tissue DHA = altered perceptions of reality = disease

This is the BIGGEST BOOM from my life experiences. I could have benefitted from 20 years ago when I was attempting to piece-meal why glutamate excitotoxicity was occurring in me.

These days I've been perplexed with a calcium issue. I think my calcium receptors may be 'shot' and have to keep my Mg levels higher overall. This also ties in with why glutamates are more potent in my body (have have been for the past 25 years). I'm serious when I say that if I have a dinner with a higher amount of calcium in it, like some feta cheese, I'll run a higher likelihood of muscle-cramping 4-6 hours later. I find that if I keep my calcium levels lower overall and/or more evened-out in a day, things are fine. Of course, the major players are nnEMF and lack of UV.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-04T12:23:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-190177>

Hormesis is a fine edge between positive and negative reactions. And on that edge quantum probability exists. Beyond this edge there’s a space/time where emptiness and substance neatly overlap, where past and future form a continuous, endless loop. Hormesis only works with this loop when the positive feedback loop is properly coupled to the negative feedback control. When it is not hormesis becomes extinction for both sides of the equation.

### Scompy — 2016-03-04T20:00:37+0200

>
> Hormesis is a fine edge between positive and negative reactions. And on that edge quantum probability exists. Beyond this edge there’s a space/time where emptiness and substance neatly overlap, where past and future form a continuous, endless loop. Hormesis only works with this loop when the positive feedback loop is properly coupled to the negative feedback control. When it is not hormesis becomes extinction for both sides of the equation.

Balance is everything in life, and at that root, it's understanding the proper answer(s) to 'what is balance?' for every given subject. Knowledge is required to make that right call, but as we know, people tend to get stuck in a very limited knowledge set. I've found creativity gets us into the place of the knowledge we want. Einstein is correct. Luckily we have nature as a guideline to help us understand what balance looks like in practice.

This truthful reality above ^^^ is very hard for a lot of people to stomach. I'm now becoming a gentle fighter, so seeking truth doesn't scare me. It did a long time ago. I also exercise the internal mind to adjust my thinking when new knowledge sets show a better fit. Looking for blame within oneself, when done correctly, builds character and keeps a person from repeating mistakes due to ever-adjusting and expanding knowledge.

The application of the knowledge is yet another hurdle. I know a lot of good people that understand truth, but do not practice it. Fear keeps people from changing behavior and it's often associated with pride and fear driving the unbalanced state. Reality can sting, sure. But it's the easy road to flee back into ignorance when the going gets rough because of the coping mechanism to ward off truth and reality. It's about finding the best way of **Stayin' Alive**... that involves hormesis and balance... a mashup. This is what I found as the best fit.

### Scompy — 2016-03-08T05:13:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-190378>

**March 7, 2016.**
So I've been thinking, what is the worst of two evils?

1\. A place with good access to strong quality sun most of the year, but more frequency of nnEMF around every corner and less access to good water. (Los Angeles or San Diego)
2\. A place with poor sun (only in the spring and summer), but less nnEMF and more access to better water. (Seattle)

Both have massive population density. Both have massive nnEMF. Because localized environments can be controlled and mitigated to a point (nnEMF), it makes more sense to have access to the sun, or at least driving distance to get sun with little or no nnEMF. Also RF-EMF continues to expand everywhere; they are practicing 5G soon in Austin, Tx ([http://www.computerworld.com/articl...rials-of-5g-wireless-in-austin-this-year.html](http://www.computerworld.com/article/3032686/mobile-wireless/att-to-run-field-trials-of-5g-wireless-in-austin-this-year.html))

Locations far from the equator with limited sunlight (like Seattle) are going to be getting far worse for health no doubt. Limited UV assimilation frequency, reliance on DHA (most people are not doing or getting enough high quality), and ever-increasing nnEMF no matter where you turn.

### Scompy — 2016-03-13T22:33:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-190780>

**March 13, 2016.**
What JK advocates resonnates with every freakin' thing I've seen in my lifetime and relates directly to personal behaviors and/or not having a choice in the behaviors of the environment around us. We truly are all in a dirty-fish bowl of nnEMF and most of us don't use the fish-lamp either. This is where my first story starts.

When I was working at Stanford as a biologist, my boss "Nancy" was one of the most incredibly, smartest individuals I have known. As an example, she would do a crossword puzzle daily at our lunch break and finished all crosswords within about 5-6 minutes. I was luckly to get a few crossword matches in the whole puzzle. Crosswords are a great reminder about knowledge sets because although I knew at the time she had a vast knowledge bank, I could reflect and see how she made 'connections' where I couldn't do it...yet. I knew with time, I could get to her own knowledge set if I wished to pursue that slice of all of knowledge's pie. Also, she was a biologist and could tell you hundreds of scientific stories about crazy experiments that happened on campus back in the 1930s through the 1960s.

I can't get into the specifics here, but stuff like crazy sleep experiements, etc. Nancy was an extremely bright biologist, and also very obese unfortunately. She had not always been that way too. She didn't have a clue why she had health issues now and did everything she could to do the right things. I'm telling you, she could tell me the reactions straight out of a chemistry book in words as to what she thought was going on in her body, but knew she had some missing pieces to the puzzle. She also had a second career as a horse trainer. She would often go out and give a lesson at sunrise, but would be in the office by 8:30 or thereabouts.

Anyway, I digress a little. So where does this story go? When i worked with Nancy, I reflect back on the environment we were in and have all of the answers NOW 20+ years later. Between the years of 22-26 years old is when I started gaining weight and not understanding why. I biked to work in the morning from an on-campus location, which only took me about 5 minutes. When I got into my office with Nancy, we were in a backroom that probably wasn't ever meant to be an office at all. It was sandwiched around two corners and completely flooded under fluorescent lighting. The room had no windows, and even the hallway leading into the room had no windows. The entire building had stone awnings that also blocked most light around the building. As a matter of fact, the entire building was filled with fluorescence lights and the only windows were tiny ones on the doors to the individual labs.

Because of where the autoclaves, prep-rooms, warm and cold rooms were located, the only time anyone got outside was maybe for lunch because she was often absorbing a new book there. But most days, Nancy spent her lunch in that office, and throughout the day, she rarely left that desk. Often, I'd bike back to my dorm room at lunch to fix a meal and take a break. To make matters worse, the significant other in my life was telling me how all of the latest science was saying how bad the sun was. Even though I was biking to and from work, I'd cover up as much as possible and wear sunglasses. On rare occassions, Nancy and I would take a trip at lunch to the fish store and got some fish supplies. We had 4 aquariums in the office along with a huge 100+ gallon tank for an animal behavior research lab. For a mid-afternoon break, we'd often heat up some microwave popcorn and split it among us. Nancy would often leave by 4pm or thereabouts to go back to her horse-ranch and give more lessons.

About 2 years into that job, I was at my heaviest around 300 pound, though I was actively working out at the college's gym about 3-4 days a week. Even one day at the gym, I spotted a set for Tiger Woods because he knew I wouldn't let him down being muscular and very big/fat. No matter what I did, I would not be able to shed fat. Exercise didn't seem to contribute to anything but extra muscle. Of course at that time, I was also eating 'low-fat' because that was the latest trend. OJ, higher carb plates. I remember eating a lot of vege-garbonzo bean dishes, burritos, toast and whatnot. All of the SAD. Drank a lot of low-fat milk at that time too. I remember one brand of 1% milk that I replaced with my 2% and thought I was so healthy at 285 pounds. One day on the scale when I saw 289 and knew I was close to 300, I just buckled down and started starving myself with willpower, not changing my diet, not changing my environment. I sort of stabilized around 285 pounds with massive hunger pangs and bad-mood to go along with it. I mentioned this next part (in previous posts) that I took at vacation to Finland when I was 24 years old. I went from 285 down to 218 over 2 months and shocked myself . I was using saunas, jumping in cold pools, and I was in near lapland away from all electronics, but made friends with millions of mosquitoes the size of houses.

So upon returning back to Stanford at 218 pounds, I did what everyone would do and attributed it all to 'my food/diet differences' at that time. No matter that in the summer in Finland, I was getting 18 hours of daylight. As I got back into that office again to work for the new school year, it didn't take but a few more months to get back up to 260 pounds. I was pulling out my hair at that time to figure out what foods were 'causing this problem.' I even went so far to get many of the foods I ate in Finland and had them shipped for me to eat in thinking it may had been their kind of rye bread, or their soups, or something that could trim me down. My future self now wants to slap my past self across the face about the stupidity of not recognizing environment is everything.

About 10 years ago, I learned Nancy had died of cancer. I was so saddened to know that because I hadn't ever gotten to come back to Stanford and see her after I moved to LA. Even at the time I was there with her, she got a wound on her leg that wouldn't heal after 9+ months. The doctors didn't have an answer for her. As I put the pieces of our puzzle together working in labs, it occurred to me we had been extremely safe about all of the handling of compounds, etc. But every day between 8:30 am and 4pm, we were under fluorescent lighting in a tiny room with no windows.

I think about how the coffee pot she used was right next to her, the microwave, the fish tanks, etc. She was surrounded my nnEMF. Then I thought about the warm and cold rooms that were abutted to this office! Those had MASSIVE fans on them and I could hear them kick on/off from that office. The main fan systems must have also been causinng a ton of nnEMF into that room besides all of the other sources... the fishtanks, the electronics, computers, etc. This room was probably 15 feet by 15 feet at best. I'd really like to take measurements right now....I'd probably be shocked at what I would discover, and that's outside of the blue-light. Nancy held that job at Stanford for at least 30 years if my mind recalls correctly, maybe even longer. When she was an adult woman, she was very slim and rode horses all of the time. I had seen many pictures of her when she was in her early 20s. She was outdoors all of the time in that part of her life. It all changes when you live most of your life away from the major moments the sun is right outside, but you are under an alien one.

This is just one of many, many stories that I've seen first-hand in my life that is similar to this one. My grandfather's death of cancer will be the next story I'll tell because it's very-much related to abscence of sunlight and continual use of artificial light on a daily basis. Indirectly, they gave us the answers because of how we can see the environments they lived in and the correlation to artificial lighting and nnEMF is profound. There it is...right in front of our noses... blue-light from artificial lighting we only perceive as white light, and the invisible nnEMF pulsing through our bodies without having a clue. The forces are real. If there would have been physicists in the labs with us, maybe they would have helped connect the dots. I'm guessing even to this very day, most lab settings at Stanford are still like this one. I'm going to call them out right now if anyone is reading because it's exactly the professors and researchers in the colleges that could make a difference if they just spend a few hundred bucks and take the measurements themselves with the nnEMF around them that they work around. Then soon, hopefully they'll change out those blue-light emitting bulbs if they can still add 1+1.

### Scompy — 2016-03-17T18:31:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-191082>

**St.Patty's Day, 2016.
**
What is on Scompy's Mind today:

\* **Hertz.** So, what type of influence would screen-refresh-rates of 50Hz versus 60Hz have on the back of the eye, specifically the RPE? Because mitochondria are naturally tuned at 100Hz, I'm wondering if the flicker-influence of the light from a monitor when it's at 12.5Hz, 25Hz or 50Hz would be problematic for the RPE if exposed over time. I bring this up two-fold: the VR tech is strapped very close to the RPE and many games loose frames and are playing at 50Hz rather than pegging at 60Hz.

\***RPE DHA quality**: What happens if the body doesn't have enough DHA? Does it try to shove other "2nd-best" compounds if DHA is lacking? Thinking backup systems here.

\***Skin Chills**: Realized today that the thicker parts of my skin get chills and the thinner parts do not. Is this direct evidence that the skin doesn't need to 'condense' because when skin-thickness is really low, the sun is able to penetrate = no need to condense?

\***Foreign Foods = Farmicuticals**: JK reports accurately, "We need more Light Gurus, not Food Gurus." When looking at pharmicuticals on the molecular level, they are no different than foods, but simply artificial. They carry additional side-effects due to their foreign structures. Natural medicine does a good job finding pharmicutical replacements with less side-effects result. However, where most people trip up is that they putting all thought-processes in one egg-basket: that food is the answer to their problems. We fail to see the egg carton that holds the eggs it the first place, and at some point, an egg cracks without it.

\* **Family tree**. I'm part Irish, part German, part mutt (but not exactly Triumph above) So I found this picture of my Great, Great Grandfather and G-G-Grandmother on my father's side (James William Compton) and his wife (Malinda Sumpter).
They had 14 children altogether in the mid-1800s (bottom center and bottom left):

And to end this post, part of a nutritious breakfast? Really?! Don't watch for more than a minute or so.

### Scompy — 2016-03-19T03:50:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-191176>
