### Scompy — 2016-03-25T18:09:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-191501>

**March 25, 2016. (Little Things are BIG)**
For the past few weeks, I have been cracking my window when I take my son to school (maybe about a foot on each side) allowing natural light to get inside the car. I also implemented the cracking open the windows in the house in the morning, especially in the spaces he has been eating his breakfast. Enough to get the light in without making it too cold for anyone to fuss about it. (Fussing is ironic as you'll see below).

Fast-forward to today: What have I noticed? General moods in the home are much more 'even' and stable. Better yet, my 7-year old has not dreaded the mornings thinking about having to get to school... As any parent understands, at times like a lot of kids come up with excuses like no tomorrow to get out of school. My answer was cracking a bit more morning light into the home. I've seen real behavioral changes and literally no resistance or fighting back getting to school in the morning. This also worked on mom as well, not knowing what a bit of a crack to a window could do in the morning.

When everyone's mood is even, life is great, huh? I just had to be very stealthlike about doing it. Wake up...! The great precursor for a good mood is good dopamine. You can still get that on a rainy day, as Mr. UVA like to show up everywhere. So my takeaway is a few tiny changes can go a long way, especially if when understanding how light works to (reflects and absorbs) with specific materials. The little things we do can matter greatly!

### Scompy — 2016-03-28T08:25:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-191626>

**March 27, 2016. (Evening Light)**
So here is a question that I haven't seen contrasted...what benefits are we getting from later-day/evening sunlight? I'm thinking we've evolved to want it and getting it is better than not. For the past week, I have been getting home around the 5:30pm area and my son wants us to have some nerf-gun targeting battles outside. At any chance I have, I'm getting him outside, even if it's later in the day. Weekends are in the morning hours. And I believe I'm seeing great improvements already just being outside all of the time; plus we have grounding shoes! I do require us to wear 'eyewear' for protection, and we both have on blue-blockers (and we take drink breaks taking off the glasses especially in the morning).

### Scompy — 2016-03-28T21:36:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-191663>

**March 28, 2016. (Grounding Shoes--SCOMPY SAYS CAREFUL!)**
I have a new biohack analysis with Grounding Shoes in 2016: take care \*where\* you wear your grounding shoes now.

In some places, the floors inside of buildings and even outside in some places, energy is being dumped (dirty energy) into them. I've measured some stronger milliGauss coming off of the floor, especially with certain materials like carpets or because of where wiring is placed below us. In a strange way, being ungrounded in some higher energy environments may be a better hack than wearing grounding shoes. For instance, if you sit at a deskjob with grounding shoes, do you actually know what the floor current is doing where you sit? As a general rule, I'm now going more like Mr. Rodgers Neighborhood. I put on the grounding shoes when I go outside into nature vs. wearing them in city environments.

### Scompy — 2016-03-29T08:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-191689>

**March 29, 2016. (My Minecraft Torch Mod)**
If you have heard of Minecraft the video game, you may have seen products on store shelves. They have nightlight torches made for real life, and these emit a fairly low lumen light, but have a small LED bulb. Even though they have a natural coating and an opaque plastic cover, they will still give off blue-light. These fake-looking torches take x3 AAA batteries and are also wall-mountable.

Instead of tossing the torch, I decided to hack mine into a 'Redstone Torch' instead (which is also in the video game). I got some brake-light tape and wrapped it around the light-source. It not only cue the lumens in half, it's only outputting red illumination now.  It's not good enough to read under, but is about the perfect lighting when the power is out to move about.

### Joe Gavin — 2016-04-05T16:26:10+0300

> [Scompy said:](/goto/post?id=192056)

> **April 4, 2016. (Brain Pondering)**
> #13 (Al) back to #12 (Mg)... Why can't it happen or why doesn't it happen well enough in life? I'm first thinking because Al gets trapped in tissues or never makes its way to the mitochondria for recycling.
>
> Why am I thinking now that increasing overall dietary potassium might be a hack to help to minimize calcium effluxing...
>

found this on periodic table i use about post transition metals (Al is one, lead is another):

The diminished metallic nature of the post-transition metals is largely attributable to the increase in nuclear charge going across the periodic table, from left to right.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-Cox17-17) The increase in nuclear charge is partially offset by an increasing number of electrons but as these are spatially distributed each extra electron does not fully screen each successive increase in nuclear charge, and the latter therefore dominates.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-18) With some irregularities, atomic radii contract, ionisation energies increase,[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-Cox17-17) fewer electrons become available for metallic bonding,[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-19) and "ions \[become\] smaller and more polarizing and more prone to covalency."[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-20) This phenomenon is more evident in period 4–6 post-transition metals, due to inefficient screening of their nuclear charges by their d10 and (in the case of the period 6 metals) f14electron configurations;[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-21) the screening power of electrons decreases in the sequence s > p > d > f. The reductions in atomic size due to the interjection of the d- and f-blocks are referred to as, respectively, the 'scandide' or '[d-block contraction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/D-block_contraction)',[\[n 4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-22) and the '[lanthanide contraction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lanthanide_contraction)'.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-23) Relativistic effects also "increase the binding energy", and hence ionisation energy, of the electrons in "the 6s shell in gold and mercury, and the 6p shell in subsequent elements of period 6."[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Post-transition_metal&printable=yes#cite_note-24)

[http://www.ptable.com/](http://www.ptable.com/)

### Joe Gavin — 2016-04-05T20:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-192120>

and to further hijack Scompy's thread: look at Zn/Cu/Ni and Fe/Mn/Cr on the table. All of these are part of OXPHOS dismutation of ROS in cytoplasm and mitochondria.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide\_dismutase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide_dismutase)

thought this was an interesting entry from article:

_Mutations in [SOD1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOD1) can cause familial ALS (several pieces of evidence also show that wild-type SOD1, under conditions of cellular stress, is implicated in a significant fraction of sporadic ALS cases, which represent 90% of ALS patients.),[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide_dismutase#cite_note-pmid20399857-31) **by a mechanism that is presently not understood**, but not due to loss of enzymatic activity or a decrease in the conformational stability of the SOD1 protein._

"SOD enzymes deal with the superoxide radical by alternately adding or removing an electron from the superoxide molecules it encounters, thus changing the O2− into one of two less damaging species: either molecular oxygen (O2) or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). This SOD-catalyzed [dismutation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dismutation) of [superoxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superoxide) may be written, for Cu,Zn SOD, with the following half-reactions :

-   Cu2+-SOD + O2− → Cu+-SOD + O2
-   Cu+-SOD + O2− + 2H+ → Cu2+-SOD + H2O2

The general form, applicable to all the different metal-coordinated forms of SOD, can be written as follows:

-   M(n+1)+-SOD + O2− → Mn+-SOD + O2
-   Mn+-SOD + O2− + 2H+ → M(n+1)+-SOD + H2O2.

where M = [Cu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copper) (n=1) ; [Mn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manganese) (n=2) ; [Fe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron) (n=2) ; [Ni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nickel) (n=2).

In a series of such reactions, the [oxidation state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidation_state) and the charge of the metal [cation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cation) oscillates between n and n+1: +1 and +2 for Cu, or +2 and +3 for the other metals ."

### Scompy — 2016-04-07T18:47:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-192229>

**April 7, 2016. (Blood Clot)**
Yesterday I did some light exercise and realized my foot was hurting me after. After investigating it, I realized I have a small blood clot in the center of the foot (on the bottom). It feels like a knot and is painful to walk on it As a possible influence to the clot, I've been eating a lot more pickled asparagus this past week, and figured that I have a higher amount of Vitamin K going around in my bloodstream.

This is what I've done so far:
\--Upp'ed my Vitamin E for blood thinning
\--10mins of Redlight on the bottom
\--Lots of hydration this morning
\--I took the day off to keep off of it

What's nice is that it's an 80F day here, so I'm planning on getting a lot of UVB7 sunlight on the bottom of the foot (along with the rest of me).

### Scompy — 2016-04-16T17:47:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-192843>

**April 16, 2016. (More Cancer thoughts)**
From my POV as a weird hypothesis/idea, cancer may be an epigenetic survival mechanism built into us to try and get a cell to convert itself into a different type of cell to attack something in a greater size that it cannot breakdown normally. In other words, cancer could simply be a "macro-form" of regeneration. Here is what I was thinking. When some foreign substance is detected like asbestos that is of a structure that the body cannot breakdown, nearby cells may try to convert themselves into another type of cell to deal with it. For instance, a lung cell may try to turn itself into a liver cell because what may be in a liver cell's genetics could be ideal for the cell to try to eradicate the foreign body. So it may just be epigenetic expression gone a muck in tissue where it doesn't belong. If something like this idea holds any water, then you can have forces like nnEMF that could convert materials already in the body that are not foreign, into foreign materials simply because forces act upon them. I'm thinking now from the perspective of nnEMF and microwaves: turning beef into beef jerky. Good beef is what we want, but if you make beef jerky, it becomes a foreign substance and then nearby cells trigger regeneration to deal with 'unnormal beef.' Because of there is an initial 'ball' of cancer cells that surround the foreign body, it makes me think of the salamander when a ball forms to make a new arm when it has been severed. The nearby cells detects there is a loss or a problem, adjusts genetics accordingly. Some thoughts for this morning.

### Scompy — 2016-04-24T19:08:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-193374>

**April 24, 2016. (Touchy Subject: How you project information)**
I wanted to talk about a 'touchy' subject today that most of us, with the knowledge we have obtained. This directly deals with family members, friends and the people we love. The subject is about 'the way we live quantumly for our health' and 'the changes attempted and required to implement nature, hacks at home and work, etc.' At the core of what I've seen on this journey is the **_lack of appreciation_** when it comes to making others' lives more healthy. Typically, I see these 3 reactions in people in conversation that don't want to hear a quantum-perspective:

**1\. Purely Dismissive:** Person is completely closed off to receiving input about their health and wants to make no change. The person has a belief that rubs so hard against what is presented that an idea won't be entertained. Often this is because that idea would be in direct conflict to the person's identity and a 'house of cards' built up that stand up on that idea if it were to be shattered.

**2\. Fearful and Flighty:** Person hears the information, but the information is interpreted as fear-invoking and sparks a negative-feeling--literally a real biochemical response of a cortisol hit. As a result, the person 'does not want to heart it' and even if the knowledge is believed, the person wants to hear it at a later time or have the information presented 'less scary.' I often keep this person in-mind all of the time and attempt to _**spin-the-information**_ in a positive manner. But more important, it can be useful to _**not volunteer**_ information. Patience is required. Over time, let the person warm-up and don't be talky, even though you may want to change the person's health yesterday. Keep in mind these types will tell us they don't want to hear the information, when they may be actually curious about it. This puts the onus on us _**to be creative**_ in how to present quantum information. And to do it correctly, we must know the information well-enough to provide the knowledge. If the knowledge cannot be conveyed accurately, admit it if you cannot present it in a positive or matter-of-fact way, but let the person know that the details are known and can be found elsewhere like having them go to the Jackkruse.com website.

**3\. Prideful and Fighty:** Especially among the educated, they are open to 'listening' and having a conversation, but they need to be overly-convinced and any emotional-passion invoked into talking about a subject usually results in a breakdown. These type of detailed thinkers may just be missing quantum knowledge sets and live in science of conventional wisdumb. Usually these people have a label slapped on them as 'know-it-alls', the alpha-personalities, etc. More often than not, a prideful person will fall-back on their previously gained knowledge to advocate their points (and therefore no other perspective can be correct). And because they are 'fighy', they get huge cortisol hits that makes them fight harder when push comes to shove rather than fleeing. Keep in mind the emotional state of yourself and the audience to weave conversations for receptiveness.

I know there are personality tests that also try to label individuals, but I personally don't think they are that useful and can actually be damaging due to personal identification with them. We as humans are pliable and change in our outlook on a yearly, monthly, daily and even second-by-second basis in my opinion for many reasons including mood, whims, thought-processes, memories, etc. In other words, I think we're way too complex to slap a meta-label on each of us. There is some level of truth in personality tests, but the truth is grain-of-salt type of truth and a half-truth at best. I believe that personality tests are a way marketing-folk try to make sense of individuals they hire for justification about work-ethics and things more nefarious.

_**The approach in how**_ the information is presented is _**huge**_ for the reasons mentioned above. I keep in mind my audience and what I know about them first to create an altered approach to help make them more receptive and not fall into a pattern of dismissal. It's especially tough as we know because we're often dealing with individuals with crashed dopamine. But even more important, admit to yourself the type of person you are.... are you big-picture in your presentation, are you detailed, can you shift back and forth based on your audience? But even more important is the non-verbal. Can you just be a role-model and not say a word, but still communicate your biohacks and the way you live? When you have a child, you see how they are 'little emulators' and that they often start taking-on their parents behaviors.

Case-in-point this past year, my approach to getting my family to start wearing blue-blockers was to not-say-a-word unless asked about it. I started putting them on at night, and yes, on the first night, I had to prepare my 'happy approach' in how I presented them seeing me with blue-blockers worn. My approach was to 'sell the idea' with fun/humor in the first phase via emulation and having them get used to the idea that dad looks this way and he keeps doing it. Then at a later time is when I'd introduce additional Phases for more information given as to 'why I'm doing it.' When my family members asked me why I push back my monitors another 2 feet back on the desk, wear my blue blockers, and moved furniture around the home into locations (after they have watched me taking EMF readings all over for the past weeks), my approach was simple in Phase 1: "we're getting healthier!" One sentence is all that's needed. If they pry more, keep Phase 1 to a minimum. Often, the people you live around will come into conflict with the actions done. For instance, after moving furniture into a pocket of low nnEMF, if later you come to find the furniture is back in a high nnEMF region, don't panic and perform verbal vomit toward the other. That will get you nowhere fast. Instead, just keep moving back the furniture even enough it's an inconvenience. Do this 10x in a row and be happy about doing it, and at some point, the other person will stop moving it back or that person will come to you asking more details about why you keep doing that 'stupid' thing. That will strike up a new conversation to start opening Phase 2 for a little more info, but be cautious. Be ultra-mindful about how the information is presented, and keep in mind it's being done for everyone's health. Don't be shoving meters in the person's face, etc. Instead, just say, 'From what I've measured, this is optimal for our health.' Be a bit more matter-of-fact and only talk about details.

Revealing too much too fast is an analog as to why authors don't write what happens until about 5/6ths of the way through the book. Otherwise, if you knew the information within the first chapter, you'd be done with the book and label the author as bogus with an F+ grade at best. This is why we have certain memories of various teacher as being **_amazing_** in our past student lives. These teachers not only mastered the 'connective' aspects to our emotions, but they also knew the exact ways to keep us curious about the subject and present the subject in such a way to not have it be overly-matter-of-fact, but also have usefulness for our lives. We all know our first algebra classes when we hear the examples, "Joe is driving down at road at 45 miles per hour. Townville is 90 miles away. How long does it take for Joe to get to Townville? Let's find out!" These type of 'trying-to-be-real-world' examples are often disconnected from us, though you can see the irony because the writer is attempting to make a _**relatedness**_ to the reader for personal **_connection_** to 'make math fun' and show the 'usefulness of this information' in daily life. This example is trying to give a broad-approach for the majority of the readers assuming they are going to be receptive. Individuals, however, are more receptive to information in various ways and it's our relationships with each person to discover what may work the best.

As I've talked about in other previous posts, stealth-tactics are also key. The more stealthy you can do biohacks, but make a difference in others' lives, the better. For instance, I've hacked many fluorescent bulbs into Edison bulbs without anyone noticing at all. I've done a ton of hacks around the home that no one is aware of, simply because of the circuit breaker and having other power sources off. I've done many things with EMF by pushing fields away from other spaces. I've hacked my pantry for less electron-theft by moving more non-food crap into it due to field penetrations. I hack my ice and water for more EZ potential. 've had relatives and my son's friends over and they've benefited bigtime, but have no idea why. All of these things I don't tell anyone else unless they know or have asked in the past. Volunteering information does feel like a moral obligation to many of us when seeing thing happening around us, but the opportunity has to present itself or the information you're trying to convey will be a negative reaction in the person's mind, even though we know it may provide amazing health-benefits. The last thing we want is for others to have negative-reinforcement about the very thing that will help their health. So just keep in mind, there's a huge ramp of 'warm-up time' in many people getting the information. Don't underestimate emulation and the non-verbal... it's often more powerful than any spoken word.

Hi, I use a Cornet 88 for RF and magnetic. Then, the Trimeter for the magnetic and electric mostly. Best hack is to be hydrated more frequently. I've also found the ground can carry dirty electricity. Also, something as simple as pushing the monitors back as far as you can may help a bit. ### Scompy — 2016-05-01T08:37:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-193932>

**May 1, 2016. (Science Fair at End of Month)**
As you all know, I have a 7-year old and have him in a private school. I've had conversations about nnEMF and their lighting with the principal and send her literature on a frequent basis. She listens, but doesn't take any action. I get no responses back. Guess what is on the horizon: Science Fair.

I'm designing a board that will be called, **"Light Physics and its Influences on Human Biology"**

The boards at science fairs look a lot like this... [http://www.cdn.sciencebuddies.org/Dirs/5/14/image1.jpg](http://www.cdn.sciencebuddies.org/Dirs/5/14/image1.jpg)

The Overview will be breaking up the wavelengths of the sun and the relationships to RF EMF. I'll show with my Spectroscope the blue-light coming off of their fluorescent bulbs and also mention the lack of UV light.

Then I want to focus on:
1\. Benefits of Vitamin D with UVB+IR [http://www.fao.org/docrep/field/003/ab470e/AB470E23.gif](http://www.fao.org/docrep/field/003/ab470e/AB470E23.gif)
2\. Benefits of dopamine with UVA
3\. Benefits of setting circadian rhythms in the morning sunlight, disruptions of blue-light.
4\. Native vs. Non-native EMFs
5\. Microwaves, Radar, WiFi, Radio and giving examples of the volume and \*intensities\* of RF being produced. I really want to show the # of cell towers and antennas within JUST A 4-MILE RADIUS OF THE SCHOOL. [http://www.antennasearch.com/](http://www.antennasearch.com/)

Check it out.... here's the actual 4-mile radius photo based on the school location:

Hehe, no worries. I won't show that image to kids and their parents. I'm not the guy that wants to give them nightmares, although I'm a bit tempted. Worse would be the oscillation of tissues in a microwave and let them know about calcium-effluxing in humans. In every classroom, they have a microwave oven. And ultra-line-crossing would be myelin-related. All of the kids are going to be reading this, so it's a matter of making something where parents that are smart enough can read between the lines and understand light when they hadn't realized RF side at all, and the UV benefits.

It's gonna be epic anyway if I just stick to the 'benefits' of light and present how RF is part of that light without going into the scary details. Educating the teachers and admins... they are gonna be pretty pissed at me about the RF side even to mention it, I'm sure of it. If anyone has ideas or diagrams that could work well, I'd love you to post them to me

### Scompy — 2016-05-04T01:59:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-194126>

_**My vision of what may happen in the future...**_

This historical document outlines the ills of the "Era of Silent Poison" that we traditionally refer to as the period between 20th and 21st century just after electricity became mainstream across the world. It was during this time humanity was continually diseased and lived about half of their normal lifespans because of the misuses of artificial lighting, nnEMF and specific chemical agents applied to food and in water that often blocked healthy functions within the mitochondria.

This slower-poisoning was coupled with the conventional wisdom among the scientific community that caused massive behavioral changes to remove sunlight from their lives in fear of the sun and not understanding the biophysics of light. Additionally, as people used modern technologies, they unknowingly caused massive disruptions to their daily circadian regulation. Luckily, many pieces of the puzzle had been answered, including the 4th phase of water and Gerald H. Pollack's Nobel Prize in 2020 for the behaviors around water.

This conventional wisdom trumped the few scientists that possessed the quantum biology knowledge and attempted to get the word out. Dr. Jack Kruse was one of the first founders to unify the overall quantum biology by giving well-rounded insights as well as the specific details for the overall population, among the accomplishments he contributed to our modern science. During the mid 21st century, the Scientific "Civil War" erupted among mainstream institutions of the time including most medical organizations. This great debate lasted another 20 years. In that interm time, those with the knowledge of quantum biology (and put it into practice) saw great health benefits, while those that believed conventional wisdom continued to suffer. Over time, the knowledge and mechanisms of quantum biology were eventually assimilated by the overall population of the world. Ultimately, the once unconventional wisdom ultimately became more conventional and accepted wisdom by most socieities by the turn of the 22nd century. Since mitochondrial health is such a normal staple in the 23rd century, we actually owe a lot of thanks to those people that pushed against the conventional wisdom of that era.

It is hard to illustrate the overall lethargy and illness people felt on a daily basis that we do not feel in our modern era. The so-called 'normal' of that time was the long-term suffering of a plethora of insideous diseases: cancer, diabetes, bone loss, joint pain, obesity and the great number of neurological conditions that manifested later into full-on disease. Because the process of disease development was such a slow-motion tidal wave, most people were not aware anything was wrong because few had the knowledge to understand mitochondrial function. Instead, they over-focused on biochemistry, pharmicuticals and foods as cures rather than the fundemental forces of physics that created those substances in the first place.

Alas, for great grandmothers and grandfathers, they had a much harder life than we can fathom. This sharp contrast shows how things have changed in just 200 years....their lifespans were falling below 70 years of age, even with their technological advancements, food-choices, medicines, pharmicuticals and advanced surgical procedures. Historians need to understand how the quality of life suffered in the Era of Silent Poison, even among the doctors and nurses in their own health facilities.

Today in the 23rd century, we have established a secure and strong relationship between the quantum scientists and any new technological advancements to ensure we implement healthy technologies. In particular, we understand light physics and electromagnetics. We have an advantage that we understand how technology can share and manipulate the same physics within our own bodies. Although it is still uncommon to see a human live past the age of 160 in our era, for the first time ever, our world median lifespan has finally reached 100 years. This milestone may be the greatest accomplishment for human health.

### Scompy — 2016-05-08T07:52:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-194353>

**5/7/2016 (New Glutamate and NMDA findings)**

[https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/05/160503131508.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/05/160503131508.htm)

#1 ^^^ They never address \*WHY\* it is happening. JK sums it up nicely in other posts showing calcium effluxing leading to excitotoxicity. What I found interesting about this article was the idea of, "leaky and glutamate can build up in these intercellular spaces." I need to dig up a study I read a long time ago about the isomer-form of glutamate that was found to bind-longer to NMDA. That also leads me to believe there could be a 3D type of lock-and-key mechanism going on where the key gets 'stuck' in the lock (NMDA) longer than expected. It also brings up thoughts about the NMDA locks themselves and if they get worn-down enough due to lower redox that could cause glutamate to act differently than nature desires.

[https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/05/160502131511.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/05/160502131511.htm)

#2 ^^^ New structural findings in NMDA and interactions with its three states. This new look at NMDA was fascinating to me because of the relationship to the eye-camera, "These changes lead to additional rotations at various points throughout both the ATD and LBD, causing the ion channel pore to open, similar to the opening of a camera shutter."

However, I _**really despise**_ statements like this at the end of the article: "We hope that this new finding will help pharmacologists come up with better therapeutic compounds that have minimal side effects." Did these people ever consider looking into taurine?! It only makes sense to look FIRST at the compounds that are already meant to be used by NMDA instead of inventing something else with additional, unforeseen issues. Idiots, sorry, just pisses me off because I know their motivation is the $$, rather than doing science for science's sake.

### Scompy — 2016-05-10T04:53:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-194455>

I was looking at a friend's new job post on LinkedIn. Then along the side of my page, I saw a SpaceX related post that said "RF/Microwave." I so poked around. Now I feel sorry for the poor person that gets the position and has no clue about the physics interactions to his/her biology.

**RF/Microwave Engineer**
**[SpaceX](https://www.linkedin.com/company/spacex?trk=job_view_topcard_company_name)**
**Seattle, WA, US**

-   Posted 15 days ago

**Job description**
Engineer The Future SpaceX is a U.S.-based advanced technology company founded by residing CEO and CTO, Elon Musk. Founded in 2002, SpaceX builds rockets and spacecraft from the ground up, including cutting edge electronics, software, vehicle structures, and engine systems. The Falcon Launch Vehicle and Dragon Spacecraft programs are some of the most ambitious engineering systems in the world, designed to support our ultimate goals of aviation-like spaceflight capability and making humanity a multi-planet species. Our team is made up of more than 3,000 SpaceXers located across our Hawthorne, California headquarters; and other facilities in Florida, Texas, and Washington, DC. Advance the course of human history and pave the ways to Mars This is SpaceX. We are not like most companies. Our goal is to do what has never before been done--enabling mankind to live on other planets. We push the boundaries of what is currently possible, and understand it takes rare individuals to help us make this happen. We celebrate our successes, and each other. We seek future colleagues with a rare mix of drive, passion, scrappiness, intelligence, and curiosity to seek what’s beyond the stars. RF/Antenna Engineer Responsibilities: As a Sr. RF/Microwave engineer you will analyze, design and test RF/Microwave components and communication systems. The job includes: Simulating/modeling RF front-end systems, working with System Architects, Modem/DSP and ASIC engineers to partition functions between HW and SW domains. Deriving RF HW specifications for the RF system and RF blocks. Develop RF/microwave circuits, components and MMICs. Evaluate and characterize prototypes and RF components. Characterize and analyze full radio performance. Assist in the development of automated test equipment for lab measurements. Work with manufacturing engineers on production testing of RF avionics components and systems. Basic Qualifications Bachelor’s of Science Degree in an engineering discipline with emphasis in RF/Microwave circuits or electromagnetic theory Must have experience performing analysis and simulation of both linear and non-linear circuits such as phase-locked loops, vector modulators/demodulators, oscillators, mixers, filters, multipliers, synthesizers, low noise and/or power amplifiers Must have a thorough understanding of RF passive components such as hybrid couplers, Wilkinson dividers, waveguide, circulators, and directional couplers Must be familiar with radio related test equipment such as spectrum analyzers, vector signal analyzers, vector signal generator, network analyzers Must have experience characterizing radio performance in microwave frequencies Must have knowledge of digital communication systems, spread spectrum, single and multi carrier techniques and modulation types such as QPSK, APSK and QAM Preferred Attributes, Capabilities, Skills, and Experience: Masters of Science or PhD 3+ years of research and/or industry experience in RF/microwave circuit design RF/microwave design using Agilent ADS, Genesys, HFSS, or AWR Microwave Office Understanding of electromagnetic propagation and antenna systems Schematic and layout using Altium Designer or other PCB design tool Experience in Matlab, Python, C++ or equivalent tools for modeling and analyzing RF systems Python and/or Labview programming experience for test automation Digital communication theory with emphasis on bit error rate and system noise figure RF/microwave design in the Ku-band, K-band, and Ka-band frequency range ITAR Requirements: To conform to U.S. Government space technology export regulations, applicant must be a U.S. citizen, lawful permanent resident of the U.S., protected individual as defined by 8 U.S.C. 1324b(a)(3), or eligible to obtain the required authorizations from the U.S. Department of State SpaceX is an Equal Opportunity Employer; employment with SpaceX is governed on the basis of merit, competence and qualifications and will not be influenced in any manner by race, color, religion, gender, national origin/ethnicity, veteran status, disability status, age, sexual orientation, marital status, mental or physical disability or any other legally protected status. Applicants wishing to view a copy of SpaceX’s Affirmative Action Plan for veterans and individuals with disabilities, or applicants requiring reasonable accommodation to the application/interview process should notify the Human Resource Department at (310) 363-6000.

### Scompy — 2016-05-20T19:29:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195134>

**May 20, 2016. (DEEP THOUGHTS BY SCOMPY)**

Yes, what you are about to read could be an analog to the Saturday Night Live skits from the 80s--in those times when we were getting massive blue-light from 11:30pm to 1am, but often we just stopped watching at 12:30pm due to the hit or miss musical acts. Anyway... I'm showing my age.

I've been thinking many years now that it's "the invisible" and the "small things" that ultimately destroys us. The invisible to me is not being able to feel what our mitochondria are doing, nnEMF, etc. and also emotional "small problems" that are viewed as "trivial" so they never get dealt with, but slowly and insideously load up over time and spill over as a tidal wave of stress. It also makes perfect sense these "small issues" and "invisible forces" are never dealt with because we simply do not perceive them as a problem.

We've often been told, "don't sweat the small things." That was actually a quote of many of life's wisdoms that my grandfather gave me at the age of 20 on a list out of the 1940s. I think "not sweating the small stuff" should only be applied to anger and frustration... Don't let it get the best of us. But a point of clarity here..."don't sweat the small things" doesn't give me the right to dismiss a small problem from being fixed. It can be a hard line to walk at times--to me at least the cleaving of emotion from matter-of-factness. Compartmentalization is part of the key to fragment a small issue away from the emotion it invokes.

As we acquire more experience and age as people, we've figured out how to deal with certain problems. This is in part how we create some sides to wisdom, but also how we don't get too stressed out and bothered over issues at all. Experience with death is also huge, is it not, to see it face to face? Smaller things in life we like to over-focus on, in comparison, look trivial. But in a strange way, that's part of how the small issues might go on and on and on because they are never dealt with and resolved. Resolution is necessary to keep stress lowered, and if we don't have outlets to share our experiences with, stress can build.

The comparison I'm going with here is what Joe Gavin said recently on another thread: many of us are frogs being slowly boiled without realizing it... the boiling are the smaller things in life and the invisible things we don't perceive that are damaging us. In combination, these adjust the heat toward boiling.

### Scompy — 2016-05-24T19:34:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195275>

**May 24th, 2016 (Scompy's Fatloss 2-Week Biohack Working Great)**

I have personally found most of my gains also come by having a late lunch and no dinner: I.e., intermittent fasting. Because leptin/insulin levels are usually baselined after X-TIME, then the body starts kicking in more ketosis for fat-burning during sleep cycles. X-TIME=somewhere between 8 to 12 hours later on average based on a few factors:

1\. What was last consumed (like if you had carbs/sugar at dinner like this is saying above is bad)
2\. Too much protein at dinner (because it cycles against ubiquitin tagging as well as getting backwards converted at times to glucose: keep protein below 20 grams for me personally)
3\. Current insulin resistance issues: the slower it takes to baseline insulin, the longer it will take to get into fat-burning during the sleep cycle
4\. (Also, HGH increases during the 15th - 18th hour of the fast)

When I has less insulin sensitivity in past and taking blood-glucose measurements, I had to push my fasts a little longer to baseline my glucose to ramp up my fatburning. So I did this hack in the past 2 weeks with CRAZY results. I've been around 180 pounds since my trip to Disneyland (up 15 pounds in the last 5 months plataeu'd), and my jeans in my legs were getting a bit too tight, so I decided to rekindle my fat-burning knowing my past IF results. I did this:

1\. I stop eating around 2:30 to 3pm each day (late lunch), ending my last meal with seafood typically... shrimp, sushi, salmon, etc. Maybe about 25-30g of protein altogether
2\. Drink ginger/roobios tea in the evening with some really good water sources, about 3x a week do some light weightlifting with core workout, squats, leg movements, etc.
3\. Get to sleep around 10:30, blue-blocking of course all evening
4\. Wake up around dawn and notice my breath is rancidified (due to ketones from ketosis, wife hates life, keep mouth away from her  )
5\. Get out into sunlight for 10-15mins, stretch, breath in a lot; eat breakfast around 7:30am, high protein, typically mixed with some onion, garlic, pickle
6\. More water; a tiny bit of dark chocolate in the morning only right after breakfast to coincide with cortisol rise
7\. Lunch getting outside rather than eating (eating sunlight), then around 2:30, get the final meal in for the day

Granted, I'm getting less calories overall.. maybe in the 1800 - 2000 range (I'm 6') vs. 2500 having 3x meals a day. Doing this pattern for just 2 weeks, I've been losing fat at too high a rate! I've had to actually push my calories up a little more so I'm not losing more than a pound a day. I'm already back down to 170 pounds using this method, but know it's fat because of how my pants are fitting in my legs and the mirror.

I should add that because my mitochondria are working more efficiently, this has got to be the largest contributor to the fatloss efficiency. In the past, I'd have to fast 18-22 hours on average to get results at all--like taking it to 1.5 meals a day and eating 1600 cal. Now a few years later at the age of 46, it appears I fast for 15- 17 hours to get massive results quickly. I believe this is because my insulin sensitivity is better, so I baseline my insulin faster to start kicking in nightly ketotic cycles faster. Quantlet later in the year is going to make this even better. Overall, I'm also ramping up my carbs now that they are in-season--probably close to 60-80g a day. I'm still eliminating all grains/breads and all dairy including cheese. I may try to reinto some feta later in very small quantities. When I reach my goal again based on how I look in the mirror (probably in a week), I'll weave my IF down to 2x per week. Right now, I'm doing 4 days on, 1 day off, and on my off-day, I'm stopping eating around 5-6pm instead. That will be my maintenance mentality. If I find my clothes start feeling tighter again, I'll just increase back up the IF. It's interesting how powerful a tool it is in my toolbox along with CT.

### Scompy — 2016-05-24T22:22:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195284>

**May 24th, 2016 (Scompy's Mitochondria)**

After watching the bootleg video from the Biohacking summit...Now I'm deciphering my mitochondrial line. My grandmother (mother's mother) grew up in Kansas and California (Los Angeles) then moved around during the war when she had my mother at various army bases when she had my mother. My mother was also born in the Los Angeles area. Grandmother later in life moved to Oregon, developed lots of cardiac issues and demensia in her mid-70s. Died around the age of 85.

My mother developed diabetes in her mid-30s after she also moved from Cali to Oregon, full on diabetes by her early 40s. She spent a lot of time indoors in that time period. She became overweight/obese in her 50s and passed at the age of 69 due to diabetic complications and artery calcification. I also know that my great-grandmother (mother's mother's mother) developed cancer later in her life also that took her; she was a smoker. I have to be extra-mindful knowing my weaker stock. I developed insulin resistance in my mid-30s after being obese in my 20s. Going ketotic at the age of 40 and using a lot of the 3-legged stool in the past year, I'm more or less cured for now.. I'm glad my son's mother and his grandmother are from a stronger stock.

Some things in common: when mother and grandmother moved from south to north, their conditions got worse I'm suspecting due to less sun. As a matter of fact, the worst issues with my mother was when she was in the Seattle area...Our family has always taken a lot of vacations to Hawaii, luckily. My mother and grandmother were the best being in southern Cali and lower parts of the country. My mother temporarily moved to Tuscaloose, AL and lost a ton of fat in that time and almost got off her insulin injections then. But a year later, they moved to Chicago, and a year after that, they were back in Oregon and all of her diabetic issues came back ful-swing. My analysis really drives home about ZIP and the personal environment we put ourselves within.

### Scompy — 2016-05-25T19:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195331>

**May 25th, 2016 (Scompy's Extended Fishtank Analogy)**

Imagine you have 3 goldfish. Goldfish1 is super-healthy and was an olympian. Goldfish2 is obese with continual migraines. Goldfish3 is just a standard guy, standard health.

Goldfish1 and 2 are put in **TankA** with abnormal water content. Although the water looks clear, the water is slowly poisonous without them being aware. Goldfish 3 is in **TankB** with standard water, no poison. Goldfish2 dies in just a few weeks after being put in **TankA**. Goldfish1 survives really well for about a year, but then starts becoming lethargic, develops insulin resistance and gains extra weight.

Goldfish1 believes what fish-society tells him, "Wow, I'm really becoming a slacker in the past year. I need to get to the gym, workout harder and eat better." And so he does. As he works out and eats higher quality food, he sees some improvements, all the while his mitochondria are still being damaged by the insideous poison in **TankA**.

Another year passes and he believes he has made some progress when the FishDoctor says he's been diagnosed with a disease. Goldfish1 scratches his head and believes he didn't do enough, or work hard enough to get healthy. So he cranks up even better food, better exercise, etc. even better than his olympian days.

But another few months pass, and Goldfish1 dies. In all, Goldfish1 lived a fairly normal life, cut a little short. In the meantime in **TankB**, the semi-slacker Goldfish3 with normal health has just been hanging out having fun. He lives on another 10 years beyond Goldfish1 and dies at an old age having a very long life. He ate normally, worked out from time to time, was just a standard Joe. FishDoctors scratch their heads and label the guy as "just having good genes" with little or no knowledge of epigenetics.

This story is meant to illustrate what is going on around us: physics that we are unaware of. Most of the physics around us now is artificial and manmade. Cellphones, WiFI, fluorescent and LEDs, Radio and Microwave are all forms of light, invisible to the human eye, but they are here. Few question the biophysical impacts upon our organelles. Our health entirely starts and ends with the mitochondria. Learn the quantum biology to have a longer life, what powers the mitochondria, what negatively influences the mitochondria in all of its detail.

**^^^Please steal this if you want it.
\--Scompy**

### Inger — 2016-05-25T21:42:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195345>

> [Scompy said:](/goto/post?id=195275)
>
> **May 24th, 2016 (Scompy's Fatloss 2-Week Biohack Working Great)**
>
> I have personally found most of my gains also come by having a late lunch and no dinner: I.e., intermittent fasting. Because leptin/insulin levels are usually baselined after X-TIME, then the body starts kicking in more ketosis for fat-burning during sleep cycles. X-TIME=somewhere between 8 to 12 hours later on average based on a few factors:
>
> 1\. What was last consumed (like if you had carbs/sugar at dinner like this is saying above is bad)
> 2\. Too much protein at dinner (because it cycles against ubiquitin tagging as well as getting backwards converted at times to glucose: keep protein below 20 grams for me personally)
> 3\. Current insulin resistance issues: the slower it takes to baseline insulin, the longer it will take to get into fat-burning during the sleep cycle
> 4\. (Also, HGH increases during the 15th - 18th hour of the fast)
>
> When I has less insulin sensitivity in past and taking blood-glucose measurements, I had to push my fasts a little longer to baseline my glucose to ramp up my fatburning. So I did this hack in the past 2 weeks with CRAZY results. I've been around 180 pounds since my trip to Disneyland (up 15 pounds in the last 5 months plataeu'd), and my jeans in my legs were getting a bit too tight, so I decided to rekindle my fat-burning knowing my past IF results. I did this:
>
> 1\. I stop eating around 2:30 to 3pm each day (late lunch), ending my last meal with seafood typically... shrimp, sushi, salmon, etc. Maybe about 25-30g of protein altogether
> 2\. Drink ginger/roobios tea in the evening with some really good water sources, about 3x a week do some light weightlifting with core workout, squats, leg movements, etc.
> 3\. Get to sleep around 10:30, blue-blocking of course all evening
> 4\. Wake up around dawn and notice my breath is rancidified (due to ketones from ketosis, wife hates life, keep mouth away from her  )
> 5\. Get out into sunlight for 10-15mins, stretch, breath in a lot; eat breakfast around 7:30am, high protein, typically mixed with some onion, garlic, pickle
> 6\. More water; a tiny bit of dark chocolate in the morning only right after breakfast to coincide with cortisol rise
> 7\. Lunch getting outside rather than eating (eating sunlight), then around 2:30, get the final meal in for the day
>
> Granted, I'm getting less calories overall.. maybe in the 1800 - 2000 range (I'm 6') vs. 2500 having 3x meals a day. Doing this pattern for just 2 weeks, I've been losing fat at too high a rate! I've had to actually push my calories up a little more so I'm not losing more than a pound a day. I'm already back down to 170 pounds using this method, but know it's fat because of how my pants are fitting in my legs and the mirror.
>
> I should add that because my mitochondria are working more efficiently, this has got to be the largest contributor to the fatloss efficiency. In the past, I'd have to fast 18-22 hours on average to get results at all--like taking it to 1.5 meals a day and eating 1600 cal. Now a few years later at the age of 46, it appears I fast for 15- 17 hours to get massive results quickly. I believe this is because my insulin sensitivity is better, so I baseline my insulin faster to start kicking in nightly ketotic cycles faster. Quantlet later in the year is going to make this even better. Overall, I'm also ramping up my carbs now that they are in-season--probably close to 60-80g a day. I'm still eliminating all grains/breads and all dairy including cheese. I may try to reinto some feta later in very small quantities. When I reach my goal again based on how I look in the mirror (probably in a week), I'll weave my IF down to 2x per week. Right now, I'm doing 4 days on, 1 day off, and on my off-day, I'm stopping eating around 5-6pm instead. That will be my maintenance mentality. If I find my clothes start feeling tighter again, I'll just increase back up the IF. It's interesting how powerful a tool it is in my toolbox along with CT.

Love this. My sleep is so awesome when I eat an early dinner, before 4 PM is best.... I really feel how good it does me
Need to tighten up on this one. Have had way too much big late dinners with the fiance, no bueno...huh!
I feel like shaping up a little now  thanks for the inspiration

### Scompy — 2016-05-27T01:42:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195433>

**May 26th, 2016 (Am I Allergic to Tumeric?)**

So last night at 2:30am I woke up in hives... mostly on fatty tissues, but all over my body. I stopped eating around 3pm yesterday, and at 7pm i had my typical tea with cut-up ginger. But this time, I added a some cut-up real organic Tumeric root. At the bottom of the tea-glass, I chewed on a few pieces of the ginger+tumeric.

Is it expected to have hives everywhere from tumeric root? Did I crank up too high a dose somehow? I immediately hydrated in the middle of the night realizing what was happening. By 7am, only a few of the remaining welts remained and by mid-morning, all were gone.

My first thought is that the curcumin worked to attack fat-cells to help liberate some of the toxic compounds stored in them. By 2am, I am thinking I was very ketotic already having 12 hours without any food (only tea). Maybe the itching a reaction to stored toxins and was a good thing...

Anyway, if anyone has had experiences with this same reaction, I'd like to know.

Btw, my 23andme had two recommendations: 1 encouraging tumeric and 1 avoiding tumeric for certain gene expressions. I don't think that's related, but I'll post if it's a piece of the puzzle.

### Scompy — 2016-05-27T21:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195489>

**May 27th, 2016 (My 3-Legged Stool + Intermittent Fasting + Ketotic influences, my N=1 observations)**

\[posted online in a quantum thread, and believe it also belongs here\]

HGH spikes during a fast... From my personal experiences as a 46 year old now, I've woven IF and ketosis since the age of 39. I originally learned on leangains, but also learned about ketotic diets then as well and have done many biohacks related here. This means you're talking to someone that has done this for 7 years now and I know all of the positives & negatives, especially with and without sunlight exposure doing them. I've experimented with 18 hour daily fasts, 15 hour daily fasts, periodic 24 hour fasts, water fasts, etc. I've experimented at all times of the day doing the fast, and with doing it with or without ketosis going (by measuring my blood glucose and my blood ketones at various times in the day).

My conclusions have been doing ketosis \*alone\* with no fasting does little to get me to lose bodyfat. Instead, the best tool in the toolbox is daily 16-19 hour fast that coincides with fasting during sleep, eating a huge protein breakfast (like 8am) and moderate-sized late-lunch (like 3pm) that is more balanced overall. Exercise ideally comes just before the late lunch, but if it's a light-exercise, I've found doing it around 5pm gets more fatloss going in the middle of the night. All workouts are now outside in sunlight.

The key thing I keep in mind is leveling out the insulin and leptin levels...and to get blood glucose normalized. So I ask myself, 'how long it takes for the body to do that?' Having a higher carb-lunch at 3pm (like 30-40g of carb and 10g of sugar) might take 12-14 hours later for everything to fall in-line and start up fat-burning. So on those days, I have found it beneficial to weave in some light anaerobics to pull the normalization time from 12-14 hours back down to 10-12 hours.

Granted, I had no fatloss doing any IF in the first 4 weeks when I tried this 7 years ago because I had so much stored up glycogen when I had been eating 100 to 200 grams of sugars daily on average. It took awhile to burn off the sugar-stores and really get into ketosis. Ketotic to me has been right around 20 grams a day window in the winter and about 40-60g in the summer when I get more sunlight. Calories don't matter at all to me if I'm weaving in intermittent fasting because there's limited time to get enough food for the day in general. If I'm not fasting, I do have to be mindful to not take in too much food. IF appears to work best in the summer.

Minimizing nnEMF exposure and increasing good-water sources are also key here...I get most gains on the weekend away from nnEMF. Ketosis doesn't kick in well or at all if I'm not getting good sleep. I know this buy how I feel in the morning, if I can look toward the sun without tearing up, and the strength of my breath (how many ketones are on them). A long fast for me (past the 14th hour) can run the risk of waking me up due to getting foot cramps, because the combination of ketotic+IF can drain salts easily in the body like low potassium.

Also, I pee more when I'm burning fat, so that extra urine can wake me up. Hydrating immediately in the morning is needed if I'm weaving IF+ketosis, especially if I had any diuretic like dark chocolate the previous day. That can synergize negatively to dehydrate too quickly/too much, ESPECIALLY if I had worked out the previous day like too hard and sweated out a lot. In my world, exercise can be overdone when weaving these, when a lot of people will still have the attitude of, 'no pain no gain' and get themselves to the hospital.

So the last 3 weeks or so now, I've pushed my daily 12-14 hour fast up to the 17-19 hour window and each day, I'm losing 1/2 pound--almost too rapidly. Now granted, this isn't necessarily all fat, but I do notice strong morning-breath indicating I'm getting strong ketosis by morning around the 18th hour. I look a blood ketone last week in the morning and found it at a 6 mmol, which was cranking a bit too high, so I incorporated a little more carb into my lunch now and pulled my IF window from 19 hours down to 17 hours as optimal for not stressing the body too much.

Now after 3 weeks again, I'm down about 10 pounds overall and my clothes at those fat-deposit places are fitting losely again like a year ago. Granted, I'm doing a lot of things right with a lot of sun in the day, lots of hydration, etc. But the IF alone is making a huge catalyst for the additional fat-shredding. That extra 4-6 hours of fat-burning a day when I sleep ha been the most effective fat-shred time... the 2am to 7am window in particular when I end my late lunch around 2pm the previous day.

Once I get back down to around a 10% total bodyfat (I'm around 12% right now), I'll probably cycle the IF about 2x per week (@18 hours) for maintenance and the benefits for my age like the HGH. So of all of the tools my the toolbox, I'd say that sun, water and minimizing exposures to nnEMF is #1 to make IF and ketosis work well. The best tool in combination has been IF in my experiences, with a lower-carb diet with lots of seafood in it.

Like I've said, I can get away with more carb in the summer if I'm getting more light and working out under the light. Ketosis is more of a hack when the light isn't around. In the past 2 years now, it's the first time in 20 years I don't have hunger, my blood-glucose is great and I have no lethargy. This is my n=1 at least at this current time. If you're doing ketosis and have the "keto-flu", you're probably not getting your environment correct, but it also may be related to dehydration and salts also.

During my 17 hour fasts now, I've been doing a lot of herbal teas in the evening or iced-herbal teas to keep hydrated. Overall, I'm looking to be down around 165 lbs. again. Probably another 2 more weeks to go to get back there where I was last summer. Note, from last fall through the winter into this past spring, I did \*no\* intermittent fasting and slowly over time and typically had a 12-13 hour fasting window,...during that time, I gained fat back (about 15 lbs.)... in part, no sunlight, more nnEMF exposure, some airline trips, etc. Just cranking back up the window another 4 hours made all of the difference to shred the fat again for me in combination with more sun also.

sad for you - I would take nettle plus quercetin - I stopped a bee sting reaction in 5 minutes with it... curious, if you took something sharp and "wrote" on yourself, does the red mark stay up? As far as glucoronidation goes, since it is a phase II detox, the body slaps a glucose molecule or something like that onto the toxin to dispose of it - well wouldn't you know it, there are bacteria - I used to know the name of it - that will actually eat the sugar and kill glucoronidation - then you reabsorb and get to do it ad nauseum - I like the Garden of Life Primal Defense as a general probiotic -

You are sending me back 7 years ago when my son developed urticaria... weirdly, the dermatologist prescribed an insanely high dose of zinc sulfate - I should have listened to him as zinc is ill absorbed by the stomach unless there is enough acid - and you need zinc to make stomach acid - so take betaine HCL with the zinc, get a product called Ulcetrol that has zinc bound to l-carnosine or take lozengers -

Make sure you take \*lots\* of nettle throughout the day - I also used it to stop my firstborn's itching issues a while ago - and you can use it to stem all those wifi nose bleeds - what a crazy world we live in -

Your analogy of the goldfish was spot on - hopefully you see the orange tinges around yourself when you look into a mirror... I'm seein' it in mine... although, I prefer the boiling lobster analogy personally

Do you have a Sperti light? I think you should get one - remember, you need vitamin d, iodine, benfotiamine and zinc for a happy tummy

### Scompy — 2016-05-28T04:21:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195501>

**May 27th, 2016 (EDUCATING THE EDUCATORS: Son of Scompy 1st Grade Science Project)

**A COLLECTION OF COLORS**
\*What is Light?
\*Light comes from the Sun.
\*Light comes in waves.
\*Light has different colors.
\*The height of the wave makes the color.
\*The tallest color is red.
\*The shortest is violet.
\*Think of R.O.Y. G. B.I.V. for the rainbow.
\*There is some light our eyes cannot see!
\*Some longer colors lions can see.
\*Some shorter colors bugs can see.
**
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I was very proud of my son. We all wore our blue-blockers at the event. We also had a spectroscope at his display for breaking down the overhead fluorescent lights in the classroom. When people asked about either the spectroscope breakdown of color, or why we had on blue-blockers, my son answered them as his EXACT quote, **"Blue light at night is bad for us because blue-light _smashes_ melatonin. This is why I have on these yellow-colored glasses because it _resists_ the blue light from getting into our eyes So if you want better health, don't get light at night."** From the mouths of babes.

At least 3 sets of parents were convinced this was why their own children were having sleep disturbances after his presentation. Even his teacher agreed that the TV and electronics at night at not healthy and supported his project. I also had my cornet meter, and 2 sets of parents wanted to know more about cell-phones being light reading the chart on the lower-left above^^^. I showed them and was getting over 3 millWatts/Sq.meter due to the # of parents in the classroom all with their cell-phones periodically pinging the cell-towers and they did hiccup. Final mention: the Sunlight/LED/Incandescent/CFL breakdown of each color spectrum really shocked most people that compared. Everyone, including the teacher, had no idea about the blue-peaks.

### Scompy — 2016-06-01T01:10:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195753>

**May 31, 2016 (Another N=1, I deplete a lot of Potassium)**
Although I haven't had my blood potassium measured, I'm pretty dang sure now that I've been suffering on and off from a lack of potassium. Here is my reasoning.

**Potassium draining observations/influences**:
\--Ketogenic diet in general
\--Diuretics (like dark chocolate, can also push BP too high)
\--Too much exercise in one sitting
\--Stresses (adrenal, mental, etc.)
\--Surgery (dumps because of stress)
\--Vomitting/diahrrea (dang that word is hard to spell)
\--Sugar (insulin as a trigger at the **Sodium<-->Potassium pump**, also why bananas are a fail and avocado is a boon)
\--Alcohol use (don't generally use unless it's wine in the summer)
\--Possibly some types of Salt (like straight NaCl because the wants to conserve sodium over potassium)
\--Calcium Effluxing? (Ca:K balance disturbed? I'm thinking so...!)
\--Low conversion potential (because Ca is stuck outside of the cell and cannot get into the mitochondria for it's magic: April webinar)

**My N=1:** In the past and even somewhat recently, if I workout harder like in the yard or play soccer, I run the risk of getting a combination of cramping and/or heart twiches. Often in the evening, I would get a full-heart arrythmia. Even after being really well-hydrated, it didn't seem to matter. I knew how to remove both the muscle cramping/heart issues by adding in a little bit of himilayan salt, but didn't know exactly what was out of balance. Now I'm very confident it's a Potassium deficiency. In my particular case, I was doing too much 85% dark chocolate in the past in one sitting, a diuretic. That sometimes often to a quick bowel movement (that word I cannot spell well), which also dumps K+. In the past like 4 years ago, I had huge fatigue, lethargy outside of insulin-resistance issues such as feeling heavy and I didn't have any more endurance during soccer games. Then extreme muscle cramping in the middle of the night in the toes/calves.

Yesterday, I replaced decking in the backyard for over 4 hours and got a bit of heart hiccups and knew what was about to go down with my muscle-cramping, etc. But this time I was smart in thinking about the Potassium need, and for the last meal, chose foods higher in potassium content. Blam, the heart issues went away and for the rest of the night, not one muscle cramp. Success!

**Dietary sources of potassium**.
\--Avocado
\--Squash
\--Shrimp/scallops
\--Spinach, Collards, salads, etc.
\--Wheat grass (maybe in high moderation only 1x per week after sun assimilation)
\--Lighter colored beans (but I won't do because of carbs and lectins)
\--Mushrooms (don't really like, but has a lot of K)
\--Bananas (no way, too much sugars)

Stomach acid creation is also gated a bit by potassium also, so this could explain a lot as to why I highly benefit from ACV in the morning before breakfast. Note in my case, I have been doing 7 years of ketosis, and only in the last 2 years, weaving out ketosis during spring and summer for a low-carb diet while getting WAY more sun. The worse times with my arrythmia and muscle cramping was about 3-4 years ago. Oh dang, looks like I have to eat more shrimp and squash for the summer.

### Scompy — 2016-06-01T22:49:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-195824>

**June 1st, 2016 (Supercharged?)**

Hmm, hopefully Supercharged is not too Undercharged. As for me being in the entertainment industry for well over 20 years now, here is what I am looking for: The quality of any movie begins and ends with the core team of script writers for narrative, seeds of mystery, flow, reveals, then what's left on the editing floor for splicing. Actor delivery to me is secondary, because flat performances rarely bust a movie. Many documentary script-writers leave out the secret sauce that makes a good movie stand the test of time.

I'd personally love the JK knowledge base getting out to the public mainstream at some point. Doing an Indiegogo or Kickstarter seems appropriate at some point. Then, assembling a key team that knows the quantum biology to do it correct. I've already had many thoughts about what the movie would be... first, the whole movie would be tinted in amber to minimize mitochondrial damage to the viewer and ask all cell-phones be turned to airplane mode as the movie opens. I have another 100 more thoughts on this...

If JK ever does a movie in the future, my advice would be for **JK to pull a "JK Rowling"** on the people that attempt to control is work, mainly the director, writers and editors, to secure the key messages and connections for reveal, flow and takeaway to the audience. I know we have many writing experts here in the ent. industry that knows these processes inside and out to make a good movie, game, etc. Some of us could be tapped.

### Scompy — 2016-06-08T05:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-196127>

**June 7, 2016**
As I was looking up at the sky, I realized the clouds have lot of stories to tell... a LOT of ?natural? EMF going on in Seattle today. At the park today, there were 25 people (me included) out on the grass soaking up the sun. However, of the 25 people, 23 had cell phones and were actively using them. The 24th person that didn't have a cell phone was me. One last person was a guy super-tanned from head to toe and away from the bulk of the people. I mentally wondered if he had quantum knowledge by his behaviors.

So I grabbed my Cornet and took a measurement of the 25. No Bueno in the main grassy field in the park... hits and peaks of 2 to 4 milliWatts/Square meter. I figured because there is no cell phone tower nearby, people are on 4G pinging towers and bringing in the signals. I did find a grassy location about 50 feet away where no one was sunbathing that was 1/30th that nnEMF (in the 0.05 to 0.2 range). Proximity does matter. This may be a lesson learned for some travelers... let's say you want to go to Hawai'i for a vacation at a resort. The public areas will be loaded with crazy volumes of nnEMF.

When I was a kid, I used to stay at the Hilton Hawaiian Village in the Rainbow tower. I often started the day by going down to the arcade and dropping in $5 worth of quarters. Then by 10am, I'd do some boogie boarding but could only be out in the water for 45minutes before getting too much sun. My parents kept me out of the mid-day rays from noon to 2pmish, but then most days in the 2-5pm window, I'd be back out in the Pacific again. Looking back on that, I'm grateful they took me there so often for the sun... especially in the dead of winter when I lived in Oregon at the time.

### caroline — 2016-06-08T07:10:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-196129>

We came upon an older gentleman \[85y0\] at the rock pool at the ocean very early one morning last week.

He was very lean and strong and very tanned ...and he was doing a series of squats. I stopped to chat with him.

Later we saw him sitting on the rocks - off by himself. He was reading and had brought an esky with his breakfast.

I felt very proud of us ....we were there early too - to have a swim in the rockpool and then lay on the rocks ...and we had brought an esky with our breakfast.

We continually see a lot of older people at the ocean early in the morning - what a win/win!

early morning sun, the ocean and good friends!

None of these folks would have a clue who Jack Kruse is ..... but they instinctively are doing great things for themselves!

We feel honoured to be part of that group.

I have seen one woman over the last 4 years in the rockpool. She comes 4 days a week.and loves it the most in the winter! She had a very serious accident and this is how she is healing herself.

You just reminded me about a 55 year old man I saw today doing everything wrong in the park's parking lot. I notice how so many people get so many things wrong now. This man had a cane and was walking very feeble trying to load his car with various items...for a 55 year old, he was behaving like he was 90. The super-thick sunglasses immediately made me want to tell him, "Hey, you might want to get some sunlight to get rid of your arthritis due to the inner workings of the brain stem." That was the thought running in my head with thinking about backing it up telling him to read John Ott's Health and Light if Lady Fate opened up a window to chat.

I did get to chat with two elderly women a few weeks ago that struck up a conversation with me about why I didn't have my sunglasses on and their fear that I might get a sunburn. They didn't know what they were getting themselves into... as fate would have that conversation, they had gone to my alma mater at Oregon State University, so it was a great transition from having studied biology there and our Pacific NW ties. By the end of that conversation, I had them both dumbfounded and convinced, using their own knowledge about their parents that had lived in the early 1900s... asked them questions about how they grew up with little electricity, using the sun cycles, eating for the seasons, etc. Changing tactics is really good when you know your audience and what may appeal the most.  At the end of the conversation, I had both women without their sunglasses on, and they looked encouraged to get outside first thing every morning into real light.

### Scompy — 2016-06-10T19:00:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-196320>

**June 10, 2016 (Walking into a Tarp)**
Just a quick mention, as I was going into work today, the sun came out behind the clouds and radiated massive heat down on me. I have my UVB meter in my car, and at 8:45am, it registered UVB4. But I only had about 30 feet from me to the door into the office. I soaked up 5mins of sun before going inside. Then I realized I must walk into the giant tarp. And inside that tarp having different forms of visible and insivible light alien to nature... I now visualize Mother Nature herself shaking her head at all of the life around her that hides and hunkers down within their self-manifested cages.

### Scompy — 2016-06-11T19:01:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-196385>

**June 11, 2016 (Wissmart vs. Wisdumb)**
Wisdom is the ability to decipher between right and wrong without the need to experience if something was right or wrong. So as a designer, I have dealt with 30+ years of game progression systems from all types and genres of games. I have also written and published many games in the past before getting into video games. Most systems use 'experience points' when a character has destroyed monsters, solved puzzles, made good relations with other people, etc. Experience points then level-up characters to unlock more abilities as well as root attributes. Back in the 80s when D&D was really popular, I played the game a lot with my peers and many of them complained about 'wisdom' as a core attribute of a character. Here was the breakdown of the character's attributes, the scale was often between 3 to 18, where the range of 8 to 12 was pretty average.

**Strength**: Muscle, physical prowess, ability to hit and strike with more damage, etc.
**Intelligence**: Book-learned information, memorization; ability to cast magical spells
**Wisdom**: Application of book-learned information, perception, creativity and deduction; ability to commune with one's deity to gain supernatural abilities
**Dexterity**: Hand-eye coordination, balance, avoidance potential, attack speed, etc.
**Constitution**: Health, resistance to damage and poison, contribution to Hit Points (the span between life and death)
**Charisma**: Influence over others, control/power, self-projection, how well words stick, beauty and even types of charm/magic in later systems

So why am I writing all of this...? In thinking about our mitochondrial health and the mechanisms that allow us to give us wisdom. A strong link to dopamine and other neurotransmitters? I think so. But what strikes me now is that all of these 6 attributes are tied directly to mitochondrial health! In every system under the sun that exists, they always divide out constitution or health or a similar attribute and lump all of the character's health into it. Now I'm on a path to make a more accurate model. You see, even in gaming, health has gone off the rails!

### Scompy — 2016-06-12T23:12:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-196462>

**June 12, 2016 (Park under nature, not indoors)**

My father that was diagnosed with Parkinson's this past months had his panels done. I've been telling him to get outside every morning, not have his glasses on, drink more quality water and reduce out the sugars in particular. He is one of these guys that still drinks a coca-cola from time to time even though he has gout flareups from time to time ...Now with the Parkinson's diagnosis, it has hit home with him. They have him on an L-dopa medication, but after I poked around a bit, I don't think it will be a long-term solution for him. He's gotta embrace nature RIGHT NOW IMMEDIATELY if he doesn't want this to progress rapidly in the coming year. Any insights below?

**Metabolic panel:**
Sodium: 140 mmol/L
Potassium: 4.7 mmol/L
Chloride: 104 mmol/L
BICARB: 23 mmol/L
Glucose: 83 mg/dL
BUN: 20 mg/dL (a year ago 28)

Coldplay "Clocks":

The lights go out and I can't be saved
Tides that I tried to swim against
Have brought me down upon my knees
Oh I beg, I beg and plead, singing

Come out of things unsaid
Shoot an apple off my head and a
Trouble that can't be named
A tiger's waiting to be tamed, singing

You are
You are

Confusion never stops
Closing walls and ticking clocks
Gonna come back and take you home
I could not stop that you now know, singing

Come out upon my seas
Cursed missed opportunities
Am I a part of the cure?
Or am I part of the disease? Singing

You are, you are, you are
You are, you are, you are

And nothing else compares
Oh nothing else compares
And nothing else compares

You are
You are

Home, home where I wanted to go

### Scompy — 2016-06-24T20:26:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-197191>

Thinking with the RF... maybe youre meter is picking up a different measurement, not milliWatts, that is a x100 difference because it's using a different european standard? I say this because you get 0.055, but I get baselined at 0.0005. So we're probably seeing the same thing, but you don't see it way out to that decimal place. I get 0.0005 milliWatts/sq. m minimum... cannot get any lower than that too. I never expect to see a 0.0000 milliWatt/sq. meter because of the normal background RF.

In magnetic, I can see my milliGauss pulse because of the meter's AC. Usually baseline it pulses between 0.05 and 0.06. I'm unsure if your meter is weighed for background magnetics or not, that could also be what you are picking up. Your home environment appears fine if this is 0.34 milligauss. If it's actually 34 milliGauss, that's not great. A good tester would be to push the meter at this screen in front of you right now. All screens put out a field, so it can be a great gauge to test the monitor against other sources to get a landmark for the magnetic EMF going on in other areas of the home.
