### Scompy — 2016-06-26T22:56:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-197330>

**June 26, 2016 (Getting closer to the Red White and Blue)**

Cortisol (and glutamate) are both are activated in the morning cycles to wake us up. I am getting extremely curious about what light wavelengths these compounds could **emit** and **mechanisms that trigger light release in the body.** We know cortisol responds to blue light. Could I make a small leap that cortisol emits blue light in our bodies also? Or maybe there is an internal triggering systems to emit blue-light when there is no sun around? Would mitochondria have that nm capability?

In contrast, we have the later day melatonin and redlight. One might also assume that optimal conditions would be the emission of redlight later in the day and we know mitochondria are capable of this nm range already. But a more important point that makes me think...what happens when we screw up our mitochondria emission range? I.e., what happens if the mitochondria are not emitting what is expected, and then, could that alone stifle melatonin's creation in higher quantities?

On a related note, I'm thinking about various emotional responses especially fear, anxiety, happiness and joy. Could there be mechanisms triggering internal blue light and/or red light emission into our bodies and burning us out faster or giving us time? Now let's think about meditation, prayer, positive thoughts, etc., and especially when we do these things at various times of the day. Are these emitting light shifted toward the red-light spectrum? It would be amazing to measure the compounds and if they have responses to various nm light frequencies. In thinking about blue-light so well-known to be cancer-stimulating for night-shift workers while blasting circadian rhythm into oblivion, and then thinking about what later-night blue-screen tech does to us, it makes me think about more subtle mechanisms not-considered for creating blue-light internally at the wrong times. The nights when something 'bad happens' at 10pm to shoot up cortisol, we know it can lead to a poor sleep, but on a quantum level, could this mean more blue-light created within the brain and body, or simply, unexpected wavelengths and therefore signaling problems?

Since water normally has a high dielectric point (78) this means that the charges it receives from incident light can directly affect its electric abilities photoelectrically. Once water is hit by full spectrum sunlight the dielectric rises to 160. That defines a photo-electric battery. Angular momentum is one of the ways electrons are linked to magnetism. Temperature also links to electron spins and so do electric fields in membranes. The dielectric constant in water is a molecular mirror for these effects. The dielectric constant measures how effectively water can shield the negative and positive charges from one another and hence reduces the force between them. This is why water fundamentally breaks symmetry in nature because it is very effective in separating electric and magnetic charges from electric and magnetic forces. This is why water and UV light are the ideal natural Faraday cages in nature's bag of tricks. I would remind you that heat released from a fat burning mitochondria shrinks or condenses water when it interacts with infra-red light. This is why frozen water floats on liquid water and does not sink; heated water shrinks and tightens things. When we tighten the hold around a mitochondria we bring respiratory proteins closer together to increase quantum tunneling speeds between respiratory proteins. This increases the electric charge of membranes and it makes the ATPase rotating head spin faster inducing a magnetic field. What do magnetic field draw? Paramagnetic ions. Oxygen, Ca, Mg, K+ all fit that bill don't they. This is why the mt genome was deleted to 37 genes and why only 13 control the respiratory proteins that critically make oxygen the only terminal electron acceptor in mitochondria. Quantum design is tidy. Cold thermogenesis does this because exposing your surfaces to cold cause heat release in mitochondria from inside of our bodies and not on the surfaces where light and temperature of the environment interact with us. This is why cold environmental temperatures = increases magnetism in tissues = increases angular momentum, "m1". Boom........we are not just electric folks........we be PHOTOELECTRIC."

When the power goes out up here in Seattle in the autumn and winter, it's often 45F or lower outside. So instead of leaving my fridge-food in the home, i just put them in sealed bags outside on the deck overnight. Nature in action.

**July 17th, 2016
**
Robert O. Becker, Cross Currents, Chapter 9: ELF and the Mind/Brain Problem
Page 225: "It is obvious that ELF fields do not alter the operation of the visual system. For example, standing directly under a 60-Hz high-voltage power line does not produce any visual sensation at all."

Scompy says no to this above ^^^, because in some people like me, I can see visual spots manifest in my central vision in 60 Hz systems due to the rupturing of some blood vessels in the back of my eyes. The back of my washer/drier is a simple way for me to have this happen in the past. Not so much now unless the field strength is higher.

### Scompy — 2016-07-18T05:31:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-198518>

My pumpkins and squash came to life today, just after 5 days of planting! This year, I changed the formula. First, the sun is able to hit the pots most of the day. I am using better water (more EZ-forming), only clay pots that touch the earth and my last year's compost. It was like magic. I have a make-shift partial-greenhouse to help prevent some animals like crows and squirrels, and to close off the greenhouse fully in the event of a windstorm or a super-hard rain. But for the most-part, the plants are open so bees and such can easily come in and fly out, etc. The smaller clay pots are bell pepper starters of various colors... green, red, orange and yellow.

### Scompy — 2016-07-21T00:42:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-198702>

**July 20th, 2016 (An Alarming Idea)**

Hmm (a big hmmmm), so I was thinking about something that could be a quantum business idea. How could any Jane or Joe know if they are in an electromagnetic field? It made me think about Fire Alarms. They don't tell us the intensity of a fire and they just approximate with fried eggs...just if a smoke threshold is reached.

So what about an EMF Alarm that has a built-in tone for magnetic, electrical and RF? I'm thinking there could be two models...one for the home and one that could be taken with someone. I think the levels would be set about a lot higher than the desired standards, something like breeching of 5 milliGauss (vs. 1 milliGauss) or 0.3 milliWatts/Square meter (vs. 0.01 mW/Sq.m). That way, this **EMFStupidMeter****(TM)** would do a great job of alerting the individual when levels change. Not ultra-loud and annoying either. And possibly there would be a few modes for setting the ranges higher or lower for breeching influences. This way, it could be cheap and effective to make, and a person could have a basic understanding if an area is not clean from nnEMF of a specific type and at certain times of the day to make the connections.

This is what happens when it alerts you...THE RED ONE HAS BEEN CHOSEN!

### Linz — 2016-07-21T16:05:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-198725>

Good news with the seedlings. We have had success with our vegetables using a biodynamic calendar for planting/transplanting at the best time according to moon phases to optimise water movements in the plants. I try to water early in the morning and have planted lots of nasturtiums in gaps to try and keep the ground moist underneath as our soil is very dry despite heavy rain almost every day throughout June and the first half of July. Brassicas under nets as the cabbage white butterflies have just arrived. We also use PKS copper gardening tools.

### Scompy — 2016-07-22T01:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-198768>

**July 21, 2016 (What the Heck!)**
Someone riddle me this: How can Cell Phone towers continually emit more than 10 milliWatts per Square meter and not be fined or reported? Is this an enforceable law? Is it up to the citizen population to report it?

There are two towers I can measure in the 5 mW to 20 mW range I measure all of the time. One tower in particular is placed about 300 feet from a hillside, and there is a road along that hillside at the same level as the tower's top. Lotta homes there, a church, apartments, etc. I'm seriously thinking about leaving notes for the neighbors that live there telling them the RF they are under.

Another tower I'm really pizzed off about because there's an elementary school within 200 feet of it, and it always shorts out my AM radio when I pass that school.

### thisbirdhaswings — 2016-07-22T04:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-198773>

> [thisbirdhaswings said:](/goto/post?id=198769)
>
> [https://www.fcc.gov/general/tower-and-antenna-siting](https://www.fcc.gov/general/tower-and-antenna-siting)
> I need to look more thoroughly myself but this is a starting point.

(b) would cause human exposure to levels of radiofrequency radiation in excess of Commission-adopted guidelines

Applicant provided insufficient information for the Commission’s staff to determine whether the proposed facility satisfies § 1.1307(b).
(From [http://wireless.fcc.gov/nepa/EA\_checklist.pdf](http://wireless.fcc.gov/nepa/EA_checklist.pdf))

From [https://www.fcc.gov/general/nepa-faq](https://www.fcc.gov/general/nepa-faq):
**Q. Which government agency or office can assist us in determining whether or not a proposed construction may require an environmental assessment (EA) under [section 1.1307](http://frwebgate.access.gpo.gov/cgi-bin/get-cfr.cgi?TITLE=47&PART=1&SECTION=1307&TYPE=TEXT)?**

A. The Commission is not an expert agency on all environmental matters. Therefore, in evaluating environmental assessments prepared and filed pursuant to NEPA, we defer to the opinions or judgments of other agencies with expertise over a particular subject matter. These agencies can also assist you in determining whether your proposed construction may affect the subject matter over which the agency has expertise, and they can offer suggestions on how to mitigate any impacts. The federal and certain state agencies with expertise and jurisdiction over each of the categories in [section 1.1307](http://frwebgate.access.gpo.gov/cgi-bin/get-cfr.cgi?TITLE=47&PART=1&SECTION=1307&TYPE=TEXT) are as follows:

-   **Wilderness Area:** [U.S. Department of Interior](http://www.doi.gov/)
-   **Wildlife Preserve:** [U.S. Department of Interior](http://www.doi.gov/)
-   **Endangered Species:** U.S. Department of Interior, [Fish and Wildlife Service](http://www.fws.gov/) You may also contact the Commission's staff biologist, Joelle Gehring, 202-418-2356, [Joelle.Gehring@fcc.gov](mailto:Joelle.Gehring@fcc.gov)
-   **Historic Property**: Contacts with the State Historic Preservation Officer and federally recognized Indian Tribes are required under the Nationwide Programmatic Agreement. For further assistance, contact the Commission's Federal Preservation Officer, Stephen DelSordo, 202-418-1986, [Stephen.Delsordo@fcc.gov](mailto:Stephen.Delsordo@fcc.gov)
-   **Indian Religious Site**: : Contact the Commission’s Federal Preservation Officer, Stephen DelSordo, 202-418-1986, [Stephen.Delsordo@fcc.gov](mailto:Stephen.Delsordo@fcc.gov), or Anne Marie Wypijewski, 717-338-2508, [Annemarie.wypijewski@fcc.gov](mailto:Annemarie.wypijewski@fcc.gov)
-   **Floodplain:** [Federal Emergency Management Agency](http://www.fema.gov/)
-   **Surface Features:** [U.S. Army Corp of Engineers](http://www.usace.army.mil/)
-   **High Intensity Lights in Residential Neighborhoods:** [Federal Aviation Administration](http://www.faa.gov/)
-   **Excessive Radiofrequency Emission:** Federal Communications Commission's [Office of Engineering and Technology](https://www.fcc.gov/oet/)
-   **Migratory Birds:** U.S. Department of Interior, [Fish and Wildlife Service](http://www.fws.gov/) You may also contact the Commission's staff biologist, Joelle Gehring, 202-418-2356, [Joelle.Gehring@fcc.gov](mailto:Joelle.Gehring@fcc.gov)

And:
[https://apps.fcc.gov/edocs\_public/attachmatch/DA-11-192A1.pdf](https://apps.fcc.gov/edocs_public/attachmatch/DA-11-192A1.pdf)

**And: [https://transition.fcc.gov/Bureaus/Engineering\_Technology/Orders/1996/fcc96326.pdf](https://transition.fcc.gov/Bureaus/Engineering_Technology/Orders/1996/fcc96326.pdf)**

**Appendix B in above document lists more specifics about radio frequency exposure limits. Begins on page 83.**

**And: OET Bulletin No. 65 (August 1997)**
**[https://www.fcc.gov/general/oet-bulletins-line](https://www.fcc.gov/general/oet-bulletins-line)**

### Scompy — 2016-08-12T04:52:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-199531>

**August 11th, 2016**
So, you have have seen my pumpkin plants growing... I'm going to sacrifice two of them (one pot) to sunscreen this coming weekend. Spray-on sunscreen on the leaves to see what happens.
Here is the current status:

### Scompy — 2016-08-12T21:32:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-199564>

**August 12, 2016**

Has anyone recently experienced their social media comments being deleted? I had a few people thank me this week for posting about some general biophysics and chronobiology with blue-light at work in a youtube comment. (Posted on a low-carb "natural-cure" doctor's youtube channel). Then within hours, my message was deleted. I proceeded to message several times more for a week on different videos too, and again, all of my messages were taken down. I've seen this a lot actually over the past year on more generalized social media sites. Because of the track-record here, I am beginning to believe that some saavy and reputable people may know the light physics are more important than food, but they do not want the biophysics and quantum information public in any way. At best, it's a morally broken peg-leg to stand on in my book and an analog to book-burning in some 451 F heat.

I am coming to a conclusion that we are living in a time where few people are aware of the biophysics going on inside of us. We have to hold strong for this reason. Compartmentalization is the common educational TACTIC in schools for promoting only a slice of science wrapped up in a narrative bubble, rarely promoting relationships among the sciences. We've got a ton of geeky over-focused physicists at CERN for example that probably know squat about biological processes. At the same time, we have a medical system that is tied up with macro-bio processes and not very often considerating of the quantum or physics at hand deeper in the cell--usually due to their own educational background promoted. Those doctors and scientists that have a persistence and hobby to keep informed and read between the lines are more likely to find the connections and relationships among the sciences...those that truly want to benefit humanity (and all of life for that matter).

This division and wedging of each scientific discipline appears to be growing in the scientific community with vast volumes of disinformation and poor studies that are not controlled for the more basic physics in play. It does make for the ulitmate conspiracy theory.... that there are forces at work that want to keep the separation of physics and biology apart for financial gain. For these reasons, I personally believe this is why my comments were taken down. Will many will go to great length to omit and distort the information around the biophysics at work? Will they then offer food products, exercise equipment, pharmicuticals and supplements for the solution where money can be made? Of course, because they satisfy the 'Fast Food shopper.' These 'quick results' are just bandages vs. using nature for the long term cures. Welcome to the internet and the 21st century.

### Scompy — 2016-08-13T02:58:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-199574>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=199565)
>
> happens to me everywhere

I can only imagine what's going on with you. This deletion/omission mentality across the internet is starting to piss me off.

Here is an initial summary of my POV:
\* Group A thinks quantum biologists are crazy folk for their connections and dismiss specific studies and connection points to fit their needs
\* Group B believes quantum is woo with little to no understanding of the Photoelectric Effect and Light mechanics
\* Group C only sees health manifesting from pharmicutical creations, food, exercise and the supplement angle putting trust into 'the experts'
\* Group D are bunch of physicists that don't understand biochemistry and biology that well and strangely see physics apart from the human condition
\* Group E does not have a deeper understanding in one area of science from Physics-->Chemistry-->Biophysics-->Biochemistry-->Biology
\* Group F are scientists that over-focus in their realm on genetics and do not yet have a knowledge of epigenetics
\* Group G are scientists that have yet to find out about the newer discoveries with exclusion zone water physics and how light interacts with it
\* Group H believe protons, photons, electrons, neutrinos and the like are abstractions of science-fiction and dismiss delving deeper into the interactions
\* Group I are not willing to give up their past educational wrongs and are not open to personally adapt to new information presented
\* Group J know the biophysics in play, but are making money off of the ignorance of others and are fine with their lowered health
\* Group K are afraid of the sun and were duped into believing the negative influences of artificial lighting experiments somehow equals sunlight
\* Group L are the vast population of having a common high-school science education that read newpapers/magazines for their beliefs
\* Group M have little to no scientific education and believes every recommendation of their doctors
\* Group N are nefarious groups with an agenda to discredit, alter and suppress information for retaining political control: the "EM Ace" in their pockets

Just a tiny uphill battle...

### Scompy — 2016-08-15T02:06:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-199617>

**x3 Time stills from experiment: 9am, 1pm, 4pm

So far today, it doesn't appear my plant is twisting back... it may have been a wind-change, etc. But I did notice at 1pm around the highest wilting time, the leaves on the back got the most UV sunlight. So this appears to be possibly a ramification of the heat inside of the greenhouse itself rather than the sunscreen application. I'm suspecting this because of the control-plants in the back and their wilting and recovery. Tomorrow, I think it's best I apply the sunscreen to both sides of the leaves from here on out...**

### Scompy — 2016-08-27T22:55:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-200335>

Still digging around into beta-oxidation and came across this paper. Once again, I wish researchers would control for light. I wish they'd retest this study under various nnEMF and blue-light conditions because of their assumptions. I think it would be jaw dropping...

"The discussion above assumes that glutamate itself is not the initial trigger for the exocytosis of insulin-containing granules (we assume that the trigger is a glucose-evoked rise of ATP level closing ATP-gated K+ channels), but that the flux of glutamate through the secretory granules leads to the granules being more acidic and less positive in potential, and thus modulates the amount of insulin release occurring. Initial claims that glutamate potentiated secretion [\[9\]](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960#pone.0022960-Maechler1), [\[10\]](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960#pone.0022960-Hoy1),[\[26\]](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960#pone.0022960-Rubi1) were based on the observation that a rise in cytoplasmic glutamate concentration correlated with increased insulin release, but were disputed [\[27\]](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960#pone.0022960-MacDonald1), [\[28\]](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960#pone.0022960-Bertrand1) when it was found that insulin release did not always correlate with the total islet glutamate concentration. These discrepancies may be partly resolved by our data, which suggest that it is not so much the total islet, nor even cytoplasmic glutamate concentration that is the important regulatory factor, but the flux of glutamate through the secretory granules, which regulates the granule pH and membrane potential. Thus, we think that alteration of the glutamate flux through secretory granules is the mechanism by which altered glutamate levels modulate insulin release. Even though a simple relationship between cytoplasmic glutamate concentration and secretion is not to be expected, because the simulations depict that secretion is largely dependent on both the granule \[H+\] and its membrane potential, granule energetics could be affected by cytoplasmic glutamate levels (cytoplasmic glutamate must present to be transported through the granule). Consistent with this is recent data showing that knocking down a mitochondrial glutamate transporter (which will presumably alter the cytoplasmic glutamate level) also leads to a decrease of insulin secretion [\[30\]](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960#pone.0022960-Casimir1)."

[http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0022960](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022960)

### Scompy — 2016-09-16T20:45:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-201514>

**Fun Friday, September 16th, 2016**

[http://www.rush.com/songs/the-spirit-of-radio/](http://www.rush.com/songs/the-spirit-of-radio/)
**
Analyze the lyrics quantumly! I always had wondered about the lyrics of this song, they never made any sense to me until now. I'm thinking Neil Peart in 1980 had a strong clue about what was up, especially considering the "Invisible Airwaves" passage.**

**Music: [Geddy Lee](http://www.rush.com/band/geddy-lee/) and [Alex Lifeson](http://www.rush.com/band/alex-lifeson/)**
**Lyrics: [Neil Peart](http://www.rush.com/band/neil-peart/)**

Begin the day
With a friendly voice
A companion, unobtrusive
Plays the song that’s so elusive
And the magic music makes your morning mood

Off on your way
Hit the open road
There is magic at your fingers
For the Spirit ever lingers
Undemanding contact
In your happy solitude.

Invisible airwaves
Crackle with life
Bright antennae bristle
With the energy
Emotional feedback
On a timeless wavelength
Bearing a gift beyond price –
Almost free…

All this machinery
Making modern music
Can still be open-hearted
Not so coldly charted
It’s really just a question
Of your honesty

One likes to believe
In the freedom of music
But glittering prizes
And endless compromises
Shatter the illusion
Of integrity

“For the words of the profits,
Are written on the studio wall,
Concert hall –
Echoes with the sounds…
Of salesmen.”

Just thinking about this- I punctured one of my toes and cut another one open and needed stiches and received a DTaP vaccine- because you know to protect me and all. This was a month ago. I went to the nutritional response doctor I see from time to time and he said that the adjuvants in the vaccine are affecting my whole body. When we did further testing one of my main allergens (Ig response) was the MSG in the adjuvant. Along with 12 others. The girl was saying how it's worse of a response because it's not going thru 1st pass metabolism through the gut but straight to the blood. Makes me really think about how many times my son had this effect through vaccines and how his result would be different (he has Aspergers and ADD) if these events had not taken place. He had a C diff infection 3 months prior to getting his MMR.

It's crazy that I never even gave MSG a thought with the vaccines until today. I always figured the Mercury, aluminum, formeldahyde, roundup were the things I needed to worry about. At least with the Chinese food, we have a gut to hopefully filter some of that shit out but shots going straight to the brain are a scary thought when it comes to my boy or any one's kid...

Mercury, aluminum, etc. are all bad stuff of course... the glutamate for me can have a very bad impact with already a lowered redox. I know this specifically with my central-vision visualizations. Better than a spectrophotometer. I also get tinnitus, but the visual dots that appear for me lets me know as a fair-warning my nnEMF is off. I'm hyper aware because I've had this for over 20+ years. 1:1 correlation with bad nnEMF now, and a high glutamate meal can get the spots going much longer with more influence...I think in me, what I suspect is that the glutamate has more of an impact with my cardiovascular system and the capillaries. Beef jerky and Parmesan are the worst for me in general with nnEMF. Getting into sunlight eliminates the issue also. So this directly shows calcium channel blocking...

### Scompy — 2016-09-24T02:21:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-201834>

> [michellehp said:](/goto/post?id=201819)
>
> Just got the email from school that we can sign up for flu shots for our kids for next week. Oh thanks so much!

These are the times we live... I think it will take a few decades from now, and the medical establishment will have to change one way or the other. They'll look back on this time period pre-2020 and realize the damage, but only if they really embrace truth and are not sold the old narrative and bill of goods to keep them bandaging problems instead of practicing more root-cause medicine. Root-cause in my book starts with the physics and getting that correct.

For the reason of physics, I believe my son seeing how he has changed in the past 3 years was really a result of getting his nnEMF cleaned up and getting him way more hydrated in the day. I shut down all electricals in the home at night and tossed out many of the appliances that would have been generating fields too frequently. Hacked his workspaces, computer screens and eliminated all of the RF for him. He still does play some video games on the PC (not console), but it's only during the times of the day real sunlight enters the room. Past dusk, we do other things now like have stories or play board games. Remove all of the blue-light. He also has blue blockers that he wears past dusk. He plays minecraft too, but doesn't zone out, but always has a plan and goal in mind. Some of the worst nnEMF instances I noticed in his past were a lamp near his bed that when he got into it at night and it was on, he'd get 'itchy eyes' and want to put water in them. Another one was when he got his haircut, and an electric razor was used. That terrified him, and now we should know why many kids freak out. The barbers will tell you it's the sound and feeling of the cutting...I think many are sensitive enough to know the EMFs they are under.

So now about 2 years later, my son actually wants me to shut down the power every night...he says he likes it better. The electrical systems cannot be overlooked. I think many people may focus on the RF and magnetic fields, which is great. But they may also be sleeping all night closer to wiring where energy is being pulled by the heating system, fridge, etc. He also knows how much light heals him. Fingers crossed, but so far this entire year, he hasn't yet come down sick. That's incredible thinking about 2 years ago when he would get sick at the drop of a hat. Also, we haven't given him any kids vitamins or supplements. When I first told my wife that he wouldn't get any supplements and everything would have to come from real food, she just about flipped out as any mom would trying to 'do the right thing' for the kid. But over time, she saw the evidence that what I recommended was working. In particular, more fluids, more soups for more fluids, better water sources, more food earlier in the day, less at night, and getting outside as much as possible. I do some ice-cube hacks where I leave my metal trays outside in real sunlight to get the ice to melt for about 15mins. So I guess that would be some 'power cubes' I'm making. The #1 thing I made sure was to get the window fully open (there's a screen on it) while he is inside in that room for more than an hour or two. Also, getting a better sleep pattern to be asleep by a certain time and waking up closer to when the sun is coming up and grounding when possible. With all of these changes, I've seen him morph into a 'normal boy' or the normality of what I experienced in the mid 1970s. That's all we want as parents, huh? It's such a different world now.

### Scompy — 2016-09-27T06:31:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-201948>

**September 26th, 2016 (Last Lamenting)**
A new day dawns tomorrow for my family. Scompy has been through much over the past few weeks that cannot yet be revealed in full. Mysteries, oh yes. I will say this... I'm leaving Seattle for central Oregon. I'm going from 28% sunny days per year to around 72%. In addition, I have direct access to UVB all-year round. But the most important aspect of this move has to do with population density: the lack thereof. My career just gets better as well with far more people that want to go quantum and see the value. The nnEMF for Scompy's daily life will be about 1/10th the amount or even better. Plus, I'll have more access to friends from high school and college, as well as my relatives. I grew up in the Willamette Valley to the west, and it's only a 3 hour road trip through forests to get to the Oregon Coast.

In times like these, even under crazy stress with moving logistics and keeping the family in one piece, it's good to reflect on the positive things that Seattle brought me over the past six years being up here. Strangely, seeing the lack of sun in first-person was a gift...an eye-opener. It's what helped bring me to learn deeply about the quantum and light, and how it's intersecting our health. Before I had been in Seattle, I had been living in Los Angeles for over 16 years... access to sun daily, but never really using it. Not having the sun woke me up after a year in Seattle. There was a period of time from 2010 to 2011 where there was not one sunny day from September until the following February. My wife kept telling me it was like she was being pounded by Terry Tate Office Linebacker being up here without any sun was a "rude, painful surprise." Me being a previous PacNW person, I knew what to expect...sort of.

### Penny — 2016-09-30T23:03:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202141>

So I read Dr. Wallaces "Mitochondria as chi" you referenced at the bottome of the linkedin glutamate post - and this particular blurb stood out for me:
_Age-related muscle weakness has major adverse consequences on quality of life, increasing the risk of falls, fractures, and movement impairments. Albeit an [increased oxidative state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidative_stress) has been shown to contribute to age-dependent reduction in skeletal muscle function, little is known about the mechanisms connecting oxidation and muscle weakness. We show here that genetically enhancing mitochondrial antioxidant activity causes improved skeletal muscle function and voluntary exercise in aged mice.
Here we tested the effects of increased mitochondrial antioxidant activity on age-dependent skeletal muscle dysfunction using [transgenic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transgenesis) mice with targeted [overexpression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene_expression) of the human catalase gene to mitochondria (MCat mice). Aged MCat mice exhibited improved voluntary exercise, increased skeletal muscle specific force and [tetanic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tetanic_stimulation) [Ca2+ transients](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcium_in_biology), decreased [intracellular Ca2+ leak](http://www.ellisonfoundation.org/node/4672) and increased [sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum#Sarcoplasmic_reticulum) Ca2+ load compared with age-matched wild type (WT) littermates.

Overall, these data indicate a direct role for mitochondrial free radicals in promoting the pathological intracellular Ca2+ leak that underlies age-dependent loss of skeletal muscle function. This study harbors implications for the development of novel therapeutic strategies, including mitochondria-targeted antioxidants for treatment of [mitochondrial myopathies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitochondrial_myopathy) and other healthspan-limiting disorders.

And this:
Most catalases exist as tetramers of 60 or 75 kDa, each subunit containing an active site haem group buried deep within the structure, but which is accessible from the surface through hydrophobic channels. The very rigid, stable structure of catalases is resistant to unfolding, which makes them uniquely stable enzymes that are more resistant to pH, thermal denaturation and proteolysis than most other enzymes. Their stability and resistance to proteolysis is an evolutionary advantage, especially since they are produced during the stationary phase of cell growth when levels of proteases are high and there is a rapid rate of protein turnover.
Haem-containing catalases break down hydrogen peroxide by a two-stage mechanism in which hydrogen peroxide alternately oxidises and reduces the haem iron at the active site. In the first step, one hydrogen peroxide molecule oxidises the haem to an oxyferryl species. In the second step, a second hydrogen peroxide molecule is used as a reductant to regenerate the enzyme, producing water and oxygen. Some catalases contain NADPH as a cofactor, which functions to prevent the formation of an inactive compound. Catalases may have another role: the generation of ROS, possibly hydroperoxides, upon UVB irradiation. In this way, UVB light can be detoxified through the generation of hydrogen peroxide, which can then be degraded by the catalase. NADPH may play a role in providing the electrons needed to reduce molecular oxygen in the production of ROS.

Much of the hydrogen peroxide that is produced during oxidative cellular metabolism comes from the breakdown of one of the most damaging ROS, namely the superoxide anion radical (O2-). Superoxide is broken down by superoxide dismutases into hydrogen peroxide and oxygen. Superoxide is so damaging to cells that mutations in the superoxide dismutase enzyme can lead to ALS, w

At the end of the paper he also chats about gingko biloba and some chinese herbs to increase OX/PHOS and decrease ROS...

I have 2 questions:
1) How does one raise mitochondrial catalase?
2) I thought some amount of ROS was required to regenerate the mito - like what happens with nicotine - so that's why too many antioxidants might not be such a hot idea? But then lots of carbs generate lots of ROS - is it a quantity thing?

I also figured out a great way to see if what you're doing is improving the ol' UV light emit-fest when your redox sucketh... if you go outside and bugs love you, you are emitting UV light... think, bug zappers and how they work - years ago I met this guy in Lake Tahoe - a mosquito-fest place - and he said B2 made it so mosquitoes left him alone - your post about the B vitamins being flourescent really made sense - so naturally, I had to try popping all those vitamins yesterday and when I went swimming in the cold ocean, I got home and wasn't starving like the prior 2 days - so it really improved my redox -

thanks_

_selfhacked has a great thread on catalase:
[https://selfhacked.com/2013/06/30/novel-solutions-to-prevent-graying-hair/](https://selfhacked.com/2013/06/30/novel-solutions-to-prevent-graying-hair/)
Quite a rabbit hole I have going here
Cardiovascular Disease, Mitochondria, and Traditional Chinese Medicine
[https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ecam/2015/143145/](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ecam/2015/143145/)_

### michellehp — 2016-10-06T07:30:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202370>

I had to go to one of my old textbooks from psychopharmacology to start to collect these thoughts on glutamate again-
This is copied from Steven Stahl:
If Benjamin Franklin said "nothing in excess, including moderation" he may have anticipated contemporary thinking about excitatory neurotransmission. Excitatory neurotransmission with glutamate ranges from talking to neurons (Fig. 4—15), to screaming at them (Fig. 4—16), to strangling their dendrites, and even to assassinating them (Fig. 4—17).

Glutamate normally opens an ion channel so that the nerve can drink calcium (Figs. 4—15 and 4—18). Sipping calcium is exciting to a neuron and a normal reaction when glutamate is speaking pleasantly. However, when glutamate screams at a neuron, the neuron reacts by drinking more calcium (Figs. 4—16 and 4—19). Imbibing too much calcium may lead in part to excitatory symptoms such as panic, seizures, mania, or psychosis (Figs. 4—19 and 4—20). Too much calcium eventually will anger intracellular enzymes, which then generate nasty chemicals called free radicals. A small commune of free radicals can crash the chemical party in the postsynaptic dendrite and strangle it (Fig. 4 — 21). A mob of free radicals can kill the whole neuron, perhaps by triggering apoptosis (Fig. 4—22; see also Fig. 1 — 18).

Why would the neuron allow this to happen? It is possible that the brain needs this excitotoxic mechanism so that glutamate can act as a gardener in the brain, pruning worn out branches from dendritic trees so that healthy new sprouts may prosper (Fig. 1—23). However, this also equips the neuron with a powerful weapon, which can potentially be misused to cause various neurodegenerative conditions due literally to pruning neurons to death (Fig. 4—22). Such an excitotoxic mechanism could be activated if the genetic program controlling it is turned on or potentially by ingestion of toxins or toxic drugs of abuse. That is, when glutamate decides to act as an abusive bully for whatever reason, neurons may seize, panic, become manic, or become psychotic (Fig. 4—20). Furthermore, such symptoms of calcium intoxi
cation may be followed by an unfortunate glutamate hangover in the form of destroyed dendrites, which can never be excited again (Fig. 4—21).

Other illnesses such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, amytrophic lateral sclerosis (Lou Gehrig's disease), and even schizophrenia may hire glutamate as a
methodical undercover assassin, eliminating a whole subpopulation of predesignated neurons over a prolonged period of time. Such a systematic process would be consistent with the pace of these slow neurodegenerative disorders. In catastrophic brain diseases such as stroke and global ischemia associated with cardiac arrest, drowning, etc., a whole army of glutamate "hit men" may be hired as mass murderers. In this case, glutamate causes the massacre of an entire region of brain neurons by suddenly subjecting them to molecular mayhem.

Thus, glutamate's actions can range across a vast spectrum. It can be a friendly neuronal conversationalist or a screaming hypothetical mediator of neurological and psychiatric disorders. How might the symptoms and clinical course of various psychiatric disorders fit this model of excitotoxicity? Psychosis possibly shares some analogies with a seizure, in that excessive transmission of dopamine in the mesolim- bic areas of brain may lead to symptoms of delusions, hallucinations, and thought disorder in various psychiatric disorders. Panic disorder may be analogous to a seizure in areas of the brain controlling emotions (such as the parahippocampal gyrus), leading to clinical symptoms characterized by a massive emotional discharge of panic, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, feelings of impending death, or fear of losing control. Thus, disorders such as psychosis, epilepsy and panic disorder appear to involve excessive neurotransmission, which may help explain the mechanism by which they produce acute symptoms (Figs. 4—19 and 4—20).

Furthermore, these disorders seem to become more resistant to treatment the longer the disorder persists and the more poorly the symptoms are controlled, as if there were an underlying mechanism of destruction accompanying symptoms that are out of control (Figs. 4—21 through 4—23). Thus, excessive neurotransmission may itself be a cause of deficient neurotransmission. If seizures beget seizures, panic begets panic, psychosis begets psychosis, and mania begets mania, these symptoms are obviously not good for the brain. The psychopharmacologist must therefore act to prevent symptoms, not only because symptom control may harness the disruptive influences of excessive neurotransmission on behavior, but also because symptom control may ultimately prevent the demise of the neurons mediating these very behaviors (Figs. 4—20 to 4—23). If these disorders of excessive neurotransmission are analogous to the brain "burning" during symptomatic crises such as a seizure, psychosis, panic attack, or mania, treatments might not only "put out the fire" but also salvage the underlying neuronal substrates, which are burning as the fuel for the fire.

Discovery of antagonists to excitotoxicity, such as exemplified by the glutamate antagonists, may portend the possibility of developing new drug therapies for neu- rodegenerative disorders. At least two approaches to controlling glutamate are showing promise. The first is to protect the neuron from drinking too much calcium by blocking glutamate receptors directly with antagonists. Thus, neurons are only allowed to quench their thirst in normal excitatory neurotransmission but not to guzzle so much calcium that they become excitotoxically inebriated. If such compounds worked, they would be neuroprotective, since they would arrest glutamate before it could assassinate any more neurons. Another approach to developing treatments for illnesses that may be mediated by excitotoxicity is to rescue the cellular machinery once glutamate's cascade of doom has been activated. Thus, free-radical scavengers are being developed that neutralize the troublesome free radicals. Certain chemicals can do this, including vitamin E and experimental agents called lazaroids (so named because they purport to raise neurons from the dead, as the biblical Lazarus was raised.)

### Scompy — 2016-10-06T18:56:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202400>

> [michellehp said:](/goto/post?id=202367)
>
> How's the mood been with your sunny skies?
> Just got back from Tortola, BVI- I look like Magda from "Something about Mary" I am very very very tan and my light hair now looks white. The main difference in this trip (there are many) is that I am still relaxed even after being home for 48 hrs. now and so productive.) Even stress (a phone call from school) didn't get me into a snippy mood.) I guess I really tapped into the right environment over there.

More than great here! I've already noticed a trend... in Seattle when it's a gray day, it's gray all day and 2 days before it and 2 days after. Seattle sits sort of in a valley between the Olympics and the Cascade mountains, so there can be month-long stretches of gray skies. Here in Bend, I'm on the opposite dry-side of the Cascades. Even if there happens to be a gray-day, it clears out pretty fast within hours. The 10,000 ft. mountains to the west help removed the clouds and dump snow up there. Where I'm living is like an endless forest of pine-trees, so massive O2 production. But better yet, it's all old volcanic lands, calderas everywhere, etc. Because it's in the 3,000 to 4,000 foot range where I live and work, I'm getting UVB all times of the day. I'll be taking measurements in Nov., Dec. and Jan., because I'm thinking mid-winter, it's still going to have at least UVB5 here and if it's less, I can take a 15 min trip west up to a lodge at 7,000 feet for UVB whenever I need it. There is little cell towers at this time, fewer radio than most cities, etc. And of course instead of being at the 49th parallel, it's at the 44th here. Lots of natural wonders around every turn, so endless weekend getaway potentials getting into nature.

### Scompy — 2016-10-06T19:05:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202404>

> [michellehp said:](/goto/post?id=202370)
>
> I had to go to one of my old textbooks from psychopharmacology to start to collect these thoughts on glutamate again-
> This is copied from Steven Stahl:
> If Benjamin Franklin said "nothing in excess, including moderation" he may have anticipated contemporary thinking about excitatory neurotransmission. Excitatory neurotransmission with glutamate ranges from talking to neurons (Fig. 4—15), to screaming at them (Fig. 4—16), to strangling their dendrites, and even to assassinating them (Fig. 4—17).
>
> Glutamate normally opens an ion channel so that the nerve can drink calcium (Figs. 4—15 and 4—18). Sipping calcium is exciting to a neuron and a normal reaction when glutamate is speaking pleasantly. However, when glutamate screams at a neuron, the neuron reacts by drinking more calcium (Figs. 4—16 and 4—19). Imbibing too much calcium may lead in part to excitatory symptoms such as panic, seizures, mania, or psychosis (Figs. 4—19 and 4—20). Too much calcium eventually will anger intracellular enzymes, which then generate nasty chemicals called free radicals. A small commune of free radicals can crash the chemical party in the postsynaptic dendrite and strangle it (Fig. 4 — 21). A mob of free radicals can kill the whole neuron, perhaps by triggering apoptosis (Fig. 4—22; see also Fig. 1 — 18).
>
> Why would the neuron allow this to happen? It is possible that the brain needs this excitotoxic mechanism so that glutamate can act as a gardener in the brain, pruning worn out branches from dendritic trees so that healthy new sprouts may prosper (Fig. 1—23). However, this also equips the neuron with a powerful weapon, which can potentially be misused to cause various neurodegenerative conditions due literally to pruning neurons to death (Fig. 4—22). Such an excitotoxic mechanism could be activated if the genetic program controlling it is turned on or potentially by ingestion of toxins or toxic drugs of abuse. That is, when glutamate decides to act as an abusive bully for whatever reason, neurons may seize, panic, become manic, or become psychotic (Fig. 4—20). Furthermore, such symptoms of calcium intoxi
> cation may be followed by an unfortunate glutamate hangover in the form of destroyed dendrites, which can never be excited again (Fig. 4—21).
>
> Other illnesses such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, amytrophic lateral sclerosis (Lou Gehrig's disease), and even schizophrenia may hire glutamate as a
> methodical undercover assassin, eliminating a whole subpopulation of predesignated neurons over a prolonged period of time. Such a systematic process would be consistent with the pace of these slow neurodegenerative disorders. In catastrophic brain diseases such as stroke and global ischemia associated with cardiac arrest, drowning, etc., a whole army of glutamate "hit men" may be hired as mass murderers. In this case, glutamate causes the massacre of an entire region of brain neurons by suddenly subjecting them to molecular mayhem.
>
> Thus, glutamate's actions can range across a vast spectrum. It can be a friendly neuronal conversationalist or a screaming hypothetical mediator of neurological and psychiatric disorders. How might the symptoms and clinical course of various psychiatric disorders fit this model of excitotoxicity? Psychosis possibly shares some analogies with a seizure, in that excessive transmission of dopamine in the mesolim- bic areas of brain may lead to symptoms of delusions, hallucinations, and thought disorder in various psychiatric disorders. Panic disorder may be analogous to a seizure in areas of the brain controlling emotions (such as the parahippocampal gyrus), leading to clinical symptoms characterized by a massive emotional discharge of panic, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, feelings of impending death, or fear of losing control. Thus, disorders such as psychosis, epilepsy and panic disorder appear to involve excessive neurotransmission, which may help explain the mechanism by which they produce acute symptoms (Figs. 4—19 and 4—20).
>
> Furthermore, these disorders seem to become more resistant to treatment the longer the disorder persists and the more poorly the symptoms are controlled, as if there were an underlying mechanism of destruction accompanying symptoms that are out of control (Figs. 4—21 through 4—23). Thus, excessive neurotransmission may itself be a cause of deficient neurotransmission. If seizures beget seizures, panic begets panic, psychosis begets psychosis, and mania begets mania, these symptoms are obviously not good for the brain. The psychopharmacologist must therefore act to prevent symptoms, not only because symptom control may harness the disruptive influences of excessive neurotransmission on behavior, but also because symptom control may ultimately prevent the demise of the neurons mediating these very behaviors (Figs. 4—20 to 4—23). If these disorders of excessive neurotransmission are analogous to the brain "burning" during symptomatic crises such as a seizure, psychosis, panic attack, or mania, treatments might not only "put out the fire" but also salvage the underlying neuronal substrates, which are burning as the fuel for the fire.
>
> Discovery of antagonists to excitotoxicity, such as exemplified by the glutamate antagonists, may portend the possibility of developing new drug therapies for neu- rodegenerative disorders. At least two approaches to controlling glutamate are showing promise. The first is to protect the neuron from drinking too much calcium by blocking glutamate receptors directly with antagonists. Thus, neurons are only allowed to quench their thirst in normal excitatory neurotransmission but not to guzzle so much calcium that they become excitotoxically inebriated. If such compounds worked, they would be neuroprotective, since they would arrest glutamate before it could assassinate any more neurons. Another approach to developing treatments for illnesses that may be mediated by excitotoxicity is to rescue the cellular machinery once glutamate's cascade of doom has been activated. Thus, free-radical scavengers are being developed that neutralize the troublesome free radicals. Certain chemicals can do this, including vitamin E and experimental agents called lazaroids (so named because they purport to raise neurons from the dead, as the biblical Lazarus was raised.)

Thanks for posting this! Exactly why Mg is my Ca controller. I use a spray about 2-3x a day and often have a tiny bit of dark chocolate after a meal (for the Mg mostly), and also why hydration is so important too. The IF (Intermittent Fasting) helps to baseline the calcium faster as a side-benefit I don't think most people have thought about. The only side-issue I've run into in the past has been lacking too much K+ (Potassium) 15+ hours into a fast, so I literally have my diet down to a science now... eggs or meats for breakfast. Then seafood and avocado for a late lunch. Seafood+avocado keep K+ higher going into the fast. This all helps keep glutamate excitotoxicity to a minimum, assuming I can stay away from nnEMF.

### Scompy — 2016-10-09T06:19:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202535>

**October 8, 2016 (Scompy's Run)**
After overhearing an AARP ad on tv at a restaurant, it invoked a new hypothesis about longevity of life and the society we live.
Call it far fetched, but the AARP ad made me realize that society has setup a window of death-expectancy between the age of around 65 to 85. Back in the 1970s, it 'felt' like people were living well into their 80s. I think what has been going on is that modern medicine has pushed up the minimum overall average, but the very-long lived is being reduced. I don't know if this is actually true, but speculation based on observation and being in my mid 40s. In other words, it appears that in the past, there was a larger age longevity range where people would die roughly between a larger age range like between 50 and 100; now it feels that range is pinched into the 65 to 85 range, so the medical community can 'claim' that the average life-expectancy is increasing. Additionally, quality of life is never pushed into these stats, and as we all know, if we're mostly mentally or physically crippled by the age of 65, it's not like the 95 year old that was agile, strong and could arm wrestle his grandson and win.

Here is where I'm gonna take this: **Logan's Run**
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logan's\_Run\_(film)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logan%27s_Run_\(film\))

If you are not familiar with this movie, there is a major thing going on in this early Sci-Fi movie that I believe is now right in front of us all!

This: **Plot
In the year 2274, the remnants of human civilization live in a sealed city contained beneath a cluster of [geodesic domes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesic_dome), a utopia run by a computer that takes care of all aspects of their life, including reproduction. The citizens live a [hedonistic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hedonistic) life but, to maintain the city, everyone must undergo the ritual of "Carrousel" \[_[sic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sic)_\] when they reach the age of 30.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logan%27s_Run_\(film\)#cite_note-10) There, they are vaporized and ostensibly "renewed". To track this, each person is implanted at birth with a "life-clock" crystal in the palm of their hand that changes color as they get older and begins blinking as they approach their "Last Day". Most residents accept this promise of rebirth, but those who do not and attempt to flee the city are known as "Runners". An elite team of policemen known as "Sandmen", outfitted in predominantly black uniforms and serving in an agency of the city called "Deep Sleep", are assigned to pursue and [terminate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Death) Runners as they try to escape.**

In particular, I just connected the AARP ad with a ping to the Logan's Run movie in a brief passing, but it has stuck with me all afternoon and evening. I immediately realized that at least in Logan's Run, most people were WILLING to kill themselves at the age of 30 because they never questioned their environment. As I heard the AARP ad, it occurred to me that life is greatly trivialized past the age of 65, but in subtle ways to promote an expectation that the Grim Reaper is closing in for them. It is almost as if 'some controlling force' wants to cap Life Expectancy around the age of 80 or thereabouts to churn humanity. After all, the longer a person lives and acquires wisdom and knowledge, the more a person may be apt to question things and the system itself. If people become so ignorant of the longevity of past lives, they'll come to expect a worse life come the age of 60 or so... they'll see those around them fail for reinforcement, and may forget (or this may become myth/fable) that there were very strong and old people that lived well into their 100s with a high quality of life like what anyone can read in the book of Genesis with Abraham as an easy example.

Then I shift and think of the natural Light Physics, and those that leash and bend unnatural Light Physics to their will and control of our environments. It is deduction, my dear Watson. When so many paths keep pointing to certain outcomes, one must question the volume of the physics implemented upon us by chance, and those implemented to control. For Logan's Run, a 'Runner' could choose to flee and know the risk. But I must ask, why even provide this type of choice to the masses when a great deal of the physics is invisible to the naked eye? I believe our current period in our world history will have a title in a century or two from now as we view various eras.

### caroline — 2016-10-09T07:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202543>

We listen to a radio show every Sunday morning - people call in from all over Australia and Aussies from all over the world as well.

This morning one caller - a woman who is 103 and she plays scrabble every day. She definitely has all her faculties! The radio presenter had made a visit to her and didn't find her at home. He asked how she manages with the crazy long driveway she has. She said it is all about exercise and being outside!

Smart lady - in spite of never having heard of Jack kruse!

Another lady who called in this morning - she is 93 and so bright and well spoken ...and so involved in life.

### Scompy — 2016-10-10T18:52:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202594>

**Oct. 10, 2016 (Riddle me this)**

Quoting from JK on social media: "Tinnitus is the brain trying to heal itself but it cannot. Tinnitus arises when this flexibility goes bad inside of the mitochondria. It's mitochondria in Ammon's horn looking for the right frequency so mitochondria can increase tunneling speeds and become better able to use beta oxidation for fats and the TCA cycle for glucose. The key to understanding neuronal function is understanding how a reflex organizes the entire nervous system. This goes back to the work of Sherrington in the 19th century. Bereft of incoming signals, the neurons lose the feedback control and they begin to do things they usually would not. This is what gives the phantom sounds of tinnitus. Neurons tuned to the tinnitus frequency in the auditory cortex became less active, thus this alters the reflex arc between the cochlea and the auditory cortex and brainstem. In all of these places, their is wide spread energy deficits in mitochondria that reduce the activity of neurons. Calcium efflux is usually behind this secondary effect of lower energy out put of tissues. It turns out out of the 5 senses hearing using the most energy because of its ability to distinguish waveforms is so sensitive. For example all humans can tell the difference between a sound that is 30 Hz or 60 Hz. The same is not true about our eyes and vision. Few people can sense the difference between a pulsing light at 30 cycles a second versus 60 cycles a second."

Interesting influence, but I am so in-tune with my internal tinnitus when it kicks in and goes away that I know intensity and length of EMF exposures. For me, the trigger is an nnEMF. For example, yesterday at my temp-housing, the circuit breaker panel is along one of the walls. It outputs about 50 milliGauss when about a foot from it. So if I stand in the field for about 15 seconds, about a minute later, the tinnitus kicks in and lasts for about 2-3 minutes. Then on a second exposure, if I increase the time to just about a minute instead of 15 seconds, the tinnitus kicks in often at the end of that minute and could last for about 5 to 6 minutes. These are magnetic fields in general, not even RF-EMF (though RF in the 2+ mW/Sq meter range does about the same).

In stronger fields or if there is more time-duration exposure, I run the risk of popping a small capillary on the back of my retina. Yesterday I went to my wife to 'said location' with over 150 people in a large room for about an hour and I got a eye rupture spot along with tinnitus. I wish I had my meter on me to know what was going on, but it was most likely the RF combo. When I noticed what was going on, I got outside in the the sunlight and also tilted my head backwards for about 5 mins to get the capillary to heal and not leak into the gel part of the eye and cause a blood spot. This has been a very common thing in my late 20s, so I've had a few decades of not only being hyper-aware, but also developing this super-power.

Here is the largest riddle of all... I get more tinnitus if I eat about a half a bar of 85-95% dark chocolate and then I expose myself to a magnetic field. Seriously, if I don't have any dark chocolate at all, my powers fade down a bit. If I eat more dark chocolate in a day, the tinnitus comes on stronger. I'm first thinking it's the caffeine and the extra blood-pressure causing more ear-ringing and capillary bursting potential. So this leads me to believe the dark chocolate may be problematic if consumed in a short period of time (and also if a person is less hydrated). I typically eat about a half a bar of dark chocolate daily, but now I am only eating a few wedges for this reason.

### Penny — 2016-10-11T22:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202657>

**The Neuroprotective Effect of Dark Chocolate in Monosodium Glutamate-Induced Nontransgenic Alzheimer Disease Model Rats: Biochemical, Behavioral, and Histological Studies.**
[Madhavadas S](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Madhavadas%20S%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26673833)1, [Kapgal VK](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Kapgal%20VK%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26673833)2, [Kutty BM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Kutty%20BM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26673833)2, [Subramanian S](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Subramanian%20S%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26673833)1.
**[Author information](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/26673833#)**
**Abstract**
The vulnerability to oxidative stress and cognitive decline continue to increase during both normal and pathological aging. Dietary changes and sedentary life style resulting in mid-life obesity and type 2 diabetes, if left uncorrected, further add to the risk of cognitive decline and Alzheimer disease (AD) in the later stages of life. Certain antioxidant agents such as dietary polyphenols, taken in adequate quantities, have been suggested to improve the cognitive processes. In this study, we examined the effect of oral administration of dark chocolate (DC) containing 70% cocoa solids and 4% total polyphenol content for three months at a dose of 500 mg/Kg body weight per day to 17-month-old monosodium glutamate treated obese Sprague-Dawley rats, earlier characterized as a nontransgenic AD (NTAD) rat model after reversal of obesity, diabetes, and consequent cognitive impairments. The results demonstrated that DC reduced the hyperglycemia, inhibited the cholinesterase activity in the hippocampal tissue homogenates, and improved the cognitive performance in spatial memory related Barnes maze task. Histological studies revealed an increase in cell volume in the DC treated rats in the CA3 region of the hippocampus. These findings demonstrated the benefits of DC in enhancing cognitive function and cholinergic activity in the hippocampus of the aged NTAD rats while correcting their metabolic disturbances.

### Penny — 2016-10-11T22:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202658>

**Targeting inhibitory neurotransmission in tinnitus.**
[Richardson BD](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Richardson%20BD%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692)1, [Brozoski TJ](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Brozoski%20TJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692), [Ling LL](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Ling%20LL%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692), [Caspary DM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Caspary%20DM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692).
**[Author information](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22405692#)**
**Abstract**
Tinnitus perception depends on the presence of its neural correlates within the auditory neuraxis and associated structures. Targeting specific circuits and receptors within the central nervous system in an effort to relieve the perception of tinnitus and its impact on one's emotional and mental state has become a focus of tinnitus research. One approach is to upregulate endogenous inhibitory neurotransmitter levels (e.g., glycine and GABA) and selectively target inhibitory receptors in key circuits to normalize tinnitus pathophysiology. Thus, the basic functional and molecular properties of two major ligand-gated inhibitory receptor systems, the GABA(A) receptor (GABA(A)R) and glycine receptor (GlyR) are described. Also reviewed is the rationale for targeting inhibition, which stems from reported tinnitus-related homeostatic plasticity of inhibitory neurotransmitter systems and associated enhanced neuronal excitability throughout most central auditory structures. However, the putative role of the medial geniculate body (MGB) in tinnitus has not been previously addressed, specifically in terms of its inhibitory afferents from inferior colliculus and thalamic reticular nucleus and its GABA(A)R functional heterogeneity. This heterogeneous population of GABA(A)Rs, which may be altered in tinnitus pathology, and its key anatomical position in the auditory CNS make the MGB a compelling structure for tinnitus research. Finally, some selective compounds, which enhance tonic inhibition, have successfully ameliorated tinnitus in animal studies, suggesting that the MGB and, to a lesser degree, the auditory cortex may be their primary locus of action. These pharmacological interventions are examined in terms of their mechanism of action and why these agents may be effective in tinnitus treatment. This article is part of a Special Issue entitled: Tinnitus Neuroscience.

I've been putting glycine in my morning coffee shake to mop up excess methyl groups that will turn into glutamates - maybe that would help you? Totally reductionist thinking - again - it seems like it would turn into TMG and that would be a good thing also - if I eat too much dark chocolate, I get heart palps and have to take magnesium - more reductionism, but really, I'm a train wreck at the moment - so if you're doing the chocolate for the mag - I would skip it - that being said, I also put more than a tsp of Trader Joes cocoa powder in my coffee and get no reaction - but I also throw a lot of turmeric in there - so I have come to the conclusion for me at least, that the chocolate isn't the problem, it's the sugar that's in these bars that is doing us in...

Last year, I was beach walking quite regularly and my tinnitus disappeared - however it is now back -

### Scompy — 2016-10-12T00:13:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-202661>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=202658)
>
> **Targeting inhibitory neurotransmission in tinnitus.**
> [Richardson BD](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Richardson%20BD%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692)1, [Brozoski TJ](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Brozoski%20TJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692), [Ling LL](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Ling%20LL%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692), [Caspary DM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Caspary%20DM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22405692).
> **[Author information](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22405692#)**
> **Abstract**
> Tinnitus perception depends on the presence of its neural correlates within the auditory neuraxis and associated structures. Targeting specific circuits and receptors within the central nervous system in an effort to relieve the perception of tinnitus and its impact on one's emotional and mental state has become a focus of tinnitus research. One approach is to upregulate endogenous inhibitory neurotransmitter levels (e.g., glycine and GABA) and selectively target inhibitory receptors in key circuits to normalize tinnitus pathophysiology. Thus, the basic functional and molecular properties of two major ligand-gated inhibitory receptor systems, the GABA(A) receptor (GABA(A)R) and glycine receptor (GlyR) are described. Also reviewed is the rationale for targeting inhibition, which stems from reported tinnitus-related homeostatic plasticity of inhibitory neurotransmitter systems and associated enhanced neuronal excitability throughout most central auditory structures. However, the putative role of the medial geniculate body (MGB) in tinnitus has not been previously addressed, specifically in terms of its inhibitory afferents from inferior colliculus and thalamic reticular nucleus and its GABA(A)R functional heterogeneity. This heterogeneous population of GABA(A)Rs, which may be altered in tinnitus pathology, and its key anatomical position in the auditory CNS make the MGB a compelling structure for tinnitus research. Finally, some selective compounds, which enhance tonic inhibition, have successfully ameliorated tinnitus in animal studies, suggesting that the MGB and, to a lesser degree, the auditory cortex may be their primary locus of action. These pharmacological interventions are examined in terms of their mechanism of action and why these agents may be effective in tinnitus treatment. This article is part of a Special Issue entitled: Tinnitus Neuroscience.
>
> I've been putting glycine in my morning coffee shake to mop up excess methyl groups that will turn into glutamates - maybe that would help you? Totally reductionist thinking - again - it seems like it would turn into TMG and that would be a good thing also - if I eat too much dark chocolate, I get heart palps and have to take magnesium - more reductionism, but really, I'm a train wreck at the moment - so if you're doing the chocolate for the mag - I would skip it - that being said, I also put more than a tsp of Trader Joes cocoa powder in my coffee and get no reaction - but I also throw a lot of turmeric in there - so I have come to the conclusion for me at least, that the chocolate isn't the problem, it's the sugar that's in these bars that is doing us in...
>
> Last year, I was beach walking quite regularly and my tinnitus disappeared - however it is now back -

I can tell you I'm a chocolate expert. Even though dark chocolate has lots of Mg, I think it's really the diuretic influence that causes dehydration and then leeching of K+ out of the body, which leads to the heart palps. I'm the exact same way as you described if I take too much at once or per day. I have to eat all of my chocolate before about 2pm if I'm gonna have some that day. And true with the sugar. I only go for about 88% these days. Another link is the way some companies craft their chocolate. Some dark chocolate is no good at all even if it's 85%+....causes more hits to my adrenals (lower back pain) as my main gauge.

The glycine use to glutamate mop is an interesting idea. I've only used taurine to back-block NMDA instead, rather than just pulling down blood glutamate levels. Wow, I'll have to try a biohack with it to see how it turns out. Some great thinking there!

**One reason why I love Nightwish so, is their connection to NATURE...**this is jammed packed!

Leave the sleep and let the springtime talk
In tongues from the time before man
Listen to a daffodil tell her tale
Let the guest in, walk out, be the first to greet the morn
The meadows of heaven await harvest
The cliffs unjumped, cold waters untouched
The elsewhere creatures yet unseen
Finally your number came up, freefall awaits the brave

Come, taste the wine
Raise the blind
They will guide you from the light
Writing noughts till the end of time
Come, surf the clouds
Race the dark
It feeds from the runs undone
Meet me where the cliff greets the sea

The answer to the riddle before your eyes
Is in dead leaves and fleeting skies
Returning swans and sedulous mice
Writing's on the gardens book, in the minute of a lover's look
Building a sandcastle close to the shore
A house of cards from a worn-out deck
A home from the fellowship, poise and calm
Write a lyric for the song only you can understand

Come, taste the wine
Raise the blind
They will guide you from the light
Writing noughts till the end of time
Come, surf the clouds
Race the dark
It feeds from the runs undone
Meet me where the cliff greets the sea

Riding hard every shooting star
Come to life, open mind, have a laugh at the orthodox
Come, drink deep let the dam of mind seep
Travel with great élan, dance a jig at the funeral, come!

Taste the wine
Raise the blind
They will guide you from the light
Writing noughts till the end of time
Ooh, come, surf the clouds
Race the dark
It feeds from the runs undone
Meet me where the cliff greets the sea, come!

### Scompy — 2016-11-22T09:01:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scompys-journal.16641/post-204200>

**Nov. 21, 2016. (Home Update)**
So after much planning and a long time in the making, I moved into my new home a few weeks ago, ala quantum goodness. Absolutely in awe of the happiness I have now. The transition-housing was worth it. We're still unpacking, but I already have a fully wired internet (internal WiFi turned off in the modem) after giving a lecture to the cable guy and breaking out my meters in front of him to verify the work.

There is also a huge story to tell here. My cable guy was ultra-high-strung when I first met him; he probably was a good 12 years younger than me and I could tell he was very smart, but also very distracted and without focus: almost like a form of ADHD or ADD. Once he got my internet working, my meters still picked up a LOT of nnEMF coming from somewhere. He had brought in his laptop to test my connection and figured that was the source, but it wasn't. I mentioned that I "want my WiFi shutdown in the box specifically due to the 'electromagnetics' that could penetrate my 7-year old's brain much more easily at his age." I left it at that. I could tell the guy had considered it in the past and he didn't dismiss it either, as I saw he also wore a bluetooth earpiece.

The cable guy said that the router was shut down and I trusted him, so I brought over my cornet and scanned some more. Because the cable guy was pretty tech-saavy and probably didn't think anything was wrong with RF EMFs, I'm gonna assume he thought I was off-my-rocker and overly-concerned about it. His cell phone was about 6 feet from the router and I it was then when couldn't believe what I was registering from his phone. 300+ milliWatts per Square meter within an inch, and I wasn't touching the phone. I didn't tell him and stayed very pleasant, but let him know it was his phone I was picking up and not my router. He saw my meter go red off the charts and I could see concern on his face, now being face-to-face with a cornet that he'd probably never seen before in his life. In retrospect, I think I should have turned on the sound-mode... that would have been more of a shocker to him. He was a bit flustered, but then started hooking up my TV cable in the other room. Once he was done, I could tell I challenged him without saying much.

The next bit is the funny part. I think he felt strange from the previous conversation and me mentioning about my son. Plus, I knew so much about his WiFi systems, and I could sense he felt like he wanted to take off from my place quickly to not continue any type of conversation with him again. So he told me he was done and had me sign some stuff, and then scrambled away...maybe it was me or maybe it was another customer he had to get to, but within 30 seconds, I had realized he left his laptop back at my router. So I had to sprint to get to him and just caught him from driving off without his stuff. In my head at that moment, I was thinking, "It's probably the RF EMF that has this guy in an ADD-like state." After telling him no worries, I got what I wanted... no RF and a great internet connection.

Now let's talk about my new place a bit...
The whole home itself sits on the ground-floor atop volcanic lava beds. The inside in the main living room has two grounding-walls made of lava stones; after the first night, I woke up before the sun and put my feet on the wall eating my breakfast. Another big feature are the skylights in every room and the huge open windows. Where I am located, I get about 200+ days of sun per year!, in comparison to Seattle: the worst place in the Continental USA for sun. I've already measured UVB many times in the 4 to 7 range mid-day right now early November because I'm at 3500 ft. So the access to UVB is outstanding. This is also 'older glass' from the 1960s, that has slowly thinned over time. Old, thin glass = more quantum yum.

The home is located in a remote location away from RF (0.0005 mW/Sq meter) after many measurements. I only got a few higher hits from a few random cars passing by. Another upside is that I have a natural gas heater that is located OUTSIDE of the home and when it runs, I get no EMF issues. A current downside with the home is that there are many dimmers. I'm having an electrician remove them soon. The home's incoming power is also ultra-optimal because the line hits the circuit breaker at the edge of the kitchen wall, but in a location where no one will be. The main bedrooms ramble off the side of the home, so when I shut down the breaker at night, the power is being cut entirely if I choose to circuit-break only the bedrooms vs. the whole home.

The home is one block from the main cascade mountain run-off river (called the Deschutes River), so I can hear it from my front door. The only problem I've run into this morning is about 10 deer in the yard wanting food because the previous home-owner fed them. In the back, the previous home-owners left a huge CT tub. I think they left it for the deer to drink out of, but I don't mind bathing in deer spit. Boonies for the win.

### Liam Taylor — 2016-11-22T12:48:45+0200

V interesting Scompy. Well done for staying strong in the face of pessimism. I found what Jack wrote in Reality 1 to be very poignant:

_"When we first unleash them on the world they can be minimized by a “yawn”; they can also be slowly bleed to death by a sharp comment, or killed by a facial frown. But when an idea is really special, it is a rude awakening to the new dawn’s sleepy eyes. A new idea is like a beautiful sunrise; it begins whispering to the world that something new is happening on the world’s stage for the very first time."_

Did you disable the wifi via internal software or physically?

Your new place sounds amazing, v v interesting.
