# Thread: NatureCoastNotSoNewbie:)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naturecoastnotsonewbie.16719/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### becca — 2015-08-13T20:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naturecoastnotsonewbie.16719/post-174619>

**Hello, I am Becca.
I joined the forum several months ago after reading Epi Paleo Rx. I discovered Jack Kruse.com through a forum post on Suspicious0bservers.org, which is a fantastic website about space weather and the connectivity between other Heavenly bodies, space plasma and our Earth. This website supports the Electric Universe theory.
I have been hiding behind the curtains in the forums, occasionally peeking out and sharing some of Jack's FB posts, and decided that today I would begin my journal. I have been wearing amber glasses to watch tv, work on my computer and have an amber screen cover for my smartphone since I read Jack’s book. Although I have not tested for EMFs, I disconnect our bedroom from EMF by turning off breaker every night, and the Best thing about living in Hernando county Florida (I live in Spring Hill Fl, which is just south of Homosassa, Florida, where Lambipoo lives) is they do not add Flouride to the water here! YAY!! Currently I weigh 217 pounds, 5’ 7 1.2”, thick, plushy abdomen, very muscular arms and legs, though interestingly, I had a rotator cuff injury in February as I was filling up my car with gas, (tripped over the gas hose and to protect my balance, hyperabducted and externally rotated shoulder suddenly)which has lead to increased weight gain (from 190) due to me using that as an excuse not to swim or walk for exercise and to be more sedentary..a plushy pity party that has ended.. And btw, through meditation and Qigong and TaiChi(quietening the SNS and enhancing /supporting the PNS) I have full AROM in my left shoulder and no pain, no crepitus-never went to a doctor or other therapist about this injury-and I could not abduct my left arm, internally rotate or extend it after the injury.
My dear husband (my second marriage), works for IBM as a software alchemist (really that is what is on his business card-he is a senior executive there, but prefers the alchemist title.) He is fairly sedentary as when he is not programming or meeting and educating toerhs he is a HUGE gamer (currently Skyrim, Minecraft), though he uses our treadmuill about twice a week and does swim occasionally-we have a lovely salt water pool in our backyard. He is about 6’1” and weighs almost 285. After our first year anniversary we went to Paleo diet and he lost down to 250, (and I went down to 182) but due to world wide traveling for work and his mom passing away recently, he gained most of that back, and I gained to 217). He is curious about the EpiPaleo Diet, but sticking to low carb only for now. I am hopeful that he will see my results and through my gentle reflection, decide to join me, though I am not attached to him joining me-the very Best aspect of our relationship is we enjoy each others company and respects each other’s decisions about what we feel is best for each of us. We do not fuss at each other when we get off track or don’t exercise, just encourage each other.
Early Life
I was born in mid October in Shreveport, Louisiana, middle child of a physician (Ob-Gyn) and a microbiologist. The first three years of my life we lived in Shreveport La, where my grandfather was my primary caregiver, as my mom and dad worked long hours. When I was almost 4 years old, we moved to West Monroe Louisiana, and I lived there until I got married at age 20.
I had a great childhood, spent many hours alone ion the woods and in nature and explored, running ,balancing, jumping over small streams, (now they call this Parkour:/ ) We had good food fixed by my mom: lots of salads, steak, broccoli and squash, fish on Fridays, usually Salmon Crocquettes (sp?), though I also remember the beginnings of processed foods (Captain Crunch was a favorite, Space Food Sticks, Tang, and Carnation Instant Breakfast, and I loved PeterPan peanut butter, which has a ton of sugar…)
The first time I remember feeling fat, was when each of us were weighed as part of a health class when I was in fourth grade…its unfortunate that I listened to my classmates, as when I view pictures of myself at that time, I had straight middle trunk, but other wise I looked fine. I was also taller than most in my class so I am certain that had something to do with it. I auditioned for the school play which was called “Blue Willow” and got the lead part, but declined to be in it when one of the lines about my character was “she’s as skinney as a \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_.”, (I don’t remember the metaphor, just the commentary when that line was read.)
**I had terrible cavities and most of my molars were full of amalgam fillings..they degraded and by my 42nd birthday I had all lower molars removed with dental implants (titanium) in their place.
All that to say, from a young age I felt self conscious about my body. One thing I was very self confident about was my strength, (folks would always tell me “You are so strong like your dad” and in fact of the three kids I looked the most like him, except I had lovely tiny ears, not dumbo ears like my dadJ) I had really good upper body strength due to always helping my dad with many projects around the house (we built a treehouse, a greenhouse, and he had a tennis court made in the very back of our property and we spent many hours playing tennis…the days of John Newcomb, E.Goolagong, Chris Evert, etc..
I also remember when I was in first grade when I squatted down to look at books on the lower shelf at the library, my right knee creaked…still does but no pain..
Fast forward to ninth grade and I tried out for a “drill team” (now they call it dance team for the football half time entertainment) and got in, grew a few inches and between puberty and dancing got in good shape. However I was a bit obsessed with my new found shape and here was my daily intake:
Breakfast: Sugarfree Dr.Pepper and about three times a week a bag of M&Ms. I never ate lunch at school, I hated the cafeteria and just spent time outdoors with friends. After I got home from school I had a cup of tomato soup and one half of a ham and cheese or just cheese sandwich. At suppertime,meals were prepared by my mom and we would all sit at the table together (my older sister, me and my younger brother), invariably my dad would eat supper in about 5-10 minutes, as usually he would be called away by a distraught mom to be, or headed to the hospital to deliver a baby. I adopted my dad’s pace of eating habit.
When I graduated the ninth grade and the following year went into high school, it was quite a change for me, went from being athletic and strong to strong with a cocoon of insulation…our high school was bulging at the seams -2000 plus in three grades packed in a school built for 800..our junior high was very small and it was difficult to know how to fit, where to fit..so I plunged myself into art classes and less exertive activities, still played tennis though.,,eating habits fluctuated wildly..ingested about one liter of sugar free Dr. Pepper a day…yikes.
Due to my dad being an Ob-Gyn in a small town, he took vacations in the winter and we would go to New Mexico and Taos to snow ski-in preparation he insisted that my sister and I run nightly so that our lungs would be in good shape for the cold weather and high altitude. Looking back, I loved those times, remember the feeling of cold crisp nights and the feeling of light numbness on my legs at the end of our runs (we usually ran in our tennis dresses) So I suppose I was doing CT way back then…****

****Young Adult to Middle Adult
I was horridly shy as a young adult and met my future hubbie in my last year in high school. He was my complement and “filled in” where I was lacking…when I was 20 and he was 19 we married. We moved several times during our marriage-Fort Collins, Co., Fayetteville, Ark, Houston Tx, and Denton Tx. When I was 20 years old My hubbie and I joined a gym, Presidents and First Lady in Pasadena Tx, not far from Mickey Gilleys dance hall of Urban Cowboy fame. My husband was tall and very lean and took steroids for a short time to bulk up but lost interest after about a year. I loved working out especially as I got lots of attention due to my muscle mass was naturally large (I weighed 155 pounds and had great UE strength) anmd LOVEd the fact that the gym had two tubs of water –a hot tub and COOOlllld plunge!!! LOVED going from one tub back and forth-AWESOME feeling…I really loved the bodybuilding lady, Lisa Lyons, as to me, she was the ultimate portrait of a healthy woman…After I graduated from school in 1984, I worked as an occupational therapist and we moved a lot (my weight fluctuated a lot as well, from 160-200) until arriving back in Texas around 1994..and as my husband was finishing graduate school in art at Univ. of North Texas in Denton Tx, we had a son, Adam. Since I was just shy of 36 years old and had had three miscarriages before Adam, we felt we would not try again. Soon after Adam was born, I had horrible dandruff and itching excema type scalp, and lost about one third of my hairs thickness within that first year. We were an enthusiastic family bed, breastfeeding family, and I breast fed Adam until he was almost five, which was also when he slept in his own room. My weight at my end of pregnancy was 226, at 5’7 ½”. I lost about 50 pounds during breastfeeding that first year and hovered between 170-190 for the next 12-13 years. The two physical things that happened to Adam were his baby teeth enamel was not present so we had to have them capped, and one of his adult canine teeth never appeared. ( His adult teether are great-thick enamel and no cavities so far-he is 19 now) My original husband and I always knew if we had children, one of us would stay home, and we also believed in homeschooling, so since I made much more than my husband I was the primary/only breadwinner, often working three jobs at the same time and it took its toll… After many months of counseling, when our son was 12 years old, Adam’s dad and I separated, though we could not get divorced and we continued to live together due to finances/debt. I ingested my sorrow and cocooned up to about 215 pounds. January 2010 our divorce was final and after almost two years, I moved out of home and to Florida. Adam, my son, decided to remain with his dad, who had fallen in love with a dear friend of both of ours, and they wanted to marry. My original husband and I (and his wife) are better friends than we were husband and wife and it is my deep knowing we were together to create a space for our dear son to be created and raised. Adam is now an outstanding musician, having played classical guitar and jazz guitar since he was 4 ½ years old…he is now also playing saxophone, viola, bass and piano. Adam’s favorite city is New Orleans. During the time I lived in Denton Texas, my weight fluctuated greatly, as did my activity level and eating habits in terms of nutritional value, eating lots of fast food then attempting healthier options (low carb), etc…however, the one constant companion drinkwise was Dr. Pepper (the sugarfied kind). During my original marriage I was diagnosed with hypothyroidism and had a thyroid nodule that was just liquid filled cyst per the endocrinologist. (two years after I had my son Adam) During this time also had several gallbladder issues with a hidascan revealing less than 30% ejection. (one year after my son was born). No more gall bladder attacks since that time, though gastrointestinal- IBS-ish problems on and off.****

****September 2012-Seeking prevention rather than just Treatment of Ills
Spring Hill is on the Nature Coast of Florida. I decided to see a preventative medicine physician, Dr. Erica Bradshaw and got lab works including saliva hormone tests and cortisol tests, CBC, etc…these test results are from September 2012: (menstrual period every 28-40 days, with my last one February 2014 at age 53) Due to poor sleep patterns, gastroinstestinal issues and overall feeling frazzled (lots of changes in a short amount of time) I guess I wanted to include them as it gives yall an idea of what I was like then…and will wait until next month to redo these labs (as well as any others that ya’ll recommend) as it will have been 10 days since I (re) started the CT and Leptin as well as Leaky gut (of which I am certain I have, gut spilling-wise) protocols.
Female Hormone Panels:
Estrone, slavia (e1) : 0.1
Progesterone, (PG): 14.4
Estriol (E3): 15.6
Melatonin, AM 3.5
Cortisol, AM .89
Cortisol, Noon: 1.14
**Cortisol, EVENING: .22
Cortisol, Night: 0.04

Estradiol, Saliva(E2) : 0.5**

Lymphs 29

Antimyleperoxidase (MPO) Abs <9.0 IU/mL

Triiodothyronine, Free, Serum 3.2pg/mL
She also did a metal toxicity test (strands of hair near roots) and found no toxicity present.
So I was taking biomimetic hormones testosterone, estriadol, and progesterone sublingually as well as armour thyroid. In January 2014, I stopped everything, went off medications gradually, as I began practicing daily Qigong and taichi, and wanted to see if these practices would help me…well, yes and no they helped-continued to have IBs type s/s, though sleep patterns better (I would wake up at 3:00Am nightly, walk in the dark to go to bathroom, then fell back asleep after about 5-10 minutes, exercise mostly Taichi, Qigong and walking on treadmill-3.6 mph , swimming, though that consists of mostly relaxing/floating and a few slower laps (5-10 laps a day, nothing outstandingJ ) I used to Love home made breads and meats, but don’t eat bread of any kind anymore due to immediate heartburn feelings (about 5-10 minutes of ingestion). Also with meats it was feeling so heavy in my tummy and so I began gravitating to seafood since I was so close to fresh sea critters, especially oysters (RAW, baby!-with horseradish and lemon) Late this Spring I began seeking something more and through suspicious0bservers.org found this webspace-place.
So, Beginning, again, is my quest, with better supplies and knowledge this journey.**

**Family History
Mom- fibroid tumors mid 40s, total hysterectomy at that time, dx with macular degeneration at age 70, stopped driving last year, she is now 82, osteoporosis at age 76, 5’ 11”, never gained over 10 pounds with stable weight of 140(except twice when she was pregnant, but she was “all baby” –lean and then a huge belly) recent dx of pulmonary fibrosis (she lived near a paper mill for most of her young life into adulthood-all of her sisters dx with that same illness) Brother died of Parkinsons and diabetes six years ago, she also has glaucoma, and dx with hypothyroidism about 30 years ago and taking synthroid and Vit D as well as B12 shots, no HTN, basal cell carcinoma face (surgically removed) and about 35 years ago squamous cell CA removed) She is blue eyed blond and very fair skinned. Mom was born in New Iberia and lived in Louisiana all her life until about 15 years ago when she and dad moved to Birmingham Alabama, to be close to my older sister, who also lives there.
Dad: Dx with aggressive Prostate CA when he was 60, had surgery and radiation and now using implanted female hormone therapy for past 15 years, no reoccurance of elevated PSA, has s/s of Parkinsons, which I first noticed almost 25 years ago (pill rolling tremor, festinating gait,) with my dad in denial (“I am just tired” geez, physicians are not so good patients  )...he developed beer gut (no excessive drinking, drank merlot wine every night) about 20 years ago and keeping weight off a chore until the past six months, when he began wheat belly diet and after four months he dropped about 20 pounds and is keeping it off and doing so much better, more active and walking daily for exercise and Parkisons s/s almost gone, interestingly…oh and a pacemaker implant about five years ago-my dad has always had a very low heart rate but great stroke volume (50-58 BPM) and recently dx with hypothryroidism, before he went on what belly diet protocol, on synthroid. Dad was born in Shreveport Louisiana and both mom and dad lived in Louisiana most of his life until they moved to Alabama to be closer to my sister.**

**Sister: 57 years old, blond hair, fair skin, blue eyes, super petite (5’4” and weighs about 115), has a personal trainer and works out three times a week, a bit wound up personality- wise, but sweet, very directive at times (read "bossy"  ) her two kiddos are adult and out of the house and they are very fit folks. She dx with hypothyroidism about 15 years ago and also take Vit D supplements due to low Vit D. She and her hubbie live in Vestavia Hills, Alabama.
Brother: 51 years old, lean/wiry and muscular, lives in Hammond La, blond, blue eyes olive skinned, has slightly yellowed schlera and slightly enlarged upper abdomen, he has some trouble with heartburn/indegiestion, he does not appear to drink excessively, though I am not around him all the time, his sweetheart says she worried he may have sleep apnea 6’1” lean, muscular and very fit…a bit worried about his eyes..is married to a great Cajun gal and they have two kiddos..**

**So…here I am, so far, and so much more will I travel towards MY Best self, not comparing myself to anyone except myself..PersonalBestIsBest, a never-ending story, each of us..**

**I would LOVE to hear any suggestions, recommendations, etc. and I apologize in advance if the lab info on this gets goobered up when I copy and paste this novella onto the forum.**
**

**"Do not wait until all the conditions are perfect for you to begin. Beginning makes the conditions perfect."
Alan Cohen**

****Thanks for listening-
Have a TantalyzingThursday,**

**Becca**
(edited to fix lab result list and a favorite quote added)**

### becca — 2017-10-24T20:11:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/naturecoastnotsonewbie.16719/post-226497>

Since August 2015 inconsistently following the leptin and cold thermogenesis protocols...which is hard to admit since I knew better and did not DO better....followed Jack for a while on the Quantum group on FB then decided to get back on as a silver member...Since re-upping my silver membership, I am consistently/daily following Leptin and cold thermogenesis protocols and now no longer hypothyroid and vitamin D (on low end of "normal" range), though want to continue to improve in this area...got rid of my transitional lense glasses, only use long distance clear lenses except when I do computer work then wear the red/amber uvex glasses..(ordered some great looking glasses from Matt Maruca last week Weight loss continues 198 (down from 209) pounds presently, not doing any weights, just do isometric/use of body as resistance after 5 pm, leisurely walk with my dog in am for about thirty minutes I float in salt water pool,supine Vitruvian Man posture, for 15-25 minutes then out in sun thirty minutes in the swimsuit that starts with "K"(for better skin access to light). Am meal is not largest yet, though lunch is very light, supper meals before 7 pm and then blue blocking glasses after...presently I am not working full time as an occupational therapist, as the whole paradigm is more distasteful, most of my time spent documenting my usefulness on a computer, and being directed by superiors how to long to continue with a patient, figure that is due to my "dope of mine (dopamine) re energizing and reforming. In addition I am traveling every three weeks to Alabama, to help my sister take care of my mom, who is in last stages of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. I am teaching qigong and taichi to some private clients and also offering PureBioenergy as a re-informing healing modality with good results. I appreciate this forum and this space place in which to NurtureMyTrueNature,.
the hardest thing for me is to watch my dear husband struggle with diabetes and although he is following their protocols to the letter and he is improving (lost ten pounds, blood sugars between 88-119, has only had to inject novalog four times since August), I know he would do well to use recommendations in reducing effects of blue light (he is executive computer software programmer, designs programs then travels world wide to teach them to companies for invoicing and insurance EOBs), and other lifestyle change recommendations; this was my excuse for being inconsistent earlier (he is a night owl, then gets up at 8am, naps at 2 pm-5pm, then stays up til midnight to 1 am), but no more. I came to realize that the only thing I can control is my own wellness, my own be~ing...and he also has that same privilege, to care for himself in the way he sees is best...

### Simon Wright — 2015-08-16T05:34:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tropical-hi.16728/post-174813>

HI Guys,

I am from the SE Asian tropics and have been implementing alot of what has been put forward on the forum and throughout Jacks blog series (20gs magnetico, Unvex Blu light blockers, DHA increase, morning sun gazing and exposure )

I am a man of 45 yearswith my intention with all of this is to maintain my mental and physical capacities as long as i can.

Where i differ in my experience is that i am very naturally focused and instead of using pharmas or hormones i have used herbs. One of the best ways i have found to increase my Testosterone levels is using the following herbs.

1.5 tsp deer antler
1.5 tsp tong kat ali
1.5 tsp Mustard Seed
1.5 tsp Celery seed
1.5 tbsp Galangal (good for NO)
1.5 Muria Purma
1.5 Hawthorn Berry

I add all of these to 1.5ltr of boiling water and then turn it down to the lowest simmer for 30 min. I then have a 1/2 a glass at bed time and upon waking and keep the rest in the fridge or elsewhere aside for later consumption.

To note I also add 1.5tsp of Macuna Pruriens to the above which is very high in Leva Dopa which is the precursor to Dopamine...I have separated this from the other herbs as it is worth mentioning since i have added this to the mix my sleep has been deep and prolific with no interuptions at all for 7-9 hours. In addition my mental clarity and focus the next day is far better. My male hormone response has also been increased noticeably.

Whilst i have no labs to support this my experience of body reactions (ie early morning, desire for sex, general feeling of arousal, lower body fat, maintained muscle mass even while doing no excercise) has all be very positive.

One thing i would like to ask the group is unfortunately i have recently been mugged and beaten with a blunt intrument and this has led me to have 3 large screws in my shoulder.....I was wondering with todays nnEMF will these be effected by such and if so is there anyway to mitigate this?

Best !!

Simon

are you doing C/T?

### jackware — 2015-09-05T21:11:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tropical-hi.16728/post-176558>

# Thread: Hospital EMF mitigation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hospital-emf-mitigation.16732/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-08-17T04:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hospital-emf-mitigation.16732/post-174889>

I've been thinking a lot about people suffering in the hospital, ways to speed recovery and prevent unknown reactions. I hear lots of stories about people that have a successful operation, but 2-3 days later, an infection sets in and the person dies a few days later. Putting on my Sherlock Holmes cap for a second, I think outside of the biological-box and instead think about the vast EMFs found in hospital settings, not to mention the # of floors off the ground and the types of magnetic fields of the devices around people recovering.

We already know that doctors ground their patients during heart operations, but what about trying this:

1\. All hospitals are built as ramblers... all rooms are on the 1st floor to maximize the grounding influence before, during and after surgery.

2\. Certain hospital rooms are Faraday caged off for maximum negation of stray energies. General hospital rooms are built in mind with more reflective wall materials to keep away stray energies.

3\. All phones are wired, and using cells are forbidden in hospital rooms.

4\. Nurse stations have an EMF meter to detect and check magnetic EMF come off of devices, as many machines are rolled in and used with unknown fields produced by them. Rules need to be set to minimize the fields around a person, especially after a recovery from an operation, etc. Nurses could do active checks to ensure other hospital guests are not bringing in stray EMF.

5\. Patients are given RO water and other electron-dense sources before and after surgery.

6\. Lights are amber, and the light-timing matches circadian rhythms. Even hallway lights right outside of rooms are amber or non-blue light emitting.

7\. Morning sunlight exposure on body/skin and for eyes to set circadian rhythm for the day.

8\. (Added 9/17/15): Removal of other RF sources in hospital including Fire Alarm systems back to hardwired.

9\. (Added 9/17/15): Patients can work with doctors to find the optimal Fat-to-Protein-to-Carb ratios before, during and after surgeries. For instance, a person that has been eating mostly a ketogenic diet for the past 3 years could ask for a food ratio of 65% fat: 25% Protein: 10% Carb.

Other thoughts? Wouldn't you like to stay in this hospital?
\--scompy

### RobH — 2015-08-17T18:30:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hospital-emf-mitigation.16732/post-174923>

Great ideas Scompy.

We have a long way to go.

One hospital I work at is now planning on making cardiac stress tests available 24 hours / day in an effort to keep patients moving through. Remember that hospital are paid based on DRGs so they get a certain amount for a diagnosis. Thus, the more volume they see in a unit time the less their labor (biggest) cost / patient, the more the profit.

I have asked in committees several times now what they think about the wisdom of doing cardiac stress tests at 3am and whether or not that could have bearing on the results or harm the patients. I have brought up the words "circadian biology" and "cortisol curve" and been given blank looks. After a few words of explanation from me they have turned back to the committee business and the process seems to move forward....

We have a long way to go to get to the hospital you speak of.

RobH

That sucks!!!!!!

### cinnamon — 2015-08-18T02:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hospital-emf-mitigation.16732/post-174987>

When I spent a week in hospital last summer, I was very glad I had worn my orange glasses in the ambulance on the way there. They were very helpful with all the horrible lighting in the hospital. I got asked a lot of questions about those glasses and they were a good conversation starter.  I don't think I even took them off (except in the OR) for the first 4 days I was there.

I also remember how euphoric I felt the day they took out the NG tube and I was able to go outside! I had not touched the ground since I walked barefoot to the ambulance from my house 6 days earlier. People were staring at me, as I'm sure I was glowing with the excitement of being outside in the sunshine! Sure, I had an IV pole and I was wearing 2 blue hospital gowns (one as a super-hero cape that was blowing in the wind! ). And as I gingerly danced my way over to a bench near the bus stop on the street, wearing goofy stretchy dollar-store slippers with happy faces on them, I was down-right giddy! Once seated, I slipped off the slippers (isn't that why they are called slippers? LOL) to get my feet onto the ground. People looked at me. Then they looked away. I suppose maybe they were thinking, oh, this person is obviously a patient, so something must be wrong, maybe she is "cancer girl" or something, so we should not stare?  LOL. I didn't care. I just kept smiling and laughing and it felt good. I'm sure it was one of the best things for me at the time.

So, yes, I vote for sunshine and grounding to help patients heal!  And some limit to artificial lighting. Maybe we can offer orange glasses to those whose condition requires they be around equipment that gives off artificial light?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-18T04:30:15+0300

### nonchalant — 2015-08-18T17:51:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hospital-emf-mitigation.16732/post-175040>

I visited my FIL in the hospital. He was having issues recovering from pneumonia and flu, and his O2 levels weren't good. I opened the blinds on his east-facing windows, luckily he was in the bed closest to them. He got steadily better, and this was with indirect sunlight through glass. He spent a lot of time looking at the blue sky and clouds while he talked with us. We finally left for a late lunch, but when we came back he was getting worse. The sunshine was on the other side of the hospital, and it was waning. They had to turn up the oxygen.

I asked people to keep his blinds open during the day, but they probably figured I was a little flakey.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-17T18:06:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-to-school-ideas-wifi-and-artificial-light.16734/post-174920>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2015-08-evidence-kids-headaches-back-to-school.html](http://medicalxpress.com/news/2015-08-evidence-kids-headaches-back-to-school.html)

These kids are not getting in enough SN-2 DHA and being bombarded with nnEMF and blue light all day long. The result? Dehydrated cells and loss of DHA from tissues. Their brains are more sensitive than adults which results in more frequent headaches and migraines. Headaches are now common in back to school times because of the combination of wiFi and artificial light. They affect surfaces of the eyes and the skin. Get your kids skin covered in class and consider getting them partial blue blockers like Gunnar's or Jin's. Monitor their sleep. The worse it gets the more these kids will develop skin conditions, stomach aches, asthma, migraines. Artificial light in combination with WiFI will alters ubiquitin proteins in your kid. What are you missing about ubiquitination? The energy contained within the wave (light or electromagnetic wave) is the only thing that determines an electromagnetic radiations power. That power is translated for biology by a protein called ubiquitin. Ubiquitin is a gear like protein to the circadian clocks in all of us. Time manifests or disappears based upon that fundamental interaction of light on atoms on our surfaces: eye, skin, and gut. When this occurs the next step to look for is altered sleep and expect to see a drop in your child's cortisol levels. This is rarely checked by docs but you can check it yourself via sales in an at home test. If you see an abnormal result you will then know your kids are heading to learning issues at school and altered hormone panels. Why? CORTISOL. Altered cortisol leads to lowered oxygen levels in your blood. We call this pseudohypoxia. What does pseudohypoxia mean to our hormone panel? The cost of a high aerobic capacity is low fertility so that we can maintain our cortisol pathways from LDL and Vitamin A as I mentioned the Hormone 101 blog. This is why so many teens have altered sex hormones and why infertility is exploding in young people. This is why the pregnenolone steal syndrome exists. Cortisol over sex steroids is due to the use of oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor in human mitochondria. So when cortisol drops chronically this tells you that your environment is not allowing you to effectively utilize oxygen in your mitochondria as a terminal electron acceptor. What does that mean in english? It means your ubiquitination levels are raised and your mitochondria are pseudohypoxic. This means you are using other gases to perform the tasks that oxygen used to when your environment was more optimized. This causes us to use too much NO, H2S, and CO. These are sub-optimal choices and this leads to altered cortisol and overall hormone panels. All these changes lead to a lack of catalase in cells and altered UV light light release in cells (too much) and all these sequential changes fuel this low fertility and a low cortisol. When you begin to read all the details of the ubiquitin series you will see how pseudohypoxia plays into every mechanism of disease we have in biology and medicine and then you will see this is the huge problem our environment creates and it alters the optical non linear communication pathways between the mitochondria and nucleus. Total failure of these optical pathways lead to many chronic with time. Other disease can manifest prior to the total failure of the energy pathways. You can stop this by understanding how protecting your skin and eyes are. Getting your kids AM sunlight and feeding them DHA laden eggs really helps. Share this info to other parents. These are simple things you can do to protect our kids.

### cantweight — 2015-08-17T21:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-to-school-ideas-wifi-and-artificial-light.16734/post-174956>

Spoke at length with the kids today about how we are going to approach this school year and so far these are the ideas we have come up with....

All waking at 6:30 for a jump in the pool...we will keep it open until freezing become a risk...late novemeber.

A quick swim and as much time in the sun/grounding as we can get followed by protein heavy breakfast

Long sleeve apparel and either gunnars or something similar during class time

After school a DHA snack...sardines, smoked salmon, roe, etc. and grounding perhaps red light at this time....have to investigate that timing.

As luck would have it they all have soccer/field hockey practice between 6-8 pm so a nice stretch of evening sun and exercise 3 nights a week.

As a bonus my husband and I are thinking of rewarding positive behaviors with money....a dollar per serving of seafood eaten, dollar per CT, etc. (wish someone would do that for me...lol!)

Its not anything new to us but its to the next level with a next level understanding as well.

\*I thought this was pretty awesome....my daughter did not make her high school volleyball team, she worked really hard for this and was initially heart broken. That same day we happened to run into a friend who told us about field hockey and immediately got her on the team. Her reaction was that the universe was saving her from an inside sport played in artificial light at night and therefore keeping her circadian rhythm intact.....awesome right???

### magneto — 2015-08-18T09:39:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dairy.16735/post-175011>

Hi,
I'm rather new to the forums but ive been doing a ketogenic paleo diet for about 2 to 3 years now and one thing I go back and forth on is dairy. this excludes grassfed butter which I do great with. its milk, cheese, kefir, and yogurt that I am unclear about. I know that for leptin resistant people Dr. Kruse advises no dairy, but this is not the case for me. I found a source of Raw 100% Grass Fed / Grain free, A2A2, BVD free, Johnes free, Tuberculosis free, Brucellosis free, high butterfat Milk. These farmers have thought of everything. I am just wondering if even beyond that there would be a reason not to drink milk? I do notice i feel better on kefir than just milk or even cheese. can anyone point me in the right direction here. I understand caseine can be a problem and lactose might be the reason I feel better on kefir. I am a college student so i cant take hs-crp, homocysteine, or other labs to test for adverse effects.

### caroline — 2015-08-18T09:55:13+0300

### magneto — 2015-08-18T20:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dairy.16735/post-175049>

From some rather quick research I have read that milk drinking didn't start until about 9000 years ago. I found some anthropological studies showing that mesopotamian sheep herders didnt even like the raw milk and were not lactose tolerant instead they only had fermented milk products. I am considering that milk may trigger some epigenetic switch due to the late introduction into our diet. however you do here of culture like the swiss in weston a price's studies showing perfect health. I am going for the optimal here though. Any thoughts? most of the studies i find on milk are never on the kind of milk I buy so its hard to really make a decision....I realize dairy may be one of those foods that you have to check your own labs to see whether its ok.

### magneto — 2015-08-18T20:24:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dairy.16735/post-175050>

Finally, if you grow up on raw milk then you might have a better equipped gut for it. you might have more lactase production and even studies on european fossils show that lactose tolerance genes didnt show up untill about 5000 years ago so adults before that would not have been able to drink milk. just wanted to add that in. thanks for the feedback.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-18T20:31:37+0300

### magneto — 2015-08-18T22:10:42+0300

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-08-19T01:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dairy.16735/post-175079>

Actually, there are a good amount of studies showing that lactose and casein have "anti-stress" effects, induce mitochondrial biogenesis, etc. Although, as I've stated before, I'm a bigger fan of Dr. Kruse, Dr. Peat has definitely shown me that it isn't dairy that is the problem, it's likely the individual (hence why about 50-60% of the people I know who "follow" his work end up being able to drink milk again, and not even raw milk, but store bought 2%, skim, etc. with no problems). Chris Masterjohn, PhD, wrote about this and regarded to the whole topic as "Peatarded", lol.

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v493/n7433/full/nature11698.html#/ref1](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v493/n7433/full/nature11698.html#/ref1) 7500 years ago, but dairy making was very prevalent in ancient and pre-historic Europe.

[http://news.discovery.com/history/a...dest-cheese-found-on-mummies-necks-140227.htm](http://news.discovery.com/history/archaeology/lumps-of-oldest-cheese-found-on-mummies-necks-140227.htm)

[http://suppversity.blogspot.com/2014/05/bcfa-gut-health-immunity-cancer.html](http://suppversity.blogspot.com/2014/05/bcfa-gut-health-immunity-cancer.html) the branched chain fatty acids are another interesting matter that most don't discuss.

[https://mrheisenbug.wordpress.com/2...antarum-a-keystone-bacteria-for-human-health/](https://mrheisenbug.wordpress.com/2014/02/26/anthropology-science-is-l-plantarum-a-keystone-bacteria-for-human-health/)
Anthropology & Science: Is L. Plantarum a Keystone Bacteria for Human Health?

As you indicated, most dairy was not only raw, but fermented or spoiled. The L.Plantarum then enabled the efficient digestion and utilization of dairy.

Interestingly, what I haven't seen discussed on this forum much, is that a variety of cold adapted cultures actually thrived on dairy (and meat) rich diets, likely due to the thermogenic, uncoupling-induction nature of saturated and branched chain dairy fats (Russians, Northern Europeans, Mongolians (especially during the Mongolian Empire) and others, were all cold adapted and consumed tons of spoiled dairy).

Based off of what Dr. Kruse has been alluding to, my mentioning of the fatty acid content of dairy in cold adapted cultures may actually be off base - the reason it may have helped these cold adapted peoples may be because the vitamin K allowed for maximization of uv light, primarily biophoton emission. This would likely help in the low light, low temp conditions.

# Thread: Randle cycle and criticism of low carb from Dr Mick Wilkinson
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### René Borg — 2015-08-18T12:59:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175015>

The BTR (Born to Run) coaching model created by Lee Saxby has begun to look a bit wider than their core speciality of biomechanics (he now partners with scientist Mick Wilkinson). I am one of the first batch of BTR coaches and respect Lee and Mick but was troubled by some recent discussion in the closed group where they go strongly against the low carb phenomenon blaming it for a lot of the injury and overtraining resulting from 'local energy crisis' .

All coaches were asked to review the original Randle cycle paper and Mick Wilkinson summarised this:

Here is a pithy summary of the Randle Cycle paper as it is quite a daunting read:
Aerobic metabolism is really three linked sets of reactions, 1) breakdown of carbs to pyruvate called glycolysis 2) breakdown of pyruvate to CO2 and electrons called Kreb's cycle 3) use of potential energy of electrons to produce cell energy, ending with water, called the electron transport chain. This is clean, rapid energy production and produces lots of CO2 which is crucial for health. Fats are metabolised by a lengthy and slow process called beta oxidation that ends with a substance that enters near the end of Kreb's cycle. Energy production is slow and much less CO2 is produced. Energy production from fats greatly reduces breakdown of carbs. This is the Randle effect and explains diabetes. Basically, sugar in the blood can't be removed by cells, regardless of how much insulin you throw at them, because the cells ability to use it is being reduced by use of fats. Insulin resistance results, the pancreas wears out and type 2 diabetes eventually become type 1. Instead of training cells to use fat, resulting in a sluggish metabolism, the ability to use sugar must be maintained by eating plenty of it. Sugar produces a high metabolism and a healthy cell. It does not lead to diabetes!

The discussion started because a member of the group had overtraining syndromes and Lee's take on it was:

"Sounds like an ‘energy problem’ to me i.e. a lack of glucose and/or oxygen to the brain. This can be due to ‘orthostatic hypotension’ due to lack of catecholamine production (adrenal fatigue) but I doubt it. In my experience hypoglycaemia is becoming a big problem amongst ‘natural’ runners due to the low carb/sugar phobic/paleo diet obsession. The liver is the ‘glucostatic’ organ and it’s function is to provide the human brain with glucose (the most metabolic expensive organ in the animal kingdom). Solution = maintain blood glucose by eating more often and more sugar. Another problem for ALL modern runners is hypocapnia due to ‘overbreathing’. The blood becomes too alkaline, increasing haemoglobin’s affinity for oxygen which reduces oxygen delivery to the working tissues. Solution = become a ‘nose breather'.

Both these recommendations seem to fly in the face of what is told here. I am curious as to what these two, otherwise, esteemed, gentlemen are missing primarily to come to these conclusions?

three of our athletes have won major running titles using a template similar to the Epi-Paleo Rx this year, so I am obviously convinced by practice but I would like to be elucidated about what the breakdown in logic here is?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-18T16:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175027>

It always ties back to light and circadian biology (and our master clock, the SCN). Clock neurons employ daily anti-phase sodium and potassium conductances to drive rhythmic activity. ATP and Potassium ions are linked to molecules of H2O. When you eat ketogenic you make more ATP by beta oxidation versus glucose metabolism.......36 vs 147 ATP (glucose to fat). ATP function in a quantum cell theory opens proteins structure up to expose more water binding sites in proteins. When electrons are added to proteins it makes them more hydrophilic and this increases the exclusion zone of water inside a cell to make a larger battery in which to run things more energy efficiently. As a result, the amount of ATP required by a cell is reduced. This happens in CT and in REM sleep. ATP production in a cell is stochastically linked to potassium concentration inside the cell. This is why potassium has a much higher concentration inside the cell and sodium is excluded. It is a function of protein recycling (ubiquitin) and ATP levels from mitochondria. So if you all of sudden eat ketotic, but not fat adapted, but become dehydrated quickly inside your cells (high BUN/creat ratio), you can not fully take advantage of the energy surge from water and you think you need carbohydrates to run your physiology. Water is designed to bind to very specific parts of proteins (COO- groups) and potassium to other sites (beta and gamma carbonyl and imino groups). This makes an "intracellular gelatin" or water cable that limits the rotational freedom of K+ and EZ water. EZ water has a net negative charge and K+ is positively charged so they are electrostatically attracted. This is how a cell structures its water from bulk water to structured water (EZ) to make a semiconductor. Structured water means it forms a large exclusion zone. This allows water to become a superconductive cable in a cell to transfer energy (proton tunneling) and it allows water to gain a three dimensional form similar to a liquid crystalline structure of matter. Water is anisotropic in this form so it gives energy flow a specific direction to make deterministic decision for the type of biochemistry possible. In this way what happens at a surface where water exists determines how biochemistry can proceed. to perform quantum processes in tight spaces, like microtubules........so if you are dehydrated and then switch fuels you have only done step one correctly. Go back and look at the Cold Thermogenesis protocol closely: what does it say to due before CT? It says drink cold 32 oz of water before you begin!!! Cold water contains more electrons and therefore it is easier to convert to EZ inside a cell with hydrophilic proteins. Why? What good is using cold to increase your current of flow if you don't have the EZ water surrounding your mitochondria and proteins to make the superconducting cables to begin with?

ATP function is not its use of a high energy substrate as modern cell theory teaches.......ATP is designed to unfold proteins fully to open their carbonyl and imino side chain groups to intracellular water. EZ water is the battery that life runs upon.[http://www.sciencedirect.com/…/article/pii/S0092867415009137](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867415009137)

### René Borg — 2015-08-19T00:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175073>

Thanks Jack, I think there is too big a gap in basic assumptions and knowledge for me to carry that message across but will try to influence bit by bit. I cannot access the full article without paying 31.50 for it unfortunately but I take it this refers to the implications of Gilbert Ling's A-I hypothesis basically as described below:

Ling’s Association-Induction hypothesis goes further in proposing an alternate purpose for ATP in the protoplasmic gel, within Ling’s model ATP acts as a “Cardinal Adsorbent”, that is ATP is capable of changing protein conformation, and hence also the structuring of water within the “cell”. Here adenosine triphosphate (ATP) binds to proteins at certain cardinal sites and in so doing induces changes in electron density of the protein changing its electronic properties, and the way in which it interacts with water. When ATP is bound to proteins it acts to withdraw their electrons allowing them to extend and structure water into a highly ordered, low entropy (Kosmotropic) resting state. Ling (1981) presents evidence that this has nothing to do with hydrolysis, and that ATP has such effects in mitochondria, returning them to a normal size after swelling.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-19T00:58:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175074>

Ling got ATP right.......water wrong. Pollack fixed Ling by moving water from a stackable dipole to a charged EZ.........Other than that Ling was spot on. When electrons are withdrawn from proteins they become less hydrophilic and more hydrophobic making the EZ lower.........This is another reason why ATP is not the be all end all for life.

### René Borg — 2015-08-19T08:52:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175095>

Ok, trying to rephrase this in my head in layman's terms and relate it to the long ongoing discussion on versus

Exercise physiologists today will say that glucose is always the better fuel because They will then go on to say that being fat adapted does not make sense because fat metabolism competes with glucose metabolism thus making an athlete 'less good' at burning the glucose 'they need' for high performance. (gross oversimplifications I know but this is what leading people in that particular field will say)

Their argument rests on two facts they consider rocksteady: 1) the fact that beta-oxidation is slower than glycolysis 2) they assume that the function of ATP is to be a catalyst upon which enzymes act to create energy (rather than its real role of unfolding proteins to increase current?). so they believe that more ATP quicker has to be the end-game because enzymes can then act on it quicker creating energy quicker. What seems to be said here is that if you have the revised physics in mind then that matters less because ATP's role is to increase the current/throughput of energy in the cell. so presumably in this scenario - with turbocharged cells - it does not matter that beta-oxidation is slighty slower than glycolysis in delivering ATP?

So take Mick Wilkinson who goes on to claim:

\>> Aerobic metabolism is really three linked sets of reactions, 1) breakdown of carbs to pyruvate called glycolysis 2) breakdown of pyruvate to CO2 and electrons called Kreb's cycle 3) use of potential energy of electrons to produce cell energy, ending with water, called the electron transport chain. This is clean, rapid energy production and produces lots of CO2 which is crucial for health. Fats are metabolised by a lengthy and slow process called beta oxidation that ends with a substance that enters near the end of Kreb's cycle. Energy production is slow and much less CO2 is produced. Energy production from fats greatly reduces breakdown of carbs. This is the Randle effect and explains diabetes. Basically, sugar in the blood can't be removed by cells, regardless of how much insulin you throw at them, because the cells ability to use it is being reduced by use of fats. Insulin resistance results, the pancreas wears out and type 2 diabetes eventually become type 1. Instead of training cells to use fat, resulting in a sluggish metabolism, the ability to use sugar must be maintained by eating plenty of it. Sugar produces a high metabolism and a healthy cell. It does not lead to diabetes!

The last assertion is staggering: 'instead of training cells to use fat, resulting in sluggish metabolism, the ability to use sugar must be maintained by eating plenty of it.'

Now Mick Wilkinson is an exercise physiologist so not a specialist in this area but the goal of the course has created with Lee (to quote Lee Saxby) is:

' looks like we are beginning to “drown in information whilst starving of wisdom”! The BTR energy course has been created in an attempt to bring clarity to the ‘natural running’ community and their perception of ‘health’ just as the technique course was developed to bring clarity to the ‘natural running’ community and their perception of ‘barefoot technique’. IMHO the ‘teaching the body to burn fat’ over-simplification is as dangerously naïve as the ‘take your shoes off and mother nature will heal you’ over-simplification. The symptoms of hypoglycaemia a la Andrew Barefut are reaching epidemic proportions within the paleo/crossfit/’mover’ fraternities and needs to be addressed. A good place to start is to understand the difference between ‘homeostasis’ and ‘allostasis’ and undisputed pathophysiology of starvation (attached)'

Seems like classical 'throwing the baby out with the bath water' - i.e. 'natural runners/CrossFitters suffer hyperglycaemia from 'teaching the body to burn fat' so 'teaching the body to burn fat is dangerous and here is the research to back it up.

As a running coach this concerns me a great deal when very important influencers in our community seem to have missed the paradigm shift!

### René Borg — 2015-08-19T11:49:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175101>

What Lee/Mick seem to be worried about is their perception that pursuing LCHF/Keto/fat adaptation creates a form of allostatic overload, with energy demand outstripping supply, pushing athletes into survival mode until this allostatic load is lessened (i.e. overtraining syndrome to stop the activity). Their fix is to ensure there is always plenty of sugar around

It is particularly interesting that they see low-carb diets as allostatic because the very core of the BTR model is evolutionary biology and evolutionary medicine. They seem to have opted for an interpretation of human evolution and subsequent physiology which puts us down as omnivores (not disagreement there) but with a preference for carbs/sugars (big disagreement there) which seems an odd choice. I can only assume it is because they believe the key adaptations we have today were formed during our species' time in the Rift Valley area and they assume that, at the time, carbs were plentiful year round and our main source of fuel. This also implies they believe that we have not yet fully adapted to the northern climates we migrated to where carbs were clearly not available year round. Alternatively, they may believe that the 'winter metabolism' (fat dominant) is allostatic and thus not suited for high performance sport whereas only the summer (carb dominant) metabolism is homeostatic. Conclusion then, for them, would invariably be that performance athletes need to stay in 'summer metabolism' year round as they cannot perform in a state of allostasis.

So allostatic theory posits that metabolic syndrome, for instance, is created by a combination of hypervigilance (the brain crying 'where is my usual food gone!') and hyposatisfaction ('I cannot believe I cannot eat that cake like all my friends'). There are some big leaps in that logic, although its compelling, such as 1) what if you enjoy the new food more than the old, 2) what if you allow small deviations at important social events, 3) what if you perception of what food is healthy changes, 4) what if you change the environmental signalling that caused the craving for the particular food from which you are now deprived is changed and 5) what if the body is setup to use the new food better than the old (thus the brain picks up an internal signal about 'healthier' cells).

I can see why you'd read this into it: i.e. Paleo guy who loves his carbs goes on ketogenic diet while continuing to do CrossFit. Suddenly there is a huge physiological demand for carbs (CrossFit exercise, environmental signalling from bluelight etc.) strengthened by a psychosocial signal creating more stress ('everyeone else is eating carbs, how will I cope? the doubt!') . Easy to imagine that going awry and coaches then loking at it and concluding 'its not worthwhile turning people who have eaten primarily carbs into anything else as the physiological and psychosocial stress caused by this change will be too big for adaptation to occur).

### Joe Gavin — 2015-08-19T14:42:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175104>

> [René Borg said:](/goto/post?id=175101)
>
> What Lee/Mick seem to be worried about is their perception that pursuing LCHF/Keto/fat adaptation creates a form of allostatic overload, with energy demand outstripping supply, pushing athletes into survival mode until this allostatic load is lessened (i.e. overtraining syndrome to stop the activity). Their fix is to ensure there is always plenty of sugar around
>
> It is particularly interesting that they see low-carb diets as allostatic because the very core of the BTR model is evolutionary biology and evolutionary medicine. They seem to have opted for an interpretation of human evolution and subsequent physiology which puts us down as omnivores (not disagreement there) but with a preference for carbs/sugars (big disagreement there) which seems an odd choice. I can only assume it is because they believe the key adaptations we have today were formed during our species' time in the Rift Valley area and they assume that, at the time, carbs were plentiful year round and our main source of fuel. This also implies they believe that we have not yet fully adapted to the northern climates we migrated to where carbs were clearly not available year round. Alternatively, they may believe that the 'winter metabolism' (fat dominant) is allostatic and thus not suited for high performance sport whereas only the summer (carb dominant) metabolism is homeostatic. Conclusion then, for them, would invariably be that performance athletes need to stay in 'summer metabolism' year round as they cannot perform in a state of allostasis.
>
> So allostatic theory posits that metabolic syndrome, for instance, is created by a combination of hypervigilance (the brain crying 'where is my usual food gone!') and hyposatisfaction ('I cannot believe I cannot eat that cake like all my friends'). There are some big leaps in that logic, although its compelling, such as 1) what if you enjoy the new food more than the old, 2) what if you allow small deviations at important social events, 3) what if you perception of what food is healthy changes, 4) what if you change the environmental signalling that caused the craving for the particular food from which you are now deprived is changed and 5) what if the body is setup to use the new food better than the old (thus the brain picks up an internal signal about 'healthier' cells).
>
> I can see why you'd read this into it: i.e. Paleo guy who loves his carbs goes on ketogenic diet while continuing to do CrossFit. Suddenly there is a huge physiological demand for carbs (CrossFit exercise, environmental signalling from bluelight etc.) strengthened by a psychosocial signal creating more stress ('everyeone else is eating carbs, how will I cope? the doubt!') . Easy to imagine that going awry and coaches then loking at it and concluding 'its not worthwhile turning people who have eaten primarily carbs into anything else as the physiological and psychosocial stress caused by this change will be too big for adaptation to occur).

Interesting Rene. Have you read the Phinney and Volek books on low-carb performance? Not just about reducing carbs, other factors at play. Might be helpful. Also, you know where this tribe stands on Light, Water and Magnestism. Circadian biology, etc. Seasonal eating of carbs. Blue Light. Fair number of confounders at play with the Wilkinson group. I guess my take is this: the body has 100,000 calories of fat, and about 2-3,000 calories of carb. Plus we know fat grams throw off MUCH more ATP vs sugar grams. Yet, Wilkinson doesn't even seem to care or conveniently dismisses. Hard to reconcile that in my mind.

### René Borg — 2015-08-19T17:18:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175113>

> [Joe Gavin said:](/goto/post?id=175104)
>
> Interesting Rene. Have you read the Phinney and Volek books on low-carb performance? Not just about reducing carbs, other factors at play. Might be helpful. Also, you know where this tribe stands on Light, Water and Magnestism. Circadian biology, etc. Seasonal eating of carbs. Blue Light. Fair number of confounders at play with the Wilkinson group. I guess my take is this: the body has 100,000 calories of fat, and about 2-3,000 calories of carb. Plus we know fat grams throw off MUCH more ATP vs sugar grams. Yet, Wilkinson doesn't even seem to care or conveniently dismisses. Hard to reconcile that in my mind.

Thanks Joe. I was trying to meet them in the area where there was most common understanding first before launching into light, water and magnetism (sage advice from Barry who has been down this road many times). I think I might have failed so far, the below was my latest attempt. I am aware I left out much of the 'real story' and again it was in an attempt to meet them on 'common ground'.

I looked at the graph (ed: Lee Saxby posted a a graph showing how gluconeogenesis happens during later stages of starvation and to support his assertion that gluconeogenesis represents an allostatic, thus stressful state, and not a homeostatic state) but it does not tell me what it tells you, to explain myself: we agree on the OBSERVATION (lots of runners getting hypoglycaemic from their attempt to implement what they perceive as ‘low-carb’) but we disagree on the FACTS that you use to form the THEORY and because of that we divert on the WISDOM (how to fix it).

The facts we disagree on:

1) the Randle cycle interpretation is too simplistic (the inhabitation of carbohydrate metabolism by fatty acids is turned off during exercise by the activation of AMPK in skeletal muscle turning on catabolic ATP-generating pathways (fatty acid oxidation and glycolysis) and turning off anabolic ATP consuming processes (lipid and protein synthesis) This provides an immediate metabolic adaptation to the stress conditions regardless of whether you are ‘fat’ or ‘carb’ adapted. And there’s much more to this than that.

2) low-carb = gluconeogenesis = starvation response = stress (it is not that simple: ok, so gluconeogenesis occurs when blood glucose is low. For that reason it also happens every night while we sleep).

The latter is an example of correlation not being causation in a nutshell -> you seem to be telling me that ‘because gluconeogenesis occurs in the later phases of starvation and starvation is a stressor then gluconeogenesis is a stressor.’ That’s a bigger assumption than I can accept – because it rests only on what I interpret as the misconception that cortisol is always released during GNG (in fact it would be ludicrous – because we use gluconeogenesis while we sleep and cortisol is low while we sleep and rises when we have to wake!) . That is because glucagon is triggered before blood sugar rises to a level that triggers cortisol (glucagon is triggered at 65 mg/Dl blood sugar threshold whereas cortisol only kicks in at 55 mg/Dl). The lower threshold is only reached if glucagon is somehow obstructed such as if you have a GNG disorder, eat insufficient protein, or have been engaged in long periods of fasting. It’s easy to test if people doubt this: buy a glucometer and eat as you have been doing. Perform a test. If the result is lower than 55 mg/Dl then start by trying to eat more protein or timing and frequency of meals. Failing that simple solution have a trained nutritionist look over three days of food intake to see what you might be doing wrong.

Cortisol is seen as leading to insulin resistance, among others things, but it only does this within very narrow contexts. Cortisol levels are not simply cortisol levels just as a 4 mmol/lactate can mean many different things. An athlete’s cortisol profile depends on:

1\. cortisol production,

2\. cortisol clearance rates and

3\. 11β-HSD1 expression in fat cells versus11β-HSD1 expression in the liver (thisdetermines when and where cortisol is regenerated)

What research actually shows is that a ketogenic diet IMPROVES the cortisol PROFILE while reversing symptoms of metabolic syndrome. If it was a contributory factor to insulin-resistance and metabolic syndrome this simply could not be the case.

To narrow it down to the more specific context: hypoglycaemia from OVEREXERCISE/OVERTRAINING. On the wisdom front: Easily fixed by carb cycling after exercise and taking in carbs during longer strenuous exercise. I.e. instead of putting the athlete straight into ‘2 hour fasted state runs’ you have him do a ‘long run without breakfast’ where he takes some dextrose or similar after 45 min and again at 90 min. next time it might be 60 minutes at 90 minutes etc. This is what Barry did – working up to 5-6 hour bike rides and ultra runs on nothing.

During the day, relying mainly on a very low level of ketones plus gluconeogenesis to supply the brain with fuel and otherwise burning fat does not require a cortisol response unless we have the aforementioned GNG disorders. This observation born out in practice (and research): I never feel more relaxed than during days where no carbs are eaten for breakfast. If a workout is then done at lunch and I can feel the ‘stress reaction/hyperglycaemic reaction’ I simply up the dose of post-exercise carbs a bit on normal. I know when I get it right because the ‘tense feeling’ disappears. If I overeat carbs I also feel the effect – getting very sluggish and wanting to nap straight away or later in the afternoon. This is N=1 but consistent with the theory are looking at and the practice of a wide range of highly respected nutritionists (although I admit, not the mainstream). Also, again, I am not discussing full-blown ketosis here. As far as I am concerned the data suggest this is mainly valuable for treatment of specific diseases and disorders, I know many scientists would like to go further than that in their conclusions.

Hypoglycemia always goes hand in hand with cortisol but low carb does not necessarily cause hypoglycaemia which is what I feel is being presented here.

So we disagree on some key facts and it leads to a different practical conclusion ‘i.e. ‘how we can we ensure people can do it right, so it is not stressful’ rather than ‘let’s stop this low-carb nonsense before it hurts anyone else’. Those are our two diverging paths out this forest it seems ,if I am reading the discussion so far right?

So in summary: I cannot accept that graph because I do not accept it as a fact that gluconeogenesis is inherently an abnormal, and stressful, response. Rather the opposite. Excessive gluconeogenesis. Different story. Gluconeogenesis in people with glucogan issues, different story.

Suppose I will know soon if I managed to raise an eyebrow.

### René Borg — 2015-08-19T17:36:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/randle-cycle-and-criticism-of-low-carb-from-dr-mick-wilkinson.16736/post-175116>

Oh, and yes I did read Volek and Phinney's two books. While I found them convincing I had the feeling I was not going to get anywhere when one group - the Czech coach Daniel McVisser, who is a master coach, in the system threw a dismissive line about Volek, basically saying that the Eskimo ketogenic myth had been debunked (no examples found in 60 years I believe was the claim) and seemed to use that as reason enough to dismiss the entirety of Volek's work.

So I just decided not to go down that alley as I'd have to first rehabilitate Volek and reestablish him as credible in the eyes of the group.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-20T16:11:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-175201>

taking antioxidants has been covered in blog........losing strategy. In researching nutrition research and conventional dogma is more or less "the more antioxidants, the more nutritious" and better health you have. Recent work in mitochondrial research says exactly the opposite because antioxidants are signaling molecules for mitochondria and this disrupts signaling and does not help it. You need some superoxide burst for mitochondrial biogenesis........taking too many antioxidants may not be wise. T2D have no superoxide pulses. Antioxidants have frequently been touted by nutrition providers as the antidote to oxidative stress of a low redox potential, but they are only part of the complex chemical story. Although oxidation can lead to toxicity, it is important to realize that redox-based molecules are essential mediators of critical functions in physiological systems and are essential to immunity against all diseases. In this way, redox molecules are designed to act to “open the doors of heaven or hell” based upon the charge status of the local environment of the cell or tissue in question.

The role of redox molecules, such as nitric oxide (NO slows ECT to make ROS) and reactive oxygen species (ROS are the signals of entanglement), as key mediators of immunity and wellness protectors has recently garnered renewed interest and appreciation in the literature. To regulate immune responses, these redox species trigger the eradication of pathogens on the one hand and modulate immunosuppression during tissue-restoration and wound-healing processes on the other. They play a huge role in the development of autoimmune conditions.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-20T16:12:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-175202>

he free radical theory of aging that Harman proposed long ago that oxidized macromolecules accumulate with age to decrease cell function and shorten life-span has little support these days (Harman, 1968). Unfortunately today this is biologic dogma because no one else has any better ideas how mitochondria are really designed to work in nature. Recent experimental work has shown it is dead wrong. When your experiments do not match your theory, your theory is wrong. Moreover, recent nutritional and genetic interventions to boost antioxidants have generally failed to increase life-span. The overall result of 19 clinical trials finds supplementation with the lipid-soluble antioxidant vitamin E failed to reduce mortality (Miller et al., 2005). The water soluble antioxidant vitamin C is also generally ineffective in reducing all-cause mortality (Bjelakovic et al., 2007). An even more important test of the free radical theory of aging involves genetic overexpression of antioxidant enzymes. To date, increases in SOD, or catalase or a combination, while lowering oxidized macromolecules, fail to increase lifespan in mice (Perez et al., 2009). Only overexpression of the peroxide and redox active thioredoxin 1 (Mitsui et al., 2002) and mitochondrial targeted catalase (Schriner et al., 2005) have been shown to increase mouse lifespan. In addition, the free radical theory fails to explain why higher levels of oxy-radical damage occurs constantly with exercise (Powers et al., 2008), which generally promotes healthy human aging (Nakamura et al., 1996) and extends lifespan in some rodents (Navarro et al., 2004) (Holloszy et al., 1985). The answer is not increasing the ORAC levels of food. It is found in metabolic epigenetic redox switches that happen upstream to mitochondria to alter where and how subatomic particles are fed into the ETC when nutrients are delivered to this organelle for processing. . The metabolically initiated redox shift happens "upstream" of the commonly observed increase in reactive oxidative damage to lipid membranes, receptors, and macromolecules in tissues. This shift has been constantly observed experimentally in normal aging; It occurs in the oxidized direction of the relative levels of important reductants and oxidants in cells. It manifests itself as an extracellular decrease in the ratio of cysteine/cystine in tissues with a simultaneous intracellular decrease in the ratio of GSH/GSSG and NAD(P)H/NAD(P). The oxidized redox shift is initiated by low demand for bursts of energy produced by mitochondria. The loss of energy efficiency is the key signal This low demand for energy also is manifest by observed low levels of physical and mental activity in people with this issue. This initiates a vicious cycle of oxidized membrane receptors, signaling molecules, transcription factors and epigenetic transcriptional regulators on a chronic basis. This increase ubiquination and the process steepens with respect to time. Organ failure and aging increase with respect to chronologic age. Epigenetic factors modulated by aging include the histone deacetylase family which includes the sirtuins as well as histone acetylases and DNA methyltransferases. Together and collectively, the epigenetic mediators impose the metabolic shift away from use of mitochondrial energy toward reliance on glycolysis. All of these complex epigenetic coupled factors are tied to inflammation generation via the NRF2 and KEAP1 transcription factors. This is why inflammation appears to be tied to all these things we talk about in the blogosphere. The key is understanding what happens proximal to the mitochondria and then how the mitochondria respond.

This metabolic shift is further mediated by insulin resistance since a sedentary life does not require the metabolic demands that need to be regulated by insulin. That product, (I think), is concerned with delivering choline thru blood-brain barrier.

That statement, likely uncovers major problem about Optimal PC.
Or any lecithin that often is called PhosphatidylCholine (because law allows that).

The whole idea about (BodyBio PC) is to made it in such a way, that the whole PhosphatidylCholine can make it to the cell membrane in the brain (and some also into mitochondrial membrane) without it being broken into individual pieces.

If Optimal PC worries about (individual piece (choline), that is already a deal breaker.

### JanSz — 2017-01-06T23:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206250>

\[qu ote="SatoriHeart, post: 206249, member: 19303"\]The phospholipids in standard health food store lecithin are degraded beyond usability. **Really the phospholipids there are soaked in oil and not able to move.**

BodyBio PC appears to be an extract from lecithin, so the problems with soy are likely insubstantial.
**But BodyBio PC have unsaturated fats vibrating. Vibration=life.**
That said, Optimal PC's mention of choline crossing the BBB is misleading. Lipid-soluble forms (such as phosphytidylcholine) bypass the liver once absorbed, while water-soluble forms (such as free choline) enter the liver portal circulation, where they are usually absorbed by the liver. **If the product states "phosphatidylcholine", then it most certainly contains the complete, lipid-soluble form.**
**Attention.**
**Lipid soluble is bad or ineffective, digested into pieces.**
**We want it to be water soluble.**
**There is a way to do it.**
**Listen to Ed Kane when he describes what lawyers did to the word Phosphatidylcholine.**

**The truth of the matter is that PC can be synthesized from dietary choline in one of two ways: via the CDP-choline pathway or through the methylation of PE (phosphatidylethanolamine).**
**Can and will are tho different states.**
**Practically it will not.**

**Sphingomyelin, in turn, is synthesized** by the transfer of a phosphocholine residue from a phosphatidylcholine to a ceramide.

**There is a problem here.
It works so well that there is way too much of sphingomyelin.
That sphingomyelin must be replaced with Phosphatidylcholine (mostly) or one of the other Phosphati-XXXX-cholines, for membrane to work as it should.**

You already know what I am going to say. If you have epigenetically activated SNPs in choline kinase, choline phosphate cytidylyltransferase, 1,2-diacylgylcerol choline phosphotransferase, or PEMT, then in vivo synthesis of PC will be compromised, to one degree or another. Such gene SNPs would make exogenous supplementation of PC not only wise but completely necessary. Now you see how I use genetic data -- to explain why lab values are what they are. I will be certain to post my observations once I have had a chance to do the Comprehensive Fatty Acids panel.
**I will love to look at your Fatty acids, TotalCholesterol and other.**

The only thing, as far as I can see, that makes BodyBio PC unique is that it is **formulated with an essential lipids ratio of 4:1,** whereas most other PC supplements use an EFA ratio of ~12:1.
**Do you know what they mean by 4:1 here, because I am not sure.**
**4:1 usually mean 4:1 of LA:ALA (when eating).**
**In blood fatty acid analysis nothing is 4:1.**

So the ratio is certainly preferred, but this does not increase the bioavailability of the constituents. It just alters the body's internal lipid profile transiently. What it does, beyond that point, has everything to do with how you operate metabolically.

**In my mind 4:1 (whatever it is), is important by secondary.**
**Paramount is a shape of that stuff, so it makes from the mouth to the brain or other cells and mitochondrial membranes, in one unaltered piece.**

Question ... how do you think endogenous phospholipase levels would affect your utilization PC? Is consuming PC orally going to alter its structure in any way on its way to the brain? I believe so. We must think of other ways to support the creation of PC endogenously. We'll save that topic for another time.
**I have no clue, can't wait for explanation.**
\[/quote\]

Life is interesting. Thank you.

mTORC2 is at the core of a Membrane Associated Membrane (MAM) signaling hub that controls growth and metabolism.

The membrane (MAM) is a subcompartment of the ER that forms a **_quasisynaptic structure_** with mitochondria which forms quasiparticles like chlorophyll does. The main function of this membrane is to facilitate the transfer of lipids and calcium between the two organelles quantum mechanically using excitons and solitons. MAM thereby controls mitochondrial physiology and apoptosis using quantum emchanical mechanisms. Phosphorus is special on the periodic table for one reason.

All a cell has to do to create energy from sunlight endogenously photoelectrically without any fancy electric power grid is to simply expose water to sunlight in the presence of phosphorus. all proteins with phosphorus are hydrated for this reason. Red phosphorous is most stable and commonly found isoforms of that element on Earth. In the suns light, phosphorus breaks up water molecules to give off bubbles of hydrogen gas, which is a clean fuel. So phosphorus in the sun's light is a photocatalyst that mimics photosynthesis on a leaf by having the ablity to burn water to liberate hydrogen and free electrons. Cyanobacteria began to use phosphorus in this way to control its circadian cycles and with evolutioanry time this energy source fueled the Cambrian explosion to make DHA and oxygen to build the foundations of human cells eventually.
