The amount and type of free radical signals released also change. When nitrogen gets oxidized, this means electrons are taken away from its molecular structure. When electrons are removed from any proteins it makes them less hydrophilic and as a result, the size of the EZ in cells drops. This is also true in the MINOS (water) that surrounds the cytochromes. The MINOS must remain reduced, ie filled with electrons, to maintain a large EZ to continue to be able to exclude protons. This is why the mitochondrial matrix is filled with protons and why there should not be a lot of free protons outside the matrix.

This sets up chemiosmotic gradient between the two areas of a cell. This can be used to do work and harvest energy. When the size of the EZ drops, ammonia rises and the mTOR pathway is activated. Resveratrol, turmeric, and metformin has been shown to prevent ammonia toxicity in cells. When protons cannot be contained within the matrix or the EZ decreases the mTOR pathway is activated because beta oxidation ability is lost in the cytosol of mitochondria and the mitochondria has to rely exclusively on the TCA for energy production. This is an inefficient strategy and when you have a human brain it becomes a curse that causes disease. It is not a problem if you are c elegance and a simple creature. This is why what happens in a worm rarely is germane to humans and why mTOR is completely misunderstood by biology today. This burns out cytochrome 1 and superoxide burst. This is why mTor expression is a problem for generating longevity, because longevuity is linked to using beta oxidation over the TCA most of the time humans are alive because it creates 3.5 times more energy from amitochondria to power human complexity. Worms have no complexity. The process works by limiting or lowering ubiquitin marking of proteins in cells because replacing proteins costs mitochondria the most energy. With a Warburg shift you cannot replace the proteins when the mitochondria is defaulted to glucose and glutamine metabolism. With mTOR activation we see pseudohypoxia develop in mitochondria at the terminal electron location and this lowers delta psi and as a result ECT slows, and disease manifest in those tissues. The collateral damage or symptoms of the redox shift are as follows: NAD+ levels drop in relation to a rising NADH level at cytochrome 1.

Why should you care deeply about the nitrogen atoms present in NAD+? When nuclear NAD+ levels drop, cells lose the ability to properly regulate mitochondrial homeostasis independently of PGC-1α/β pathways. As a result % heteroplasmy in a tissue rises and energy flows slow even more. This is why anesthetics demolish brains with AD and PD. PCG-1α is a so activated by endurance exercise and sprinting. This is why exercise helps PD and AD patients on a short term basis. > **mTOR Inhibition**
>
> -   Metformin inhibits mTOR both directly and indirectly through activation of AMPK
> -   Resveratrol also activates AMPK via STK11 by increasing cellular AMP
> -   AMPK activation increases NAD+
> -   Higher NAD+ activates SIRT1, leading to NF-kB and PPAR-gamma inhibition (i.e. less inflammation / WAT)
> -   Higher SIRT1 expression leads to deacetylation of PGC-1a
> -   PGC-1a methylates mitochondrial DNA to stimulate biogenesis and oxidative metabolism
>

This paper has some interesting observations on water

> The authors conclude that below 60°C the hydration shells have a hydrophobic-enhanced water structure with a greater tetrahedral order and fewer weak hydrogen bonds than the surrounding bulk water. This configuration disappears above 60°C and is replaced by a structure with weaker bonds. These findings support the existence of two different hydration shells in liquid water with a crossover temperature of ≈60°C

### Joe Gavin — 2017-01-10T19:12:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206532>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=206437)
>
> April 2016 webinar was all about what? Scale. Water does anomolous things below 2 nm.

from Mae Wan Ho paper:

New Quantum States of Water in Nanospaces and Superconducting Protons
Research led by George Reiter at the University of Houston Texas uses deep neutron inelastic
scattering to measure the momentum distribution of protons in water confined to nanospaces. The
momentum of the proton is mainly determined by the wave-function of the proton’s ground-state (least
energetic state).
The team investigated carbon nanotubes, glass sponge and Nafion membranes and found similar
results \[37,38\]. The confined water adopts a variety of new quantum states distinct from bulk water
and highly dependent on the precise dimensions of the nanospace. In other words, water adopts a
single quantum state, wherever it is, which is quite remarkable in itself (I invite you to think about the
ocean, when it is clear and calm and when raging with tsunami (see \[39\])). Water confined in space
dimensions of 2 nm or smaller has protons that are coherently delocalized in two momentum states.
In xerogel, a glass sponge with Si-OH (silanol) groups lining the pores that can hydrogen bond with
water, the proton momentum distribution of water in 24 Å pores at room temperature is confined in a
double-well potential. For larger pores of 82 Å, the average momentum distribution was close to that
of bulk water, though still quite distinct \[37\].
The perfluorosulphonic acid membranes, Nafion 1120 and Dow 858, are polymers consisting of a
hydrophobic poly(tetrafluoroethylene) backbone and randomly distributed side chains of
Entropy 2014, 16 4882
perfluoroether terminating with sulphonic acids. When hydrated, nanophase separation occurs in which
water is confined in domains of a few nanometres in diameter surrounded by hydrophobic regions. The
sulphonic acid group (-SO3H) donates its proton to water when there is sufficient water in the pores,
making them very good proton conductors. The momentum distributions at room temperature for
water in the two membranes are dramatically different from water in the bulk: the kinetic energy is
higher in Nafion by 107 meV/proton and in Dow 858 by 124 meV/proton. They are qualitatively
different quantum states from bulk water. At a concentration of 14 H2O/SO3H for both membranes,
Dow 858 has a significantly higher conductivity by 70% than Nafion, consistent with a higher degree
of proton delocalization \[38\].
In a later publication, X-ray Compton scattering was used to probe the electronic ground state of
nanospace-confined water \[40\]. They found that the difference in “bond disorder” between water
confined in Nafion and bulk water is 17-times larger than that between bulk water just above the
freezing point and just below the boiling point. That is not surprising given that the proton is
coherently distributed in double wells separated by ~0.3 Å. The kinetic energy has gone up because
each of these wells is more tightly binding the proton than the covalent bond of the isolated water
molecule. The kinetic energy (measured by deep inelastic neutron scattering) is 245 meV and 268 meV
for Nafion and Dow, respectively, compared with 148 meV for bulk water at room temperature. The
change in kinetic energy in going from 5 °C to 95 °C for bulk water is only 0.5 meV.
Further support for the new quantum states of nanospace-confined water comes from excited state
proton transfer measurements of a fluorescent probe molecule, 8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,5-trisulphonate
(HPTS). HPTS tends to stay in the middle of the water-filled regions in Nafion. When excited by a
laser pulse, the proton in the OH group of HPTS is ionized and rapidly transferred to the surrounding
solvent \[41\]. The recombination time depends on the transport processes affecting the free proton.
Diffusion in bulk water showed t−1.5 dependence. In Nafion, the rate observed is much slower at t−0.8,
because it requires the redistribution of electrons through the hydrogen-bonded network. Similar
results were obtained in reverse micelles, droplets of aqueous solution enclosed in a lipid membrane
and dispersed in an organic solvent. Both reverse micelles and the water-filled nanospaces in Nafion
are similar to the nanospaces inside the cell. Additionally, these findings will certainly have
implications for biochemical reactions in the cell (see later).
Hydrated Nafion consists of long parallel, but otherwise randomly-packed water channels
surrounded by partially hydrophilic side branches, forming inverse-micelle cylinders. At 20% by volume
of water, the water channels have diameters between 1.8 and 3.5 nm with an average of 2.4 nm \[42\].
Nafion films have a proton conductivity of about 0.1 S/cm (S, Sieman = 1 Ampere/Volt), among the
highest in proton exchange membranes (PEMs). For a comparison, the electrical conductivity of
copper is 596,000 S/cm and silicon, a semiconductor, 0.156 S/cm \[43\]. However, the conductivity of a
single high purity Nafion nanofibre 400 nm in diameter made by electrospinning reached 1.5 S/cm, an
order of magnitude greater, as demonstrated by Yossef A Elabd at Drexel University Philadelphia in
PA, USA, and his colleagues \[44\]. This is due to the alignment of interconnected ionic aggregates
along the fibre axis direction, as evidenced by X-ray scattering.
Entropy 2014, 16 4883
8\. Proton Transport through Carbon Nanotubes
Very fast proton transport was reported in carbon nanotubes, ~40-times the rate in bulk water in
molecular dynamic simulations \[45\]. More recent ab initio (starting from first principles) path-integral
(quantum approach) molecular dynamics simulations showed no energetic barrier to proton transfer in
every case when quantum delocalization is taken into account. The main difference between bulk
liquid water and water confined in a carbon nanotube is a favourable pre-alignment of water molecules
in the latter case. Configurations where the excess proton is quantum delocalized over several adjacent
water molecules along with continuous interconversion between different hydration states reveal that,
as in bulk water, the hydrated proton under confinement is best described as a fluxional defect, rather than
any individual hydration state of the excess proton propagated classically along the water chain \[46\].
Wonjoon Choi at Massachusetts Institute of Technology Cambridge in the USA led an investigation
into the diameter-dependence of proton transport through the interior of isolated, specially fabricated
single-walled carbon nanotubes 1 mm long, and demonstrated a surprising five-fold enhancement of
stochastic proton transport rates at a diameter of approximately 1.6 nm, dropping off sharply to either
side of the peak \[47\]. The diameter of 1.6 nm is near the transition between water behaving rather as it
would in an ordinary capillary tube at diameters >1.6 nm and water behaving just the opposite way at
diameters <1.4 nm in a temperature-diameter phase diagram \[48\]. At a diameter of ~1.5 nm, double- or
triple-walled water nanotube structures have been proposed. Whether these structures are responsible
for the sharp increase in proton conduction at 1.6 nm is not yet known.
Not only does nanospace-confined water show high temperature superconductivity, it also exhibits
superfluidity, with a flow rate enhancement of 50- to 900-fold, depending on diameter, compared with
that predicted from conventional fluid-flow theory \[52\]. This has large implications for water transport
into cells and electrolyte balance crucial for the health of cells and organisms.
**These results of carbon nanotube-confined water are most relevant to water associated with
collagen fibres.**

### Jack Kruse — 2017-01-11T23:51:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206630>

Water is a fascinating complex topic........most people do not jump down this rabbit hole enough because they just are not educated deeply enough on physical chemistry. Blood is effectively a magnetohydrodynamic plasma for sun light. Hemoglobin's peak absorptions' telegraph this idea to the ripe mind.......and the fact that aromatic amino acids are the favorite amino acids in construction of the blood should be a tell too.

Ketchup is thixotropic.......so is blood. When sunlight energizes it, it changes its density. This is a physical change. This is the mark of a plasmoid. Guess what else undergoes density changes with light? Water.........and water makes up 93% of blood plasma. Water is not homogenous........it has very different physical properties when the environment changes. When the scale of action is also varied the anomalies of water become quite important for biology.

EZ is 10% more viscous than bulk water. What are the physics of viscosity? Are these general to all liquids or is water different? Water is very different and does things we do not expect especially when cooled or heated as the water is confined in cells. The density of EZ water is also 10% higher than bulk water. Confined water does exhibit different thermodynamic properties, such as reduced freezing point, and understanding how the effects of confinement change with confinement volume and geometry is an active area of research and is by no means settled. Martin Chaplin is my go to water researcher. Pollack is easy to understand for the lay person but for my money is not sophisticated enough for the quantum biologic realm that medicine needs. Nonetheless I find his book helpful to teaching the public about water. The thermodynamic changes I believe can largely be explained by the macroscopic phenomena of Laplace pressure of the solid-liquid boundary but I think this idea does not go far enough to explain how water that is confined and heated does some unusual things around mitochondria.

A very interesting and very promising theory for the exclusion zone can be found in these papers:
(1) Phenomena Associated with Gel-Water Interfaces. Analyses and Alternatives to the Long-Range Ordered water Hypothesis ( J. M. Schurr, J. Phys. Chem. B 2013, 117, 7653-7674)
— for background, see A Theory of Macromolecular Chemotaxis (J. M. Schurr et al., J. Phys. Chem. B 2013, 117, 7626-7652)
(2) Long-range Repulsion of Colloids Driven by Ion Exchange and Diffusiophoresis (D. Florea et al., Proc. Natl. Acad. Sri. USA 2014, 111, 6554-6559)

### Jack Kruse — 2017-01-12T00:06:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206631>

I've told you all in the blog that hydrogen is the ultimate chameleon of the periodic table. I think some critics thought I was joking or overstating it. I was not. I was being quite literal. Hydrogen bonds increase the dipole moment (polarity) of the hydrogen bond donator in reactions. This actually goes to why water expands when it freezes and why it shrinks in clusters at small scales as its density changes.

That is why the dipole moment increases from the gas phase value of 1.85 D to the value in the liquid of 2.7-3 D. In ice, where each molecule has 4 strong H-bonds, the dipole moment is I believe around 3.2 D.

The liquid phase value of ~3D is well established at this point, for instance see:
[http://scitation.aip.org/content/aip/journal/jcp/117/11/10.1063/1.1501122](http://scitation.aip.org/content/aip/journal/jcp/117/11/10.1063/1.1501122)

Another way to see this basic fact would be to look at some of vast literature on water clusters. Martin Chaplin website talks about this in serious detail. (which are obviously important in atmospheric physics and mitochondrial medicine)
Some of the simulations and experiments give dipole moments. When you examine it, you will see the dipole moments increase as the number of hydrogen bonds increase. This is a critical piece of data most do not know about in biology and becomes quite important in the MINOS layer.

There is an anticorrelation between the OH bond strength and hydrogen bond strength. The presence of a hydrogen bond weakens the OH bond.

This is why hydrogen bonding causes a red-shifting of the frequencies in water. This is why water is a true red light chromophore. The effect is very subtle and complicated by nuclear quantum effects, though and this is not well appreciated by Pollack but is by many others data. It is by other researchers like Chaplin. In heavy water like D2O, where the effects of quantum delocalization are smaller, the covalent OD bond is stronger than an OH bond and the hydrogen bonds are weaker. This causes an anomolous expansion of D2O ice and water when temperature cools: [https://mvfsgroup.wordpress.com/2012/04/02/first-paper-of-betul-pamuk-is-accepted-to-phys-rev-lett/](https://mvfsgroup.wordpress.com/2012/04/02/first-paper-of-betul-pamuk-is-accepted-to-phys-rev-lett/)

### PaulG — 2017-01-12T11:23:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206668>

"A team at MIT has found an unexpected discovery about water: Inside the tiniest of spaces — in carbon nanotubes whose inner dimensions are not much bigger than a few water molecules — water can freeze solid even at high temperatures that would normally set it boiling"

Water can behave in unanticipated ways in confined spaces as this paper shows...
[http://news.mit.edu/2016/carbon-nanotubes-water-solid-boiling-1128](http://news.mit.edu/2016/carbon-nanotubes-water-solid-boiling-1128)

**“If you confine a fluid to a nanocavity, you can actually distort its phase behavior,” Strano says, referring to how and when the substance changes between solid, liquid, and gas phases. Such effects were expected, but the enormous magnitude of the change, and its direction (raising rather than lowering the freezing point), were a complete surprise:"**

and from [https://arxiv.org/ftp/arxiv/papers/1202/1202.1008.pdf](https://arxiv.org/ftp/arxiv/papers/1202/1202.1008.pdf)

"Confinement of conventional liquids inside nanoscale structures gives rise to fascinating physical properties that form the basis for an ever increasing number of novel applications. Significant efforts have been devoted to the study of structure, dynamics and thermodynamics of water inside carbon nanotubes (CNTs). 1-8 Both experimental and computational studies suggest that confined fluids exhibit a variety of new properties that differ greatly from those of bulk fluids. **For instance, water readily forms ice-like structures inside CNTs even at room temperature**.4, 9 Confinement notably softens water dynamics, resulting in a drastic reduction of shear viscosity as the CNT diameter decreases to a few effective solvent diameters.10 Polar solute molecules, driven by dipole-dipole interactions, tend to aggregate,5 and the aggregation is essential for CNT filling."

and from here [https://web.engr.illinois.edu/~aluru/Journals/NL03-1.pdf](https://web.engr.illinois.edu/~aluru/Journals/NL03-1.pdf)

"Confinement can induce unusual behavior in the properties of matter. Using molecular dynamics simulations, **we show here that water confined to carbon nanotubes of a critical size under ambient conditions (1 bar, 300 K) can undergo a transition into a state having icelike mobility with an amount of hydrogen bonding similar to that in liquid water**. The onset of this behavior occurs rapidly, raising the possibility that confinement inside nanotubes, and perhaps even buckyballs, can provide an environment in which the dynamics of phase changes may be studied directly by simulation. **Moreover, because of a variety of evidence suggesting that water ordering may modulate proton conductance via a “proton wire” hydrogen bonding network, the ability to modulate water ordering with geometry suggests a possible mechanism for a switchable nanoscale semiconductor."
**

### Jack Kruse — 2017-01-15T05:44:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206852>

When you listen to people who don't know the published literature about how water acts when it is heated or cooled you get confused because you might not be aware of the literature on the subject. I don't want any of my members in doubt. The researcher says verbatim “I found that the thermal volume contraction is due to the deviation of bond angles from the regular tetrahedral angle,” Matsumoto says. He also applied his former idea of vitrites to classify local structures. “Any kind of local structure shrinks when bond angle is distorted from the regular tetrahedral angle. In other words, local structural variety is not the principal factor contributing to the thermal contraction. Water shrinks homogeneously by thermal angular distortion, regardless of local structural variety.” [https://phys.org/news/2009-07-cools-explanation.html](https://phys.org/news/2009-07-cools-explanation.html)

### JanSz — 2017-01-15T15:30:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondrial-membranes-and-lipid-replacement-therapy.16737/post-206856>

[https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/BattleShift-Armageddon/i\_LKuFwUU2Y/fXBY5iFoAzEJ](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/BattleShift-Armageddon/i_LKuFwUU2Y/fXBY5iFoAzEJ)
rhombic dodecahedron: Maraldi's angle

\-----
It can be seen as a tetrahedron with triangular pyramids added to each face.

\-----
As Plateau pointed out in 19th century, four beams of a foam crosses at a node with regular tetrahedral angle - Maraldi's angle - similar to the water’s hydrogen bond network.”

**Matsumoto** used computer simulation to look at three ways to change the volume of the foam cells: extension of the bonds, a change in the containing angle between the bonds, and a change in network topology. “By discriminating the three contributions, the mechanism became very clear.
One contributes to thermal expansion,
another one contributes to thermal contraction,
and the last one does not.
Density maximum is a result of these competing contributions,” he explains.

Read more at: [https://phys.org/news/2009-07-cools-explanation.html#jCp](https://phys.org/news/2009-07-cools-explanation.html#jCp)

### healthy2015 — 2015-08-18T18:06:14+0300

Hope you don't mind the questions.
Thanks

# Thread: Caveman consumed carbs not fish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caveman-consumed-carbs-not-fish.16742/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Bill1 — 2015-08-19T17:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caveman-consumed-carbs-not-fish.16742/post-175115>

don't shoot me - I'm just the messenger  New research study shows caveman consumed carbs not fish - the scientists said carbs not DHA are important for brain development. Futhermore, they conclude low carb diets are not healthy. I'm sure Jack would love to talk to them lol

**read more of the article:**
[http://www.marketwatch.com/story/the-paleo-diet-has-it-wrong-cavemen-did-eat-carbs-2015-08-18](http://www.marketwatch.com/story/the-paleo-diet-has-it-wrong-cavemen-did-eat-carbs-2015-08-18)

University of Chicago study [published earlier this month](http://press.uchicago.edu/pressReleases/2015/August/150806_qrb_hardy_et_al_paleo_diet.html) suggests that carbohydrate consumption, especially in the form of starch found in plant root tubers, like those found on potatoes, was vital for the acceleration of brain growth over the last 3 million years. The study says that to truly eat Paleo, starch and higher levels of carbohydrates are necessary. It explains that the human brain uses about a quarter of the body’s energy budget and about 60% of blood glucose — energy needs that wouldn't have been met on a low-carbohydrate diet.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-19T20:02:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caveman-consumed-carbs-not-fish.16742/post-175144>

many articles have been published showing the opposite. You cannot form a human brain eating carbs. You can destroy it.

The study says that to truly eat Paleo, starch and higher levels of carbohydrates are necessary. It explains that the human brain uses about a quarter of the body’s energy budget and about 60% of blood glucose — energy needs that wouldn't have been met on a low-carbohydrate diet.

Sunlight and proteins making viri in massive amounts daily in sea water. Where did primates get it? From infected shellfish and seafood because a small group of them were isolated from the forrest in the East African Rift zone about 6 million years ago. De novo gene formation are what took us from primate to human quickly. The MHC1 gene was such a gene that I spoke about in the Brain Gut series. The MHC1 gene is responsible for both neurogenesis and the development of the human immune system. You need to have a leaky gut to assimilate the viral DNA to make a junk yard of small parts that make small proteins that act like prions. # Thread: Meat and Oysters

### JessEvans — 2015-08-20T04:53:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-oysters.16746/post-175178>

Hi everyone, I'm new so this is the beginning of my journal.

I've been doing the Leptin Reset Rx for a few weeks now. I figured out that the easiest way for me is to cook 8lbs of grassfed chuck roast at a time and eat that every day for breakfast. I sometimes add grassfed cheese or an organic egg or two, or some duck liver pate. It's hard for me to get the full 50 grams eating only one thing...variety seems to help. So far I've lost 6 pounds. I've also just barely started messing around with the cold thermogenesis. And I swim in the ocean here a few times a week, which I guess is good for grounding. I have a lot of questions which I will post in the other forums.

### caroline — 2015-08-20T06:14:44+0300

BPC = bullet proof coffee with butter and coconut oil

sardines, salmon, mackerel .....

great job on the weight loss!

### JessEvans — 2015-08-21T00:31:47+0300

Hi Caroline,
I do eat a lot of grassfed butter. Coconut oil I can do by making chai, but I have to have the really good kind...even some of the cold press ones taste nasty to me. I am picky! I miss the Netherlands where you can buy duck and smoked mackerel at the grocery store for cheap!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-21T02:14:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-oysters.16746/post-175263>

Since the Leptin Rx revolves around light what about your environment? DHA intake from high DHA foods? How much blue light do you allow? The more you allow the less grass fed beef one needs and the more seafood one would require. CO is a summer time tropical fat. Is this something of a staple for you?

### JessEvans — 2015-08-21T03:00:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meat-and-oysters.16746/post-175266>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=175263)
>
> Since the Leptin Rx revolves around light what about your environment? DHA intake from high DHA foods? How much blue light do you allow? The more you allow the less grass fed beef one needs and the more seafood one would require. CO is a summer time tropical fat. Is this something of a staple for you?

Hi Dr. Jack!
I would say that I am moderate in blue light but could be much better. I have FLux on my screens, but the amber light bulbs and the amber glasses are in the mail to me now.

Coconut oil is not a staple for me. I have it but I only use it occasionally. I used to use it more to make cocoa and then I found out that all my sources of cocoa powder were contaminated with cadmium. Sometimes I will use the refined coconut oil in combination with butter or olive oil (like when I make mayonnaise.)

Grassfed butter is my staple cooking and eating fat. Organic EVVO is second, lard is third and coconut oil is fourth. I don't use vegetable/seed oils except the occasional splash of sesame oil to flavor a Chinese or Korean recipe.

# Thread: Hi all, can you fact check my understanding please?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JessEvans — 2015-08-20T05:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175183>

Hi everyone! I'm new here. So this is my story.

-   I'm 47 years old and in basically good health.
-   I have androgenetic hair loss and I take birth control pills to prevent re-occurance of my (surgically treated) endometriosis.
-   I have struggled with anemia in the past and have had low levels of vit D.
-   I am 35 pounds overweight and carry it mostly in my belly.
-   I have a very extreme mental reaction to severe sleep deprivation...if I stay up until 3am, the next day I think everyone hates me and I'm very angry with them. It's bad. Fortunately, I don't have insomnia so this is under my control.
-   I do sometimes have mood swings and I do sometimes have fatigue/energy swings.
-   On the bright side, I am moderately fit (I walk a lot) and live next to the Pacific Ocean in lovely Santa Cruz, CA.

I believe, based on my reading of Dr. Jack's blogs, that I am leptin intolerant. To me, this means that I forced my hypothalmus to ignore leptin for years by overeating starchy carbohydrates, and now I have a 'new normal' of 35 pounds overweight.

Some history:

As a young woman I ate a ton of refined and starchy carbs and saw my weight slowly creep up...about 5 pounds a year from the time I was 20 to the time I was 30. I was always quite active, with a lot of bicycle riding, walking and hiking. So at 33, when I got pregnant with my twins, I was 35 pound over weight. I had a very healthy and successful pregnancy, which I attribute to walking for 2 hours a day at the gym for the whole pregnancy. I gave birth with no anesthetic, no interventions, to full-term identical twin boys. So yay!

But afterwards, the weight just wouldn't come off. When the boys were 3 I was still (again?) 35 pounds overweight. And this weight looked very different on my stretched-out, post-partum body than it did before my pregnancy! I have been asked over and over and over again by friends and strangers alike if I am pregnant. Sigh. No. I am not pregnant, dear. I'm JUST FAT! lol.

Anyhow, I did a bunch of research back 2004 and decided to try eating a low glycemic load diet. It worked! Yay! I lost all the excess weight and was stoked. Then it slowly crept back up on me. Now I weight exactly what I did before starting that diet, even though I eat WAY fewer carbs than I used to.

This is the diet I used: basically a diet that excludes high glycemic load carbohydrates, like grain, sugar, potatoes, sweet potatoes, and bananas, and includes everything else (meat, most veggies, including beets and carrots, and most fruit including apples and oranges.)

I arrived here at Dr. Kruse because I started doing research on how to reset your metabolic set-point for weight. So, I am glad that I didn't mess up my body worse by doing a VLC diet, but clearly even eating lower on the glycemic scale was not enough to reset my leptin sensitivity back to optimal.

Right now I am eating a large meal of grassfed beef chuck every morning for breakfast. At lunchtime I either eat nothing, or eat a small meal. Some examples are a single orange, or a few slices of cheese. At dinnertime I eat a smallish meal of whatever I made for the family. It is typically a meat or seafood and a vegetable or two, though sometimes it is just various different vegetables with sauces (butter, pesto, tomato sauce).

I swim in the ocean a few times a week. The ocean is very warm right now: about 68, so this is fun and easy.
I have also started doing some CT although I haven't moved fast with this. The face thing was no problem at all for me and then I moved on to cold baths but I didn't have the ice so it was more like room temp! The water was 70 or so. I did finally get some of those reusable ice packs but I haven't tried them yet...I was going to wait until I have a compression garment.

I have questions! So many questions!

First, what do you guys think of my eating? Fruit is definitely in season for me right now, but is eating it at lunchtime messing up my leptin reset? I am losing weight but that isn't really the point...I want to change my weight set point! I don't want to yoyo again! I have lost 6 pounds since starting eating this way, but I do sometimes ignore hunger at lunch time. Is that bad? If I ignore it, it goes away until dinner time. If I eat a tiny little bit it the hunger also goes away until dinner time. So should I eat that little bit at lunch when I get hungry? Or not?

Also, should I even do the CT? Or is that intended for people who have more illnesses?

OK, I will sign out before this turns into an even bigger monster thread. Thanks for being here, everyone!

\-Jessica

### caroline — 2015-08-20T06:05:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175184>

Hi Jessica and welcome!

C/T is a really good thing!

More seafood! way more seafood!!! Are you leaving at least four hours between meals and before bed?

I don't think it is bad to ignore a little hunger - my opinion. do you keep a diary? start a journal here in the journal section and tell us exactly what you are eating...

what water are you drinking?

nnEMF???? where do you live? work? your postcode matters huge! does your house have a smart meter?

How is your sleep? How is your libido? \[this is a tell tale sign\] are you in menopause?

Have you read Dr. K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX .... a great overview of the first couple of years of the blog

Do you listen to Jack's podcasts?

How are you doing with the blogs???? does your head hurt yet?????

if you can swing it - become a gold member and you will have access to the monthly webinars and to a live Q&A with Dr. Kruse

### JessEvans — 2015-08-20T07:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175191>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=175184)
>
> Hi Jessica and welcome!
>
> C/T is a really good thing!
>
> More seafood! way more seafood!!! Are you leaving at least four hours between meals and before bed?
>
> I don't think it is bad to ignore a little hunger - my opinion. do you keep a diary? start a journal here in the journal section and tell us exactly what you are eating...
>
> what water are you drinking?
>
> nnEMF???? where do you live? work? your postcode matters huge! does your house have a smart meter?
>
> How is your sleep? How is your libido? \[this is a tell tale sign\] are you in menopause?
>
> Have you read Dr. K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX .... a great overview of the first couple of years of the blog
>
> Do you listen to Jack's podcasts?
>
> How are you doing with the blogs???? does your head hurt yet?????
>
> if you can swing it - become a gold member and you will have access to the monthly webinars and to a live Q&A with Dr. Kruse

Seafood: very expensive! I won't eat canned because of BPA. I have bought oysters in a jar a few times and I'll keep getting them. I'd buy the herring in a jar but it has sugar added. So which is worse? Seafood with sugar, or less seafood? Remember I am not just feeding myself, I am cooking for a family of 4. Teenage boys eat a lot! I have frozen salmon from Costco but it is the cheaper (sockeye) salmon, not king, so it is lean and I can't figure out how to cook it without it getting dry and nasty. Maybe I should poach it and make it into fish salad...I make good home-made coconut oil mayonnaise and I eat spreads on celery instead of crackers. I can start a food journal.

4 hours between eating and bed: this is a tough one. We usually have dinner at 7, go to bed at 11 and lights out at 11;30. The problem is, if the schedule slips, that is cutting the timing very close. I want to start eating earlier. I have to convince my husband to come home at 5;30 instead of 6;30 and then work after dinner if he needs to catch up. We'll see if he goes for it. Since he already works hard at a fairly sucky job I don't want to ask him to do things that will make his life harder.

My work is housewife and homeschool mom. My boys are starting the local public high school in a week though! My homeschool job is done! I am very excited because I will have time to take care of myself and also the house and property which is sadly neglected at the moment.

I drink filtered tap water. We have a soda stream (water carbonater) so sometimes I drink it filtered and carbonated. I don't drink juice or soda. I drink a few cups a day of black or green tea, made with filtered tap water. I am lucky that here in Santa Cruz the tap water is not fluoridated.

EMF: My house has a smart meter and not only that we have wifi too! And a cordless phone! And cellphones! But I do swim in the ocean at least three times per week. I think that's supposed to help with grounding or whatever, right?

My sleep is good. I have decent sleep hygiene. I wear earplugs and I also put a sock over my eyes when my husband is reading late. I did order some amber bulbs but they haven't come yet. I have FLux on my computer and tablet. Anyhow, I usually sleep from around eleven thirty to 7 or 8 am, with brief awakenings once or twice to go pee. As long as I keep my stress low and wear earplugs I don't have a problem with wakefulness. Frankly, reading the blogs is the only thing keeping me up at night these days! My schedule will change when the boys starts school, though. I'll probably be up earlier and in bed earlier which is another reason I want to eat dinner earlier.

Libido, hmm. Hard to tell. I think it is decent. Relationship issues make it hard to tell sometimes, and human sexuality is complicated. But the urge is there, all right.

Menopause: I am perimenopausal. I take birth control pills round the calendar (no breaks except once every few months) both to control the endometriosis and to prevent the excessive bleeding that led to my anemia before. I got severely anemic from having a copper IUD, which caused, basically, monthly hemorrhaging. Then when I got perimenopausal, my periods became very erratic and sometimes extremely heavy. The birth control pills are working well to control that.

I have read a bunch of the blogs, mostly the leptin ones and the CT ones. I haven't read much of the EMF ones. I kind of got it that all the EMF in our lives is bad and that we should get grounded in ocean water, and left it at that. My husband is a computer programmer and works from home a fair amount. EMF isn't going away in my life, so it'll have to be salt water for me! I am planning on continuing the ocean swimming in winter when the water gets down around 45. I found a nice group of people who swim year round without wetsuits.

I could turn the router off at night before I go to bed if you think it would make a difference. It will still be on all day, plus the smart meter is on 24/7.

As for the gold member thing, we'll see. Maybe at some point I will feel moved to join up, but right now I am hoping to rely on the blogs and the forum.

I did read the book but it didn't have anything different from what I'd already read in the blogs!

### caroline — 2015-08-20T10:26:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175195>

birth control pills are very dehydrating.

Have diner early - eat when YOU need to ... and then have a cup of tea and some salad or some cocoa crack at dinner with your DH.

turning the router off at nite would be such a good idea! Have you got all electronics out of the bedroom?

Get a land line and do away with wifi ....

where is the smart meter?

Ocean swimming is awesome ...do it as much as possible. work in your yard bare foot and with little clothing - naked is best - your hormones will love you!

some canned seafood does not have BPA ....king Oscar is one.

I buy salmon trimmings from our fish market and make Thai salmon patties - there is a recipe in the recipe section.

what about frozen mussels, shrimp etc? with the salmon - wrap it in some foil with lots of butter and fresh herbs and a slice of lemon and bake at high heat for 10 min per inch. Don't over bake!

Become best friends with your fish guy! you can make seafood stocks with trimmings/bones/head etc. Often they will give them to you.

### Scompy — 2015-08-21T00:37:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175259>

> [JessEvans said:](/goto/post?id=175191)
>
> Seafood: very expensive! I won't eat canned because of BPA. I have bought oysters in a jar a few times and I'll keep getting them. I'd buy the herring in a jar but it has sugar added. So which is worse? Seafood with sugar, or less seafood? Remember I am not just feeding myself, I am cooking for a family of 4. Teenage boys eat a lot! I have frozen salmon from Costco but it is the cheaper (sockeye) salmon, not king, so it is lean and I can't figure out how to cook it without it getting dry and nasty. Maybe I should poach it and make it into fish salad...I make good home-made coconut oil mayonnaise and I eat spreads on celery instead of crackers. I can start a food journal.
>
> EMF: My house has a smart meter and not only that we have wifi too! And a cordless phone! And cellphones! But I do swim in the ocean at least three times per week. I think that's supposed to help with grounding or whatever, right?
>
> I could turn the router off at night before I go to bed if you think it would make a difference. It will still be on all day, plus the smart meter is on 24/7.

Welcome...!

In my opinion, cleaning up the environmental sources needs to be the primary focus. I hacked my Smartmeter by putting a Faraday cage around it, and back reflecting the wall it's on. In order for me to hack it, I had to buy an EMF-Rf meter to know for sure what was going on in my house and at work. Still dealing with work-EMF at this very moment. My electric company is about to offer analog for customers again for smartmeters, so the more people call and complain, the more likely they will be to offer alternatives. I hard-wired my home with ethernet, and I get way better connection times because of it too. I only turn on my cell certain times a day and have it on airplane mode otherwise. EMF dries you out because it sucks electrons from the body, requires more water all of the time, not to mention the other damage, so minimizing it as much as possible goes a long way--especially in kids and teens. Sounds like the swimming in the ocean is mitigating a ton of the damage you'd get normally. I also found the best pockets to sleep in specific rooms with the least amount of EMF.

I hear you with sugars in the meats. But I'd rather take a bit of poison with DHA if it comes to that choice. There are other ways to get DHA too if you know what you're doing, like getting cold-packed fish oils knowing it hasn't been stored in a warm warehouse and gone rancid. Food used to be #1 with me being on a ketogenic diet for the past 3 years. Now it's not at all, but still important to a degree. The environment we live in and keeping ourselves hydrated is far, far more important than most food choices. I still eat under 20 grams of sugar per day on average, but don't focus on it as much as I had in the past. Focusing on mitigating environmental damage has gone a lot farther in my progress, well-being, health and sleep. Sleep is deceptive too. I thought I was getting 'good sleep' several months ago and didn't realize how crappy it actually was up until recently. Not only are my dreams extremely vivid, I now remember them and can recall them all day long if I think about the previous night. I also require about 7-8 hours of sleep at night (compared to needing 8-9 months ago). When I wake up in the morning, I do so automatically without my alarm going off (literally on the minute 5 mins before the alarm rings), and getting out of bed is extremely easy and no longer tiring like it used to be. I'll immediately go outside and absorb the light for 15minutes after waking up, and expose as much skin as I can. I'll do some grounding during that 15mins as well. At lunch, I always get outside too and do some grounding--getting out of the office. All of these smaller changes have gone a long way to make me feel better--just as good, if not better than the dietary changes made years ago.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-21T02:10:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175262>

you told us very little about your environment........that is where it all begins.
Your genome is designed to respond to environmental signals, not control them. So food is not a panacea just a tool. Your canvas is your environment.
No human being itself should be considered impaired innately, instead there are environmental short comings that cause the impairment. Thus, it is incumbent on the on the clinician to recommend treatment of the environment their patient is in. People react to an inferior environment, way before their genome is altered.

### JessEvans — 2015-08-21T02:33:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-all-can-you-fact-check-my-understanding-please.16747/post-175264>

Thanks Caroline and Scompy! I will look into finding a source for local fish that is less expensive. Now that you are pushing me a little I remember that with some extra effort I can go down to the harbor and buy fish off the boats. I need to research when the boats come in. I will have so much more time to do this when the boys start school! It is going to be a whole new stage of life for me. I can ride my bike down to the harbor with a cooler in the bike trailer and buy a whole salmon or a big load of local sardines. Exciting!

Thanks so much for reading this post and responding. You are already making a difference for me and I appreciate it. This particular diet/lifestyle project is way more wacky than I am used to, so the support is needed! Usually here in Santa Cruz I am the conservative one who just eats lots of vegetables (compared to all my gluten-intolerant and/or vegan and/or raw-food-only peers.) It feels odd to be embarking on such an extreme path. Hard to know how rigorous I will ind up being. But I will definitely eat a BAB, buy more fish, and keep swimming in the ocean. And any day now I'm gonna get in that ice bath. Any day now...

Dr. Jack, thanks for weighing in. OK, I will try to tell you about the environment:

-   I live in a house with both WiFi and a SmartMeter. The SmartMeter is on the south-facing kitchen wall downstairs. The bedrooms are upstairs.
-   I live in Santa Cruz, CA, which is dead center between northern and southern CA, on the Monterey Bay, which is a National Marine Sanctuary and typically has very good ocean water quality.
-   The ocean temps range from the (current, unusual) highs of 68f to a winter low of around 45f.
-   The air here is great...very clean and sweet. It is cool and foggy in the morning and sunny in the afternoon except when is is sunny in the morning and cool and foggy in the afternoon.
-   It is very temperate...basically a Mediterranean-type environment.
-   There is a harbor and a local fishing community.
-   There is a large and thriving organic farming community and I can purchase pastured local pork and lamb and put it in my chest freezer. (I have some lamb there now.)
-   There is very little in the way of industry, otherwise. It is basically a beach and college town with an organic farming community.
-   The water is locally sourced surface water: rainwater runoff trapped in a reservoir in the Santa Cruz mountains. It is treated and chlorinated but not fluoridated. I have an under-the-sink filter on the cold tap to filter out the chlorine and I use that for drinking and cooking.

Does that answer the environment question? If not, let me know what else to tell you about.

Thanks again and best wishes, Jessica

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-21T05:39:38+0300

I started reading the blogs at Leptin, and then CT. I'm hoping to work in those areas, with ocean swimming to address the EMT load. Should I just forget it? The thing is, I'm not sick. I'm well! I'm just fat, and I want to stop being fat so that I can stay well.

On a side note, congratulations on [https://www.jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-10-quantum-puzzle/](https://www.jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-10-quantum-puzzle/)
It's a brilliant and clearly written post. I've been wondering for years about how life manages to stay organized in the face of the natural tendency towards entropy. The sun, constantly pushing electrons at us, is how we organisms can capture the excess energy needed to stay organized. So cool.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-02T05:09:37+0300

### 5G Canary — 2020-03-03T18:01:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ocular-melanoma-is-quite-rare-but-may-grow-with-4-g-networks.16755/post-286997>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=175315)
>
> 4 G and 5G is now going into schools everywhere to provide 24/7 Wifi and emergency call signals.
>
> [http://www.lakenormancitizen.com/ne...ing-theories-for-ocular-melanoma-cluster.html](http://www.lakenormancitizen.com/news/news/item/8798-two-shocking-theories-for-ocular-melanoma-cluster.html)

Wish I was a member back in 2015 when you posted and predicted this.... YOU NAILED THIS ONE!

How do I know? This ^^^ is my old neighborhood!!! We’ve played many soccer games at this high school. They have recently reported that at least two dozen people have been diagnosed with ocular melanoma – a rare cancer that should only affect 5 in a million.

I also know that what they are reporting isn’t even accurate. They have been down playing the numbers the last few years. I know of at least 4 people on just the street we lived on that aren’t counted in those numbers. I also always thought it was weird how many had detached retinas and how many children where having eye issues.

This article is from Summer Heath written in 2015. She was one of the canaries in your ^^^ above article. Below this article is how she is doing today.... same zip code. So sad.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

HUNTERSVILLE, NORTH CAROLINA OCULAR MELANOMA CANCER CLUSTER

Since my diagnosis in July of 2013 we’ve had a strange hunch that there may be potential cluster in Huntersville. The day after my diagnosis my family and I learned that there was not one, but two other young women who attended my high school who were diagnosed with OM. I remember thinking, the doctors saying “This is a rare case because your 19..” and “This happens to 6 out of a million a year.” So if it is so rare, then why are there more and who attended my high school? This when we all started pushing the thought of a cluster and investigation needed. Well a year and a few months later the numbers have kept growing, we are at a total of 6 now and that’s just who we know of and have agreed to speak with us. Really? All 6 of the people who get this are from the same town and have a relation to our high school, huh that’s not strange or anything, right?! (Sarcasm) Not only are the numbers scary,we have lost the two beautiful women who attended high school with me. Watching their families with the loss and feeling my own pain as well as so many lives they touched, this has done nothing but encourage to further an investigation. We are begging for any fighters, survivors, or family of OM’ers who have lived in Huntersville, NC to contact me. I will keep everything private but if there are more out there the more pressure we out on researchers to find a cause which may help find a cure.

Best of luck,
Summer Heath

This was posted recently about her....

Hello friends and family! I'm asking for your support, prayers, and donations for Summer Heath. She is known to many as friend, teacher, coworker, daughter, and sunshine because she shines her light wherever she goes! In July of 2013 Summer was diagnosed with Ocular Melanoma. She went through several different chemo treatments and was in remission for a short period of time. Now 6 ½ years later the Melanoma returned and has rapidly metastasized to her liver, lungs, breast, pancreas, gallbladder, and bones. The term silver lining is used when you want to emphasize the hopeful side of a situation that might seem gloomy on the surface. The common expression "every cloud has a silver lining" means that even the worst events or situations have some positive aspect. Summer is 26 years old and works as a second-grade teacher. She loves her job and working with all of her kiddos. Lately, the classroom has been her "silver lining" where she can escape and keep her mind off of her diagnosis. Until her recent cancer relapse, Summer was working 2-3 jobs to help get by with all of her medical expenses, student loans, and car payments. Now Summer has a new clinical trial that requires her to travel to Philadelphia monthly for exams and treatment which limits her ability to earn extra funds. She undergoes MRI and CT Scans every 8 weeks along with multiple local doctors appointments in between. We're asking for any amount of donations you might be able to contribute to help her get through these next steps in her treatment process. It is easy to feel overwhelmed and stressed during this exhausting time, so we wanted to start this page to help her feel a little more at ease with all she is going through. Hopefully this will help keep her mind from worrying about medical expenses and stay focused on the silver lining ahead! Summer is a strong, young independent woman and I know we will win this battle! We will not give up and we will conquer this with her! Check back for new updates as she goes through her treatments. Thank you for your support and prayers!

Updates (2)

-   FEBRUARY 28, 2020
    by Lindsey White, Organizer
    From Summer: Our day consists of packing and preparing. We found out earlier this week that cancer has spread to the brain. We leave to go to Philly tomorrow for a week. While we are there I will have direct brain radiation and liver radiation. Thank you for all the prayers thus far and as always we are asking for continuing prayers through this next chapter of my life. With everything going on I have taken medical leave from work until everything has settled down. It’s hard to be away from all of my kiddos, past and present, but their little faces keep me going.

-   “Even now, as broken as you may feel, you are still so strong. There’s something to be said about how you hold yourself together and keep moving, even though you feel like shattering. Don’t stop. This is your healing. It doesn’t have to be pretty, or graceful. You just have to keep going.”

-   FEBRUARY 22, 2020
    by Lindsey White, Organizer
    Thank you so much for everyone who has reached out and donated. Earlier this week I went to the ER, where we found out my liver tumors have doubled in size, as well as new tumors in the liver and down the spine. I have a brain scan on Sunday to check for any lesions on the brain after the right side of my face went numb and my hearing is going in and out randomly. Please pray for a clear brain scan and a successful new treatment plan to be given on Monday or Tuesday! Thank you to everybody who has donated to the GoFundMe. This has taken a huge stress off of my mind during this very tense time! The GoFundMe will be open another week!

\*\*\*\* I thought this was ironic that this girl (quoted below) also diagnosed at the same time as Summer but in Auburn Alabama... and the cancer followed the same path? Thoughts why that particular path?

“Allison Allred's cancer has recurred nine times in six places in her body.”

"Two days ago found out that it's come back to my brain," Allred said, "So, I'm actually gonna have radiation on my brain tomorrow."

# Thread: Optimal Disaster Preparedness - GO!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-disaster-preparedness-go.16756/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TerrierMom — 2015-08-22T00:01:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-disaster-preparedness-go.16756/post-175328>

So - the dreaded Carrington event happens and fries all our electronics, and the world economy goes to hell in a handbasket, and supplies aren't moving anywhere and even if they were, our digital money can't buy them anyway....

You realize all of a sudden that you will have to find your own food, water, sanitation, defense, etc and be totally self sufficient.

There are lots of preppers out there in the world, and I know lots of us Optimal-focused folk probably have MUST HAVE lists of stuff or even acquired the supplies already (I live in quake country so I have started to amass some stuff, anyway) - what's on your list? What would you have in your Optimal Survival Kit?

Assume you are stuck in the city and don't have a lake house somewhere you can bug out to. What's on the shelf in your garage ready to carry you through a disaster?

### jenaf — 2015-08-23T00:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-disaster-preparedness-go.16756/post-175399>

oooh I'm very interested!!

for me I'd want skills that i can use anywhere, and a bug out bag so i could hike out to a safe place away from the crazies. i need good resources to create my supplies list... off to the library for some Bear Gryls info! lol

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-23T00:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-disaster-preparedness-go.16756/post-175401>

We are 8 days short of the decade anniversary of Katrina. The city has made a stunning reversal. If you were here before you might be shocked at how NOLA has become a power house. We had Senator's (Hassert now in jail) telling people ten years ago not to rebuild this city. Walking around the French Quarter today during a rainstorm and seeing all the faces of this city, this would have been the biggest mistake this country could have made. If you have not visited recently be prepared to be shocked. This will be a somber week for some here but from the ashes rose the Phoenix. For me I am proud to be part of the rebuild of this city. Most of you have never been to NOLA. I grew up in NYC, but only geography makes New Orleans part of the South. It is as close one can get to NYC without being in Manhattan. The people are certainly different and that is wholly a good thing. Most of my friends are New Yorker's and New Englander's. The differences between the two are about as far apart as the 1500 mile van trip you are about to undertake. People in NOLA are naturally polite. It’s not artificial ether; it’s who they are. They smile. They hold doors for you. They call you “baby,” "sweatheart", “shug,” or “darling” because that’s the way they were taught to treat fellow man. There’s nothing in it for them, but consider it a really good thing for your soul. People in New Orleans are seldom in a hurry. This was a nice change for me a city dweller, after I bitched about it for a year when I first moved here. Slowing life down allows you to live it fully. People in New Orleans will talk to anyone at anytime about anything. It is the one thing I have changed about me. I stop people on my porch and open champagne with people to talk. In NYC I did not even make eye contact on the damn subway with other people. Now I am a night mare on NYC subways. LOL In NOLA I relearned how to like people. In New Orleans, speech is not a mode of communication; it is an art form. People in NOLA never apologize for having a good time. They partied when the Saints sucked and they partied when thousands died of yellow fever. They even partied post Katrina, although that one took some time. The spirit of this city is built into its inhabitants souls. It is palpable. It is magnetic for a person like me even when I was loathe to admit it. People in NOLA still appreciate the work of volunteers and people looking to repair their city. When I renovated my place on Bourbon St I had people coming in to thank me for what I was doing. That is the kind of things we need more of in this world. Think about this city on August 29th 2015. Ten years ago they left it for a dead desert of looting and mayhem. Come check us out now, because we back in a BIG EASY KINDA WAY. These people know what it takes to survive a tragedy of epic proportions..........you need love, faith, and work ethic and no complaining. [http://www.nola.com/katrina/index.ssf/2015/08/katrina\_flooding\_map.html](http://www.nola.com/katrina/index.ssf/2015/08/katrina_flooding_map.html)

### TerrierMom — 2015-08-24T19:27:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-disaster-preparedness-go.16756/post-175548>

I definitely experienced that when I was in NOLA - people walking by your porch were so open and would stop and chat and I got a little boy to try some seafood and he liked it (he didn't think he would!!). Aside from this summer the last time I was in NOLA I was 16 (so 23 years ago) - and this time I was there about a day - safe to say I need to come back and spend some quality time there and experience some of the MONSTER oysters I've seen y'all downing!!

But back to disaster - this works for hurricane, fire, earthquake, etc.

I see 3 sets of supplies - one easily carried in a backpack/duffel - for a quick escape.

Another set you can pack in a car or truck and bug out.

Another set for sheltering in place which of course will have the most stuff. (With Set 2 being a grouped-together subset of Set 3 for easy loading).

If people post stuff I will start compiling a document in each area with links to the things people recommend

Needs: KNOWLEDGE - what books/resources will you want (on paper, on kindle). (like, knowing what you can forage wild, survival info, canning/preserving knowledge, etc) - Any good resources people can recommend?
SANITATION - how will you deal with human waste in a way that will not create disease?
FOOD - high-quality food to store
WATER - storage ideas as well as filtration on the go ideas
HUNTING/GATHERING - when your food supplies run out and you need to get some more - including canning/preserving, and seeds to grow
FIRST AID - what's in your kit?
SHELTER/LIVING - lighting, bedding/tent, can openers, gear in general.

This group has to have some great ideas!

# Thread: Pregnant women need to know...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnant-women-need-to-know.16759/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-08-22T01:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnant-women-need-to-know.16759/post-175347>

...about nn-EMF. They need to know this for their unborn and born babies to help prevent the potential of autism and other neurological conditions.

1\. Clean up your environment before getting pregnant or when you're aware
2\. Most newer cars now give off magneticEMF; Put the seat back farther from the dashwhile driving.
3\. Wear some silver-threaded clothing over the stomach
4\. Never use your cell phone while touching it
5\. Never rest your cell phone on your stomach while using it
6\. Don't sleep or use an A/C unit or fan while close to you--high magneticEMFs
7\. If you make food on an electric stove, it's the perfect height to put your fetus in an EMF
8\. Hardwire your home with ethernet and get rid of your Wifi

Remember too that after birth, it takes awhile for the baby's cranium to seal. But nn-EMF damage continues for the next 20 years (and longer in the electrosensitive).

How much damage do we think we're doing to newborns and babies that fly on airplanes being ungrounded and exposed to electronic devices? Makes me wonder if a baby's sudden and unexpected crying bouts, in some cases, could be caused by EMF exposure.

By the way, in my 2009 minicooper, it pulses ULTRA-strong magnetic EMF every 1/2 second because it uses a special key tech; between the pulses, the background EMF is strong enough that I wouldn't want to be sitting close to the dash. Car-EMF exposure has got to be the primary reason of getting thirsty, tired and lethargic on longer trips.

# Thread: Visual-sensory detection of bad redox and/or nn-EMF a possiblity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/visual-sensory-detection-of-bad-redox-and-or-nn-emf-a-possiblity.16762/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scompy — 2015-08-22T04:55:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/visual-sensory-detection-of-bad-redox-and-or-nn-emf-a-possiblity.16762/post-175359>

After watching the latest Aug. 15th Webinar, a thought has been on my mind. My 6 year old fits EHS very well. Gets sick/colds easily, has eye issues where he feels like something is in them when there is not, complains of phantom pains in his limbs that come and go, etc. Up until about 2 months ago, we've done a TON of stuff to eliminate all EMF sources. (Remotes from video game consoles, smart meter, all the known stuff...)

Getting to the point here, he said something profound to me last week saying when he goes to sleep at night, he sees those flowing clouds/waves of green and red colors in the dark. Now, it hit me at that very moment, I don't experience those clouds/waves ANYMORE... In the past, I know exactly what he was talking about. I had more profound issues. I always attributed this to 'looking through the retinal artery' in my mind's eye and being able to see the flow of blood happening. Some migraine images give a similar type of influence, but not to the brightness and flashiness of a migraine.

In the last 3-4 years, I haven't seen this effect at all going to sleep, but it's exactly in that window of time I have been shaping up my life, eating ketotic and haven't had any capillary issues/blood seepage into my central vision.

Could it be that:
1\. we are seeing the result of bad redox or calcium efflux?
2\. we are seeing actual nn-EMF coming into our vision and interacting?

Thought I'd post to see if others have the same visual effects and if it's a known process, the causes, etc. Or if this is still a mystery why some of us see this stuff when we go to sleep at night (or have seen it in the past).

### Gracelind — 2015-08-22T14:02:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/visual-sensory-detection-of-bad-redox-and-or-nn-emf-a-possiblity.16762/post-175375>

I can't speak to the flow of colors or EMF effects, but regarding the sensation of something in the eye when you can't see anything there: many people with Sjogren's have this sensation. It can be due to dryness in the eye. If his eyes seem to water excessively, that can, paradoxically, be a sign of dryness. You might try eye drops that don't have preservatives when he has this feeling in his eyes.

BTW, I'm not suggesting that he has Sjogren's as it's rare in males, let alone in children. That's just where my experience of dry eyes comes from.

I hope you can find a way to relieve his symptoms and support his health. It's hard enough to be a little kid, let alone having to battle health issues. Best wishes to you and your family!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-22T16:46:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/visual-sensory-detection-of-bad-redox-and-or-nn-emf-a-possiblity.16762/post-175379>

xerophthalmia is common in gamers. I think this perception becomes reality when the eye surface changes and effects the melanopsin receptors in the central retina. Once again showing how surface alterations can alter deeper tissues biochemistry or photochemistry.

Let me give you an example from neurosurgery of a peripheral nerve injury:

-   How should patients and surgeons change their perception on underlying internal relationships when the surface changes?
    Consider the question: water, light, and magnetism... Atmospheric barometric pressure changes greatly effect / influence neuropathic pain in injury or T2D. Hydration is key for minimizing pain activation. This is why T2D get it bad because they are chronically dehydrated. A PNS (surface) damaged at CNS (deeper tissue) say from a brachial plexus avulsions, with arm paralysis. These people often report they have always felt post injury there had to another way to minimize pain through some other modality. We have horrible options for these patients today. There is another interesting environmental fact that is a teaching point: The fact that outer body air pressure (atmospheric H2O) increase or decrease universally affects arthritic pain and many neuropathic pain suffers, suggests there must be some treatable cause & effect recipe. So what might that be from a quantum perspective?
    -   Water flows in cells are all controlled in the CNS by AQA 4 channels. They all contain 4 nitrogens suggesting that they would respond to pressure and light much like a leaf or RBC would. Both alter their surfaces to environmental changes they find themselves in. Free diving is a way to bring a hydraulic pressure to alter bariatric pressures sensation that is missing and DHA in the remaining cell membranes of the bad wing will limit your pain because its breakdown products are all anti-inflammatory in contrast to omega 6's. If the water is colder to the surface I would expect the reversal to be even better and maybe faster. DHA works better and longer in cold. One could design a harness device that simulates these environmental changes at the damaged surface and see if the bad effect reverses of goes away. You need constant sensory thalamic inflows to populate the somatosensory cortex in the CNS.......if it is absent other fibers from other modalities from the thalamus will own the real estate instead. Since movement is the efferent pathway it will stimulate and affect the modality that took over the real estate. In the eye saccades are the motor component of the visual apparatus.

        Interesting thinking huh?

>
>         Interesting thinking huh?

Thank you for this explanation. This really appears to be at the root. His eye issues always subside 2-5 mins later after I get him cold washcloth presses. My son's main source of DHA has been grassfed butter and grassfed beef patties, as he still don't have a palette for anything fish. But I've already seen positive changes after getting him high quality RO water as well as doing biohacks on my ice cubes and always putting them in his drinks now. Behaviors with gaming consoles have been a problem. We're curving his behaviors actively and it's already making a difference. I think #1 now has been eliminating the Rf-EMF from the smart meter, back-blocking it so it's no longer entering the home. I removed Wifi from the home over 2 years ago as my wife got tinnitus from it and I figured out on my own it was the cause. It was during that time when my son was the most sick too looking back on it.

About games: For me, as a professional game designer for the past 20+ years, I can 110% tell you I've had xerophthalmia off and on. Not only do I boot up games about 50+ times a day to make them, I also play a ton outside of work for 'research.' But after many of the biohacks, I don't get eye issues anymore. I see my son getting issues. I'm very knowledgeable with game-related or tech-anything because of my skillset... I put together my own computer systems, I hardwired everything in my home, etc. When I recently discovered that many of my consoles emitted Rf, I curbed my son playing with the Wii anymore. It registers 5 to 20 mW/Sq.m on average along the right-side of the Wii remote controller. Shutting down the Wii causes all Rf to go away. But, the Xbox360 outputs all of the time as I mentioned, even when plugged in. A person must physically unplug the X360 unit from the wall or from the power-supply to stop all Rf-EMF. So I enviro-hacked the X360 a bit now... we only use hard-wired controllers, but also, the Rf coming off comes out of the front near where the power-button is... if you turn the X360 180-degrees, the Rf will emit away from the gamers that are playing.

### ColinGorham — 2015-08-22T22:41:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/visual-sensory-detection-of-bad-redox-and-or-nn-emf-a-possiblity.16762/post-175393>

My surface interventions are helping a lot. I have been eating tons of raw seafood (ketogenic), CTing, sleeping on the magnetico & ditched rubber soled shoes, & controlling light every day and the results are impressive indeed. I dropped 10 lbs over the last 12 days and inflammation in my eyes (dryness) and my sinuses/ turbinate enlargement (15 years old process) is all much better. I am finishing the big dig rewire (14 hardwire ports) of the house, so wifi can be shut-off on a more permanent basis. I rent out rooms in the house so I feel I am helping my tenants as well. Everyone is in agreement re: wifi after I gave them Jack's bread or potato turning to stone in the microwave example. Everyone gets it immediately. I don't understand the technical science above but I know what Jack means (to a limited degree) as I triggered a disease in my eyes (dryness) from too much staring at the computer screen in Grad school. So the common explanation to help someone might be: You know how people who play video games all the time start seeing stuff floating in air at night when they close their eyes. The surface of your eye is being altered and a disease is around the corner for you bro. lol. Want to know more here is what is really happening: "xerophthalmia is common in gamers. I think this perception becomes reality when the eye surface changes and effects the melanopsin receptors in the central retina. Once again showing how surface alterations can alter deeper tissues biochemistry or photochemistry."

I think #1 now has been eliminating the Rf-EMF from the smart meter, back-blocking it so it's no longer entering the home.
